summary: All matter is composed of extremely small particles called constituent particles. These particles are too small to be seen even under an ordinary microscope. Breaking chalk or dissolving sugar demonstrates that matter can be subdivided into its basic building blocks without changing the substance's identity.
diagram_description: Magnified schematic showing chalk powder grains under a magnifying glass
subject: chemistry

summary: A constituent particle is the basic unit that makes up a larger piece of a substance or material. Grinding chalk or dissolving sugar breaks matter into ever-smaller pieces until an indivisible unit remains. Each tiny grain of sugar is made up of millions of such constituent particles.
diagram_description: Four-part figure showing a chalk stick, chalk broken into two pieces, chalk ground into fine powder, and chalk powder viewed under a magnifying glass
subject: chemistry

summary: Interparticle spaces are the empty gaps that exist between the constituent particles of matter. When sugar dissolves in water, its particles occupy the spaces between water particles, causing the total volume of the solution to be less than the sum of individual volumes. These spaces contain nothing at all — not even air.
diagram_description: Magnified schematic picture showing distribution of yellow sugar particles among blue water particles (Fig. 7.11)
subject: chemistry

summary: Matter exists in three physical states — solid, liquid, and gas — determined by the thermal energy of particles and the strength of interparticle attractions. Solids have maximum attraction and minimum spacing; gases have negligible attraction and maximum spacing; liquids are intermediate. Thermal energy of particles ultimately governs which state a substance adopts.
diagram_description: Summary comparison chart with particle diagrams for solid, liquid, and gas showing interparticle spacing, packing, attraction, and movement
subject: chemistry

summary: In solids, constituent particles are tightly packed with very strong interparticle attractions and minimum interparticle spacing. Particles can only vibrate or oscillate about their fixed positions and cannot move past each other. This gives solids a definite shape and definite volume.
diagram_description: Six solid objects — iron nail, rock salt, stone, wooden block, key, and piece of aluminium — displayed for comparison
subject: chemistry

summary: In liquids, interparticle attractions are slightly weaker than in solids, allowing particles to move freely within a limited space. Liquids have no fixed shape but possess a definite volume, taking the shape of any container they are poured into. Particles in liquids are slightly farther apart than in solids.
diagram_description: Hand moving a finger through water in a shallow vessel to demonstrate liquid particle movement
subject: chemistry

summary: In gases, interparticle attractions are negligible and particles move freely in all directions, occupying all available space. Gases have neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume; they expand to fill any container. The interparticle spacing in gases is maximum among the three states.
diagram_description: Three images of a closed gas jar containing solid iodine showing iodine vapour spreading freely inside the jar
subject: chemistry

summary: Both liquids and gases are classified as fluids because they flow and do not retain a fixed shape. This shared property distinguishes them from solids. The ability to flow arises because their particles are not locked in fixed positions.
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Effect of Interparticle Distance on Attraction
Role of Thermal Energy in State Changes
summary: Constituent particles of matter are held together by attractive forces called interparticle attractions. The strength of these forces depends on the nature of the substance and the distance between particles — even a slight increase in distance drastically decreases the force. The relative strength of interparticle attractions determines whether a substance is a solid, liquid, or gas.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Effect of Interparticle Distance on Attraction
summary: Interparticle attractive forces decrease drastically when the distance between particles increases even slightly. In solids, minimum spacing produces maximum attraction; in liquids, slightly greater spacing gives weaker attraction; in gases, large spacing makes attraction negligible. This inverse relationship between distance and force strength is central to understanding state differences.
diagram_description: Magnified schematic pictures comparing particle packing and interparticle spacing in solid, liquid, and gas states (Fig. 7.12)
frequently_confused_with: Role of Thermal Energy in State Changes
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Role of Thermal Energy in State Changes
summary: The thermal (heat) energy of particles determines the interparticle distance and hence the physical state of matter. In solids, low thermal energy keeps particles close with strong attractions and only small vibrations. As thermal energy increases through heating, particles move more vigorously, increasing spacing and weakening attractions until a state change occurs.
frequently_confused_with: Effect of Interparticle Distance on Attraction
subject: chemistry

summary: Phase changes are transformations between solid, liquid, and gaseous states driven by changes in thermal energy. Heating a solid provides energy to overcome interparticle attractions, converting it to a liquid at the melting point, and then to vapour at the boiling point. Evaporation is a slower surface phenomenon that occurs at all temperatures below the boiling point.
subject: chemistry

summary: The melting point is the minimum temperature at which a solid melts to become a liquid at atmospheric pressure. On heating, particles vibrate more vigorously until they leave their fixed positions, weakening interparticle attractions and converting the solid to a liquid. Solids with weaker interparticle forces have lower melting points (e.g., ice: 0°C; iron: 1538°C).
diagram_description: Table 7.1 listing melting points of ice (0°C), urea (133°C), and iron (1538°C) with images
subject: chemistry

summary: The boiling point is the temperature at which a liquid boils and turns into vapour at atmospheric pressure. At this temperature, particle movement is so vigorous that particles escape the liquid state both at the surface and from within, observed as bubble formation. The interparticle forces of attraction are overcome, converting liquid into gas rapidly.
subject: chemistry

summary: Evaporation is the slow conversion of a liquid to vapour that occurs only at the surface and at all temperatures, even below the boiling point. Unlike boiling, it does not involve bubble formation throughout the liquid. Spilled water disappearing at room temperature is a familiar example.
subject: chemistry

summary: Diffusion is the spreading of one substance's particles through another due to the constant random motion of particles. It occurs in both liquids and gases but is faster in gases because of larger interparticle spaces and weaker attractions. Increasing temperature speeds up diffusion by increasing particle kinetic energy.
subject: chemistry

summary: When potassium permanganate is placed in water, water particles pull out its particles and spread them throughout the liquid, turning the water uniformly pink over time. This demonstrates that liquid particles are in constant motion. Higher temperatures increase particle speed, causing faster diffusion; ice-cold water shows the slowest spreading.
diagram_description: Glass tumbler showing streaks of pink colour spreading from potassium permanganate grain, and then uniform pink colour throughout the water
subject: chemistry

summary: Gas particles move constantly and rapidly in all directions, causing substances like perfume or incense fragrance to spread throughout a room. Air particles collide with fragrance particles and help distribute them. This demonstrates that gas particles have negligible interparticle attractions and large interparticle spaces, allowing free movement.
diagram_description: Diagram of a room with a burning incense stick in one corner and a person on the other side smelling the fragrance
subject: chemistry

summary: Compressibility refers to the extent to which a substance can be compressed by reducing the space between its particles under external pressure. Gases are highly compressible because of their large interparticle spaces, while liquids and solids are practically incompressible due to their much smaller interparticle spaces.
subject: chemistry

summary: Gases are highly compressible because their particles have large interparticle spaces that can be reduced by applying external pressure. Pushing the plunger of a sealed syringe containing air shows a visible decrease in volume. When pressure is released, gas particles spread back and the plunger returns to its original position.
diagram_description: Syringe shown in three positions — plunger pulled out, thumb over open end, plunger pushed inward — demonstrating gas compressibility
subject: chemistry

summary: Liquids and solids are practically incompressible because their particles are already closely packed with very little interparticle space. Attempting to compress water in a syringe shows no measurable decrease in volume. This contrasts sharply with the high compressibility of gases.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Atoms and Molecules as Constituent Particles
summary: The constituent particles that make up matter are atoms and molecules. Some elements like iron and gold exist as individual atoms, while atoms of hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur combine to form stable molecules (e.g., H₂, H₂O). Students will study atoms and molecules in detail in higher grades.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Acharya Kanad and the Concept of Parmanu
summary: Acharya Kanad, an ancient Indian philosopher, proposed that matter is made up of tiny, indivisible, eternal particles called Parmanu — an early concept analogous to the modern atom. This idea was recorded in his work the Vaisheshika Sutras. It represents one of the earliest known formulations of the particulate nature of matter.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Role of Soap Particles in Cleaning
summary: Soap particles have a dual nature: one end attaches to oil particles on fabric while the other end mixes with water, enabling oil to be lifted off and washed away. This is a practical application of the particulate nature of matter. It illustrates how particle-level interactions drive everyday chemical processes.
diagram_description: Illustration of a person washing clothes with soap, showing soap particles surrounding oil particles on fabric
subject: chemistry

summary: Matter is classified into mixtures and pure substances. Pure substances are further divided into elements and compounds. Elements cannot be broken down further; compounds are formed when elements combine chemically in fixed ratios.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: A mixture consists of two or more substances mixed together where each component retains its own properties and no chemical reaction occurs between them. Components of a mixture can be separated by physical methods. Mixtures are classified as uniform (e.g., sugar in water) or non-uniform (e.g., sprout salad).
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: A pure substance contains only one type of particle and cannot be separated into other kinds of matter by any physical process. In science, 'pure' means the substance is made of a single type of constituent, unlike the common usage meaning unadulterated. Pure substances are either elements or compounds.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Elements are the simplest pure substances that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means. Each element is made of identical particles called atoms, and 118 elements are currently known. Elements are classified as metals (e.g., gold, iron), non-metals (e.g., oxygen, sulfur), or metalloids (e.g., silicon).
diagram_description: Depiction of diatomic molecules: (a) two small circles representing a hydrogen molecule H₂; (b) two larger circles representing an oxygen molecule O₂.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Compounds are formed when two or more elements combine chemically in fixed ratios, producing a new substance with properties entirely different from the constituent elements. The constituent elements of a compound cannot be separated by any physical method. For example, water (H₂O) has hydrogen and oxygen in a 2:1 ratio and extinguishes fire, unlike flammable hydrogen or combustion-supporting oxygen.
diagram_description: Molecular depiction of water showing oxygen atom bonded to two hydrogen atoms in multiple water molecules, illustrating the fixed 2:1 hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Mixtures are classified based on the physical state and distribution of their components. Uniform mixtures have evenly distributed, indistinguishable components; non-uniform mixtures have visible, distinguishable components. Special mixtures of metals called alloys exhibit distinct properties from their constituent metals.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Uniform mixtures have components evenly distributed so they cannot be distinguished even under a microscope (e.g., sugar dissolved in water, air, seawater). Non-uniform mixtures have components generally visible to the naked eye or with a magnifying device (e.g., sprout salad, sand and water).
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Alloys are uniform mixtures of two or more metals (or a metal with non-metals) that have properties distinct from their constituent metals. Examples include stainless steel (iron, nickel, chromium, carbon), brass (copper and zinc), and bronze (copper and tin). Alloys are stronger and more durable than pure constituent metals.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Air is a uniform mixture of mainly nitrogen (~78%), oxygen, argon, carbon dioxide, and water vapour. Dust particles suspended in air are pollutants, not integral components. The Air Quality Index (AQI) describes air quality based on pollutants such as particulate matter, carbon monoxide, and sulfur dioxide.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
summary: Chemical reactions reveal fundamental differences between mixtures and compounds. In mixtures, components retain individual properties and can be separated physically; in compounds, elements combine chemically and cannot be separated physically. Key experiments include electrolysis of water, heating iron with sulfur, and thermal decomposition of sugar.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

parent_concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
summary: Passing electricity through water (with dilute sulfuric acid) decomposes it into hydrogen gas and oxygen gas, confirming water is a compound. Hydrogen is identified by a 'pop' sound with a burning candle; oxygen makes the flame glow brighter. This is a chemical change since new substances with different properties are formed.
diagram_description: Two test tube setups testing collected gases: (a) burning candle producing pop sound confirming hydrogen; (b) flame glowing brighter confirming oxygen.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

parent_concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
summary: A mixture of iron filings and sulfur (Sample A) retains individual properties: iron is attracted by a magnet and reacts with HCl to give odourless hydrogen gas, while sulfur does not react. On heating, they form iron sulfide (Sample B), a compound with uniform black appearance, no magnetic attraction, and reacts with HCl to give foul-smelling hydrogen sulfide gas.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

parent_concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
summary: When sugar is heated, it decomposes to give carbon (charcoal) and water, proving sugar is a compound of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Water droplets form inside the boiling tube, and a black charcoal residue remains. This demonstrates that compounds can be broken down by chemical (not physical) means.
diagram_description: Boiling tube held by test tube holder over spirit lamp showing sugar heating: (a) initial setup; (b) brown sugar charring with water droplets; (c) black charcoal residue on watch glass.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

concept_name: Detection of Carbon Dioxide in Air
subtopic_names: Lime Water Test for Carbon Dioxide
summary: The presence of carbon dioxide in air is confirmed by exposing lime water (calcium hydroxide solution) to air; it turns milky due to formation of insoluble calcium carbonate. This is a standard qualitative test for CO₂. The reaction is: calcium hydroxide + carbon dioxide → calcium carbonate + water.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

concept_name: Lime Water Test for Carbon Dioxide
parent_concept_name: Detection of Carbon Dioxide in Air
summary: Lime water (calcium hydroxide solution) turns milky when exposed to carbon dioxide, forming insoluble white calcium carbonate particles. This is the standard test for detecting CO₂. Lime water left in open air turns milky, confirming the presence of CO₂ in air.
diagram_description: Two petri dishes: (a) clear lime water before exposure to air; (b) milky lime water after CO₂ from air reacts with calcium hydroxide to form calcium carbonate.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

parent_concept_name: Detection of Carbon Dioxide in Air
summary: Calcium oxide (quick lime) reacts vigorously with water in an exothermic reaction to form calcium hydroxide (slaked lime), which dissolves to give lime water. The solution is filtered and used as a reagent for CO₂ detection. The reaction is: CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂.
frequently_confused_with: Lime Water Test for Carbon Dioxide
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Minerals are natural, solid substances found in the Earth with a fixed chemical composition. Most rocks are mixtures of minerals. Minerals can be pure elements (native minerals) like gold or sulfur, or compounds like quartz, calcite, mica, pyroxene, and olivine.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Native minerals are minerals that occur as pure elements in nature, not as compounds. Examples include metals such as gold, silver, and copper, and non-metals such as sulfur and carbon. Gold is simultaneously a native mineral and a metal.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Most minerals are compounds composed of more than one element with a fixed chemical composition. Common examples include quartz, calcite (used in cement), mica, pyroxene, and olivine. Talcum powder is derived from the mineral talc, and cement is made from calcite, quartz, alumina, and iron oxide.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Graphene aerogel is a carbon-based material described as the lightest material on Earth. Its highly porous structure gives it exceptional absorbing capacity, making it useful for cleaning oil spills, fabricating energy-saving devices, and special building coatings.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: Ancient Indian texts such as the Charaka Samhita and Rasaratna Samucchaya described Mishraloha—mixtures of two or more metals with properties distinct from their constituents. Bronze (Kamsya), made of copper and tin, was used medicinally to improve digestion and boost immunity.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

summary: A mixture is a combination of two or more substances. Mixtures can be uniform (components evenly distributed) or non-uniform (components unevenly distributed). Uniform mixtures are called solutions, where the solute dissolves in the solvent.
diagram_description: Magnified schematic showing solute particles evenly distributed in a solvent.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: A uniform mixture has components evenly distributed throughout, as in salt dissolved in water. A non-uniform mixture has unevenly distributed components visible to the naked eye or under magnification, as in sand or sawdust in water.
diagram_description: Side-by-side glasses showing sand in water and sawdust in water as non-uniform mixtures.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: A solution is a uniform mixture formed when a solute dissolves in a solvent. In a solid-liquid solution the solid is the solute and the liquid is the solvent. In a liquid-liquid solution, the component present in smaller amount is the solute and the one in larger amount is the solvent.
diagram_description: Magnified schematic picture of a solute evenly distributed in a solvent inside a beaker.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: A mixture of gases, such as air, forms a uniform mixture and is therefore classified as a solution. The gases are evenly distributed throughout the mixture, satisfying the definition of a solution.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subtopic_names: Solubility and Effect of Temperature on Solids
summary: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a fixed quantity of solvent at a particular temperature. A fixed amount of solvent can only dissolve a limited amount of solute before it becomes saturated. For most solids, solubility increases with increasing temperature.
diagram_description: Two glasses comparing an unsaturated solution (clear) and a saturated solution with undissolved salt at the bottom.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: An unsaturated solution can still dissolve more solute at a given temperature. A saturated solution has dissolved the maximum possible solute at that temperature, and any additional solute remains undissolved at the bottom. A saturated solution becomes unsaturated if temperature is increased.
diagram_description: Two glasses — one showing a clear unsaturated solution and the other showing undissolved salt settling at the bottom of a saturated solution.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Concentration is the amount of solute present in a fixed quantity of solution or solvent. Solutions are described as dilute (less solute) or concentrated (more solute) — these are relative terms. For example, 4 spoons of salt in 50 mL water is more concentrated than 2 spoons in 100 mL water.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

concept_name: Solubility and Effect of Temperature on Solids
summary: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a fixed quantity of solvent at a given temperature. For most solids, solubility increases with increasing temperature — demonstrated by baking soda dissolving more completely at 70 °C than at 50 °C or 20 °C.
diagram_description: Full laboratory setup for heating baking soda in water showing spirit lamp, tripod, wire gauze, beaker, glass rod, thermometer, and laboratory stand.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subtopic_names: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
summary: Gases such as oxygen dissolve in water to form uniform mixtures. Unlike solids, the solubility of gases in liquids generally decreases as temperature increases. Dissolved oxygen in water, though present in minute quantities, is essential for sustaining all aquatic life.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Oxygen dissolves in water only to a small extent but forms a uniform mixture. This dissolved oxygen is vital for aquatic organisms including fish and plants. The mixture of gas in water is uniform because the gas distributes evenly throughout the liquid.
frequently_confused_with: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

concept_name: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
summary: The solubility of gases in liquids generally decreases as temperature increases, which is the opposite of most solids. Cold water dissolves more oxygen than warm water, which is critical for aquatic organisms. Warming of water bodies reduces dissolved oxygen and can threaten aquatic life.
frequently_confused_with: Solubility and Effect of Temperature on Solids
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Density is the mass per unit volume of a substance, expressed as Density = Mass/Volume. The SI unit is kg/m³, but g/mL and g/cm³ are also used for liquids and solids. Density is independent of shape and size but depends on temperature and pressure.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Density is defined as the mass present in a unit volume of a substance: Density = Mass / Volume. The SI unit is kg/m³; other common units include g/mL and g/cm³. The density of a substance is independent of its shape or size but depends on temperature and pressure.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Relative density is the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of water at the same temperature. It is a dimensionless number (no units). For example, aluminium with density 2.7 g/cm³ has a relative density of 2.7 with respect to water.
formula: \text{Relative Density} = \frac{\text{Density of substance}}{\text{Density of water at that temperature}}
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Generally, density decreases with an increase in temperature because particles spread apart, increasing volume while mass stays constant. This explains why hot air rises (less dense than cool air) and how hot air balloons work. Water is a notable exception, with maximum density at 4 °C.
diagram_description: Two-part experimental setup: (a) test tube with water inside a glass tube on a stand; (b) same test tube placed in a beaker of hot water showing the water level rising in the glass tube.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Pressure affects density differently across states of matter. Increasing pressure compresses gas particles closer, decreasing volume and increasing density significantly. Liquids are nearly incompressible so pressure has a small effect, and solids are even less affected — changes in their density under pressure are negligible.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Mass (quantity of matter) is measured using a balance in grams or kilograms. Volume (space occupied) is measured using a measuring cylinder for liquids, or by calculation for regular solids, or by water displacement for irregular solids. These measurements together allow density to be calculated.
diagram_description: Digital weighing balance and measuring cylinders of various capacities shown as laboratory apparatus.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Mass is the quantity of matter in an object, measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg) using a balance. A digital weighing balance is tared (zeroed) before placing the object to obtain an accurate reading. Most balances measure weight (in newtons) but display values in mass units.
diagram_description: Digital weighing balance with watch glass being tared, then a stone placed on the pan reading 16.400 g.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Volume of a liquid is measured using a measuring cylinder; the reading is taken at the bottom of the meniscus (curved liquid surface) for colourless liquids, and at the top for coloured liquids. Eyes must be level with the meniscus to avoid parallax error. A narrower, taller cylinder gives better accuracy.
diagram_description: Correct eye-level reading at the bottom of the meniscus of water in a 100 mL measuring cylinder showing the 50 mL mark.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: The volume of an irregular solid (e.g. a stone) is determined by water displacement: the solid is immersed in a measuring cylinder and the rise in water level equals the solid's volume. Volume of displaced water (in mL) equals volume of solid (in cm³) since 1 mL = 1 cm³.
diagram_description: Two measuring cylinders side by side: one with water at 50 mL and one with a stone submerged raising the level to 55 mL.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Water reaches its maximum density at 4 °C. When cooled below 4 °C to form ice at 0 °C, water expands — particles rearrange into a structure that occupies more space. This makes ice less dense than liquid water, causing it to float, which insulates aquatic life in frozen water bodies.
diagram_description: Photograph of ice floating on a water surface.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Water is considered a highly versatile solvent used extensively in traditional Indian medicine systems like Ayurveda and Siddha for preparing medicinal formulations. Other solvents such as oils, ghee, milk, and alcohol-water mixtures are also used in these systems for specific therapeutic benefits.
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

summary: Mixtures are classified based on uniformity of composition and particle size. Homogeneous mixtures (solutions) have uniform composition throughout, while heterogeneous mixtures do not. A further classification into solutions, suspensions, and colloids is based on the size of dispersed particles.
diagram_description: Beakers showing homogeneous mixture (clear, uniform) and heterogeneous mixture (visibly non-uniform with settled particles).

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: A homogeneous mixture has uniform composition throughout, meaning every part of the mixture is identical. Examples include sugar solution, vinegar, and aerated drinks. These are also called solutions and always remain homogeneous.
diagram_description: Beaker showing a clear, uniform sugar solution labelled as a homogeneous mixture.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: A heterogeneous mixture does not have uniform composition; its components are not evenly distributed. Particles are visible and may settle over time. Examples include sand in water, oil in water, and smoke in air.
diagram_description: Beaker showing sand particles visibly settled in water, labelled as a heterogeneous mixture.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Suspensions are heterogeneous mixtures in which undissolved solid particles (greater than 1000 nm) remain suspended in a liquid medium. Particles are visible to the naked eye and settle upon standing. Examples include muddy water and sawdust in water.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Colloids are mixtures with particle sizes between 1–1000 nm that appear homogeneous but are heterogeneous in nature. Particles do not settle and cannot be separated by ordinary filtration. Examples include milk, blood, tomato sauce, and ice cream. The components are called the dispersed phase and dispersion medium.
diagram_description: Three beakers side by side showing a clear solution, a turbid suspension, and a translucent colloid for visual comparison.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Concentration expresses the amount of solute dissolved in a given amount of solvent or solution. It is essential in medicine, agriculture, and food science. The three common percentage methods are mass/mass, mass/volume, and volume/volume.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: Mass by mass percentage expresses how many grams of solute are present in 100 grams of total solution. It is calculated as (mass of solute / mass of solution) × 100. Used for solid-solid and solid-liquid mixtures such as packaged foods and milk powder.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: Mass by volume percentage gives the number of grams of solute present in 100 mL of solution. It is calculated as (mass of solute / volume of solution) × 100. Commonly used in medicine and laboratories, e.g., 5% glucose intravenous solution and 0.9% saline drip.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: Volume by volume percentage gives the number of millilitres of solute present in 100 mL of solution. It is used when both components are liquids, such as in vinegar (5% v/v acetic acid), perfumes, and cosmetics. Calculated as (volume of solute / volume of solution) × 100.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that dissolves in 100 g or 100 mL of solvent at a given temperature. A solution that cannot dissolve any more solute is saturated. Solubility of solids in liquids generally increases with temperature, while solubility of gases in liquids generally decreases with temperature.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: The solubility of solid solutes in liquid solvents generally increases with increasing temperature. In contrast, the solubility of gases dissolved in liquids generally decreases with increasing temperature. This temperature dependence is exploited in crystallization to separate and purify substances.

builds_upon.concept_name: Solubility and Effect of Temperature on Solids
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

builds_upon.concept_name: Role of Thermal Energy in State Changes

subject: chemistry

summary: A solubility curve is a graph of solubility (g per 100 g of water) versus temperature. It allows prediction of how much solute dissolves at any given temperature and how much solid will crystallise when a saturated solution is cooled. Different substances have different curve shapes and slopes.
diagram_description: Graph showing solubility curves of two compounds A and B in water, with solubility on the y-axis and temperature on the x-axis, with values at 40°C and 60°C marked.
subject: chemistry

summary: Homogeneous mixtures require specific techniques to separate their components since they appear uniform. The main methods covered are crystallization (for solids from solution), distillation (for miscible liquids or liquid-solid), and paper chromatography (for dissolved compounds with different mobilities).
subject: chemistry

summary: Crystallization is the process of forming pure solid crystals from a saturated solution by cooling it slowly. It exploits differences in solubility at different temperatures to separate and purify solids. For example, cooling a saturated copper sulfate solution yields blue crystals of copper sulfate. It is preferred over simple evaporation when a pure solid is required.
diagram_description: Four-step diagram showing: preparing a saturated copper sulfate solution in a water bath, filtering the hot solution, cooling the filtrate, and collecting the dried crystals on a watch glass.
subject: chemistry

summary: Distillation separates miscible liquids by heating the mixture so the component with the lower boiling point vaporises first, then condensing the vapour back to a pure liquid. It requires a difference of at least 25°C in boiling points. For example, acetone (b.p. 56°C) is separated from water (b.p.
diagram_description: Labelled distillation setup showing distillation flask, thermometer, water condenser with inlet and outlet, tripod stand, burner, wire gauze, and collection conical flask.
subject: chemistry

summary: Paper chromatography separates dissolved components of a mixture based on differences in their rates of movement along chromatographic paper as a solvent rises through it. The sample spot is placed below the solvent level on the paper strip. It is used to separate ink pigments, leaf pigments, and food dyes. The word 'chromatography' derives from Greek for 'colour writing'.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a chromatographic paper strip with pencil line and ink spot placed in a gas jar with water, illustrating the setup for paper chromatography.
subject: chemistry

summary: Heterogeneous mixtures are separated using properties like density, volatility, and particle size. Methods include using a separating funnel for immiscible liquids, sublimation for volatile solids, centrifugation for fine solid-liquid mixtures, and coagulation for colloidal/suspended particles.
subject: chemistry

summary: Immiscible liquids form distinct layers based on their different densities and can be separated using a separating funnel. The denser liquid forms the lower layer and is drained first through the stopcock. For example, mustard oil (less dense) forms the upper layer and water the lower layer.
diagram_description: Labelled diagram of a separating funnel on a laboratory stand showing mustard oil (upper layer) and water (lower layer) with stopcock, glass stopper, and collection conical flask.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

subject: chemistry

summary: Sublimation is the direct transition of a solid to vapour below its melting point, without passing through the liquid state. The reverse process (vapour to solid without becoming liquid) is called deposition. It is used to separate sublimable substances like camphor or naphthalene from non-sublimable solids like sand.
diagram_description: Apparatus for sublimation of camphor: china dish with camphor-sand mixture on tripod stand with wire gauze, inverted glass funnel plugged with cotton, and burner below.
subject: chemistry

summary: Centrifugation separates components of a mixture by spinning tubes at high speed, causing the centrifugal force to push heavier particles to the bottom of the tube while the lighter liquid remains at the top. It is used to separate blood components (red blood cells from plasma) and in chemical industries.
diagram_description: Outer and inner views of a centrifugation machine, showing sample tubes arranged in a rotor inside the machine.
subject: chemistry

summary: Coagulation is the process of adding a coagulant (such as alum) to a suspension to cause fine particles to clump together into larger aggregates that settle by gravity. The settled clumps are then removed by decantation or filtration. It is used in water purification and also in making paneer (using lemon juice or vinegar as coagulant).
diagram_description: Three-panel diagram showing powdered alum being added to muddy water, alum coagulating suspended impurities, and the settled impurities at the bottom of the beaker.
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Light Scattering in Colloids and Suspensions
summary: The Tyndall effect is the scattering of a light beam by particles in a colloid or suspension, making the beam's path visible. It does not occur in true solutions because solute particles are too small to scatter light. Named after scientist John Tyndall, it is used to distinguish colloids and suspensions from solutions.
diagram_description: Three beakers with laser light passing through: salt solution (beam invisible), chalk-water suspension (beam visible), and milk-water colloid (beam visible), demonstrating the Tyndall effect.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Light Scattering in Colloids and Suspensions
summary: When a beam of light passes through a colloid or suspension, the dispersed particles scatter the light making the beam path visible from the side. This scattering occurs because colloid particles (1–1000 nm) and suspension particles (>1000 nm) are large enough to interact with visible light. Real-world examples include sunlight through forest gaps, floodlights in stadiums, and fog.
subject: chemistry

summary: Solutions, colloids, and suspensions are distinguished by particle size, settling behaviour, filterability, and the Tyndall effect. Solutions (<1 nm) do not scatter light, do not settle, and pass through filter paper. Colloids (1–1000 nm) scatter light but do not settle. Suspensions (>1000 nm) scatter light and settle upon standing.
frequently_confused_with: Light Scattering in Colloids and Suspensions
subject: chemistry

summary: An alloy is a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal, formed by mixing them in the molten state. Alloys cannot be separated by physical methods. Common examples are brass (Cu + Zn), bronze (Cu + Sn), and stainless steel (Fe + C + Cr + Ni + Mo), which are stronger and more corrosion-resistant than pure metals.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Emulsions are colloids in which both the dispersed phase and dispersion medium are liquids. Oil-in-water emulsions include milk and vanishing cream; water-in-oil emulsions include butter and body lotion. Emulsifying agents (such as proteins in milk) stabilise emulsions by preventing phase separation.
subject: chemistry

summary: The Deg-Bhapka method is a traditional distillation technique used in Kannauj, Uttar Pradesh (India's perfume capital) to extract natural fragrances from flowers and plant materials. It is used to make perfumes such as Mitti ka Ittar (earthy fragrance). This method has been passed down through generations and demonstrates an ancient application of distillation.
subject: chemistry

summary: A paperfuge is a low-cost, hand-powered centrifuge made from cardboard and string that can spin blood samples at high speed without electricity. It separates heavier blood components from lighter ones and can help detect diseases like malaria and anaemia in remote areas. It applies the principle of centrifugation using ideas from a common spinning toy.
subject: chemistry

summary: Atomic models evolved from Dalton's indivisible sphere through Thomson's plum pudding, Rutherford's nuclear model, and Bohr's quantised shell model. Each model was revised as new experimental evidence emerged. The quantum mechanical model is the modern description.
diagram_description: Timeline diagram showing progression from Dalton's to Thomson's to Rutherford's to Bohr's to modern quantum mechanical atomic model.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Thomson proposed that an atom is a sphere of uniformly distributed positive charge with electrons embedded throughout it, like plums in a pudding or seeds in a watermelon. This model maintained electrical neutrality by balancing positive and negative charges. It was the first model to incorporate subatomic particles.
diagram_description: Diagram of Thomson's atom showing electrons (dots) distributed within a positive sphere.
subject: chemistry

summary: Based on the gold foil experiment, Rutherford concluded that an atom is mostly empty space with a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus containing most of the mass, and electrons revolving around it like planets around the Sun. The nucleus diameter (~10⁻¹⁵ m) is about 10⁵ times smaller than the atom (~10⁻¹⁰ m). This model could not explain atomic stability.
diagram_description: Planetary model diagram showing nucleus at centre with electrons orbiting in circular paths around it.
subject: chemistry

summary: Niels Bohr (1913) proposed that electrons revolve in fixed circular paths called shells or stationary states, each with a definite energy. Electrons do not lose energy while in a fixed shell, explaining atomic stability. Shells are labelled K, L, M, N (n=1,2,3,4) and energy increases away from the nucleus. Electrons jump between shells by absorbing or releasing fixed amounts of energy.
diagram_description: Concentric circles representing K, L, M, N energy levels around a central nucleus.
subject: chemistry

summary: Atoms are composed of three subatomic particles: negatively charged electrons, positively charged protons, and neutral neutrons. Protons and neutrons reside in the nucleus; electrons revolve around it. The relative charges are −1, +1, and 0 respectively.

builds_upon.concept_name: Atoms and Molecules as Constituent Particles

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Electrons are negatively charged subatomic particles discovered by J.J. Thomson through cathode ray tube experiments. They have a charge of −1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (taken as −1 by convention) and negligible mass compared to protons and neutrons. Electrons are present in all atoms and their number equals the number of protons in a neutral atom.
diagram_description: Line diagram of a cathode ray tube showing cathode, anode, high voltage supply, and direction of ray travel.
subject: chemistry

summary: Protons are positively charged subatomic particles discovered and named by Rutherford. They reside in the nucleus, carry a charge of +1, and have a mass much greater than electrons. The number of protons in the nucleus defines the atomic number and the identity of the element.
subject: chemistry

summary: Neutrons are neutral subatomic particles discovered by James Chadwick in 1932, with mass nearly equal to that of a proton. They are found in the nucleus of all atoms except ordinary hydrogen. Neutrons contribute to mass number and help stabilise the nucleus by reducing proton-proton repulsion through nuclear force.
subject: chemistry

summary: The atomic number (Z) is the number of protons in the nucleus and uniquely identifies an element. The mass number (A) is the total number of protons and neutrons (nucleons) in the nucleus. These two numbers along with the element symbol form the standard atomic notation.
subject: chemistry

summary: The atomic number (Z) is defined as the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. Since a neutral atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons, Z also equals the number of electrons. For example, hydrogen has Z = 1 and helium has Z = 2. Atomic number uniquely identifies an element.
subject: chemistry

summary: The mass number (A) is the total count of protons and neutrons (collectively called nucleons) in an atom's nucleus. Electron mass is negligible and ignored. In standard notation, mass number is written as a superscript and atomic number as a subscript before the element symbol, e.g. ¹²₆C.
subject: chemistry

summary: Electronic configuration describes how electrons are distributed among the shells (K, L, M, N) of an atom. The Bohr-Bury rules govern the maximum capacity of each shell and the order of filling. The configuration determines the chemical properties and combining capacity of an element.
diagram_description: Schematic atomic structures of the first 18 elements showing electrons filled in K, L, and M shells.
subject: chemistry

summary: According to Bohr and Bury, the maximum electrons in any shell is given by 2n², so K=2, L=8, M=18. The outermost shell can hold at most 8 electrons. Shells fill in sequence from the nucleus outward (K, L, M, N…), and an outer shell begins filling only after the inner shell is complete.
subject: chemistry

summary: The outermost shell of an atom is the valence shell; its electrons are valence electrons. Atoms with a complete octet (8 electrons, or 2 for helium) are stable and unreactive. Valency is the number of electrons gained, lost, or shared to complete the octet. For example, sodium (2,8,1) loses 1 electron → valency 1; oxygen (2,6) gains 2 → valency 2; carbon (2,4) shares 4 → valency 4.
subject: chemistry

summary: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same atomic number but different mass numbers due to differing neutron counts. Isobars are atoms of different elements sharing the same mass number but different atomic numbers. These concepts explain why atomic masses are non-integer values.
subject: chemistry

summary: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same atomic number (Z) but different mass numbers (A) due to varying numbers of neutrons. For example, hydrogen has three isotopes: protium (¹H), deuterium (²H), and tritium (³H). Isotopes have identical chemical properties (same electron configuration) but different physical properties like boiling and melting points.
diagram_description: Schematic diagrams of three isotopes of hydrogen (protium, deuterium, tritium) and three isotopes of carbon (C-12, C-13, C-14) showing different numbers of neutrons.
subject: chemistry

summary: Average atomic mass is the weighted mean of the masses of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element, accounting for their relative abundances. A simple arithmetic mean ignores abundance and gives an inaccurate value. For chlorine (⁷⁵% ³⁵Cl and ²⁵% ³⁷Cl), the weighted average is 35.5 u.
subject: chemistry

summary: Isobars are atoms of different elements that have the same mass number but different atomic numbers. For example, calcium (Z=20), potassium (Z=19), and argon (Z=18) all have mass number 40. Unlike isotopes, isobars have different chemical properties because they have different numbers of protons and electrons.
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Gold Foil Experiment Setup and Observations
summary: Geiger and Marsden (under Rutherford, 1911) directed alpha particles at a thin gold foil. Most passed undeflected, some were deflected at large angles, and a few bounced back. These results disproved Thomson's model and led to the nuclear model of the atom.
diagram_description: Schematic view of the gold foil experiment showing a beam of alpha particles striking thin gold foil with paths showing undeflected, deflected, and back-scattered particles.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Gold Foil Experiment Setup and Observations
summary: A narrow beam of positively charged alpha particles was aimed at an extremely thin gold foil. Most particles passed straight through undeflected, indicating empty space. Some were deflected at large angles, and a very few bounced back, suggesting a concentrated positive mass. Alpha particles are helium nuclei containing two protons and two neutrons.
diagram_description: Diagram showing beam of alpha particles hitting thin gold foil with paths of undeflected, slightly deflected, and sharply deflected particles.
subject: chemistry

summary: Rutherford concluded that most of the atom is empty space (most particles passed undeflected), the positive charge and mass are concentrated in an extremely small, dense nucleus (few particles bounced back), and electrons orbit the nucleus. Thomson's plum pudding model was disproved because it could not account for large-angle deflections.

builds_upon.concept_name: Gold Foil Experiment Setup and Observations

frequently_confused_with: Gold Foil Experiment Setup and Observations
subject: chemistry

summary: Elements are represented by standardised symbols approved by IUPAC, typically the first one or two letters of the element's English or Latin name. The first letter is always uppercase and the second (if present) lowercase. Some symbols derive from Latin, Greek, or German names, e.g. Fe (ferrum), Hg (hydrargyros), W (wolfram).

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: IUPAC approves standard one- or two-letter symbols for elements derived from their English or classical names. The first letter is uppercase and the second (if present) is lowercase. Examples: Al (aluminium), Fe (iron from Latin ferrum), Na (sodium from Latin natrium). Standardisation removes language barriers among international scientists.
subject: chemistry

summary: John Dalton introduced the first systematic pictorial symbols for elements in 1803 to simplify the study of chemistry. Each element had a unique drawn symbol (e.g. a circle with a dot for hydrogen, a shaded circle for carbon). Berzelius later replaced these in 1813 with alphabetic symbols derived from Latin names.
diagram_description: A set of Dalton's original pictorial symbols for elements including hydrogen, carbon, oxygen, phosphorus, sulphur, iron, copper, lead, silver, gold, platinum, and mercury.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: In Rutherford's model, a revolving electron continuously accelerates, so according to classical physics it should emit energy and spiral inward into the nucleus, causing atomic collapse. Since atoms are stable in reality, this was a fundamental flaw. Bohr's model addressed this by postulating stationary states where electrons do not radiate energy.
diagram_description: Diagram showing the spiral inward path an electron would follow if it lost energy continuously while orbiting a positive nucleus.
subject: chemistry

summary: Several isotopes have important practical applications: ²³⁵U fuels nuclear reactors; ⁶⁰Co is used in cancer radiation therapy; ¹³¹I treats goitre and thyroid cancer; ¹⁴C is used in radiocarbon dating of fossils and artefacts. These applications span energy, medicine, and archaeology.
subject: chemistry

summary: Because atoms are too small to measure in kilograms or grams, scientists use the unified atomic mass unit (u) to express atomic masses. One u is defined relative to the mass of a carbon-12 atom. Previously abbreviated 'amu', the modern symbol is 'u'.
subject: chemistry

summary: Ancient Indian philosopher Acharya Kanada proposed that matter (dravya) can be divided until reaching indivisible particles called parmanus, recorded in the Vaisesika Sutras. Independently, Greek philosophers Leucippus and Democritus called these particles atomos (indivisible). John Dalton formalised this as scientific atomic theory in 1808 based on experimental evidence.

builds_upon.concept_name: Acharya Kanad and the Concept of Parmanu

subject: chemistry

summary: Two foundational laws govern all chemical reactions: mass is neither created nor destroyed, and compounds always contain elements in fixed mass ratios. These laws together formed the experimental basis for Dalton's Atomic Theory.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Proposed by Antoine Lavoisier in 1789, this law states that matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. The total mass of reactants always equals the total mass of products. For example, 4.0 g CaCO₃ + 2.92 g HCl gives exactly 6.92 g of products.

builds_upon.concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Proposed by Joseph Louis Proust, this law states that a compound always contains the same elements combined in a fixed ratio by mass, regardless of its source or method of preparation. For example, pure water always contains hydrogen and oxygen in a mass ratio of 1:8.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Limitations and Context of Dalton's Theory
summary: John Dalton proposed in 1808 that all matter is made of tiny indivisible atoms that participate in chemical reactions by rearranging rather than being created or destroyed. This theory explained both the Law of Conservation of Mass and the Law of Constant Proportions at the atomic level.
subject: chemistry

summary: Dalton postulated that all matter is made of atoms that cannot be created or destroyed; atoms of a given element are identical in mass and properties; atoms of different elements differ in mass; and atoms combine in simple whole number ratios to form compounds with constant composition.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Limitations and Context of Dalton's Theory
summary: Dalton's postulate that atoms are indivisible was later revised with the discovery of electrons, protons, and neutrons. However, the core idea that atoms rearrange without being created or destroyed in chemical reactions remains valid and underpins conservation laws.
subject: chemistry

summary: Atoms combine by sharing or transferring valence electrons to attain a stable octet (or duplet for H). The force holding atoms together is called a chemical bond. When atoms bond, the total energy of the system decreases, making the arrangement more stable.
subject: chemistry

summary: A covalent bond forms when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons so each attains a stable configuration. Sharing one pair gives a single bond (H–H, Cl–Cl); sharing two pairs gives a double bond (O=O). Covalent bonds occur typically between non-metal atoms.
diagram_description: Electron dot diagrams showing formation of H₂, Cl₂, and O₂ molecules by electron sharing.
subject: chemistry

summary: An ionic bond forms by the complete transfer of electrons from one atom (forming a cation) to another (forming an anion), held together by electrostatic attraction. For example, Na loses one electron to form Na⁺ and Cl gains it to form Cl⁻, producing NaCl.
diagram_description: Diagram showing electron transfer from sodium atom to chlorine atom forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions that combine to give NaCl.
subject: chemistry

summary: Atoms of the same element can share electrons to form molecules. H₂ and Cl₂ form single bonds; O₂ forms a double bond; N₂ forms a triple bond. Noble gases like helium exist as single atoms because their valence shells are already complete.
diagram_description: Electron shell diagrams showing formation of H₂ (single bond) and O₂ (double bond) from individual atoms.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Atoms of different elements share electrons to form covalent compound molecules. In HCl, H and Cl each share one electron forming a single bond; in H₂O, two H atoms each share one electron with O, giving the formula H₂O. The formula indicates the exact number of each type of atom.
diagram_description: Electron shell diagrams for HCl and H₂O showing covalent bond formation between different elements.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: Systematic naming of compounds uses prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-) for covalent compounds and cation-then-anion order with -ide suffix for ionic compounds. The naming rules differ depending on whether the compound is covalent or ionic.
subject: chemistry

summary: Covalent compounds are named using Greek prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-, penta-, hexa-) to indicate atom counts; mono- is omitted for the first element. The second element ends in -ide. For example, CO is carbon monoxide and CO₂ is carbon dioxide.
subject: chemistry

summary: Ionic compounds are named by stating the cation name first, then the anion with an -ide suffix for simple anions (e.g., sodium chloride). Polyatomic anions retain their own names without -ide (e.g., sodium sulfate). Metals with variable valency specify the charge in parentheses (e.g., iron(II), iron(III)).
subject: chemistry

summary: Chemical formulae are written by the criss-cross method: write element symbols, note their valencies or ionic charges, then swap them as subscripts. For ionic compounds the subscripts are simplified to lowest whole number ratios. Polyatomic ions are enclosed in brackets when subscript >1.
subject: chemistry

summary: Write element symbols, record valencies, then criss-cross to obtain subscripts. If the resulting valency for an element is 1, no subscript is written. For example, H (valency 1) with S (valency 2) gives H₂S; C (valency 4) with Cl (valency 1) gives CCl₄.
subject: chemistry

summary: Write the cation symbol first then the anion, record ionic charges, and criss-cross the numbers as subscripts. Simplify subscripts to lowest ratio (e.g., Mg₂O₂ → MgO). Brackets enclose polyatomic ions when subscript >1 (e.g., Mg(OH)₂, Al₂(SO₄)₃).
subject: chemistry

summary: Ions are charged species formed by electron gain or loss. Common cations include Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Al³⁺; common anions include Cl⁻, O²⁻, SO₄²⁻. Polyatomic ions (OH⁻, NO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, SO₄²⁻, NH₄⁺) consist of two or more atoms and carry a net charge. Valency equals the magnitude of the ionic charge.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Properties of Ionic and Covalent Compounds
summary: Ionic and covalent compounds differ systematically in solubility, electrical conductivity, and melting/boiling points due to differences in bonding. These differences can be experimentally observed through simple solubility and conductivity tests.
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Properties of Ionic and Covalent Compounds
summary: Ionic compounds are generally soluble in water but insoluble in non-polar solvents like kerosene and petrol. They have high melting and boiling points due to strong electrostatic inter-ionic attractions. In the solid state, ions are locked in a crystal lattice and cannot move.
diagram_description: Diagrams of NaCl crystal structure showing alternating Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions in a 3-D lattice arrangement.
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Properties of Ionic and Covalent Compounds
summary: Most covalent compounds are insoluble in water but dissolve in non-polar solvents like kerosene and petrol. They typically have low melting and boiling points. Some covalent compounds (e.g., sugar) dissolve in water but do not ionise, so they do not conduct electricity.
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Properties of Ionic and Covalent Compounds
summary: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when dissolved in water (or in molten state) because ions become free to move, but not in the solid state where ions are fixed. Covalent compounds generally do not conduct electricity because they do not produce ions, even when dissolved in water.
diagram_description: Experimental set-up with electrodes, bulb, and battery testing electrical conductivity of a solution in a beaker.
subject: chemistry

summary: The mass of a molecule or formula unit is calculated by summing the atomic masses of all constituent atoms. Molecular mass applies to covalent compounds; formula unit mass applies to ionic compounds that do not form discrete molecules.
subject: chemistry

summary: Molecular mass is the sum of atomic masses of all atoms in one molecule of a covalent compound. For H₂O: (1×2) + (16×1) = 18 u. For CO₂: (12×1) + (16×2) = 44 u. It is expressed in atomic mass units (u).
subject: chemistry

summary: For ionic compounds, which exist as crystal lattices rather than discrete molecules, the formula unit mass is the sum of atomic masses of all atoms in the simplest formula unit. For example, Na₂O: (23×2) + (16×1) = 62 u; Ca(NO₃)₂: 40 + 2(14 + 48) = 164 u.
subject: chemistry

summary: Ionic compounds form three-dimensional crystal lattices in which each ion is surrounded by oppositely charged ions in a repeating pattern. In NaCl, each Na⁺ is surrounded by six Cl⁻ ions and vice versa. This extended structure explains their high melting points and brittleness.
diagram_description: NaCl crystal structure diagram showing alternating Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, crystal lattice dot representation, and photograph of NaCl crystals.
subject: chemistry

summary: Atoms release enormous energy when nuclei split (fission) or combine (fusion), termed nuclear or atomic energy. In nuclear power plants, this thermal energy generates electricity. India's nuclear programme was advanced by scientists like Raja Ramanna, the Father of the Indian Nuclear Programme.

builds_upon.concept_name: Limitations and Context of Dalton's Theory

subject: chemistry

summary: Cinnabar (HgS), known as hingula in India, always contains mercury and sulfur in a fixed mass percentage of approximately 86.22% and 13.78% regardless of source, illustrating the Law of Constant Proportions in a historical context.
subject: chemistry

summary: A chemical reaction involves the breaking and making of bonds between atoms to form new substances with different properties. Indicators include change in state, colour, gas evolution, or temperature. Reactions are represented symbolically as chemical equations with reactants on the left and products on the right.

builds_upon.concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: A chemical reaction can be identified by observable changes: change in state (solid to gas), change in colour (blue CuSO4 fading), evolution of a gas (H2 from acid-zinc), or change in temperature (heat released or absorbed). These physical observations signal that new substances have formed.

builds_upon.concept_name: Chemical Reactions Distinguishing Mixtures from Compounds
builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

subject: chemistry

summary: A chemical equation uses chemical formulae to represent a reaction concisely, with reactants on the LHS and products on the RHS separated by an arrow. Physical states are denoted (s), (l), (g), (aq). Reaction conditions such as temperature, pressure, or catalyst may be written above or below the arrow. A skeletal equation has correct formulae but may be unbalanced.
subject: chemistry

summary: Balancing ensures the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides, consistent with the law of conservation of mass. Coefficients (not subscripts) are adjusted using the hit-and-trial method. The fully balanced equation for iron reacting with steam is 3Fe(s) + 4H2O(g) → Fe3O4(s) + 4H2(g).
subject: chemistry

summary: Chemical reactions are classified based on the nature of bond breaking and making. The major types covered are combination, decomposition, displacement, double displacement, and oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions. Each type has characteristic reactant-product relationships and can often be identified from the equation structure.
subject: chemistry

summary: In a combination reaction, two or more substances (elements or compounds) combine to form a single new product. Example: CaO(s) + H2O(l) → Ca(OH)2(aq) + Heat. Many combination reactions are exothermic. Other examples include burning of coal and formation of water from hydrogen and oxygen.
diagram_description: Beaker showing calcium oxide reacting vigorously with water (slaked lime formation).
subject: chemistry

summary: A decomposition reaction breaks a single reactant into two or more simpler products, requiring energy as heat (thermal), light (photo), or electricity (electrolytic). Examples: CaCO3 → CaO + CO2 (thermal); 2AgCl → 2Ag + Cl2 (photodecomposition); 2H2O → 2H2 + O2 (electrolysis). These reactions are endothermic.
diagram_description: Electrolysis of water apparatus showing hydrogen and oxygen gas collection at cathode and anode respectively.
subject: chemistry

summary: In a displacement reaction, a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound in solution. Example: Fe(s) + CuSO4(aq) → FeSO4(aq) + Cu(s). Iron displaces copper because iron is more reactive. Zinc and lead similarly displace copper from its compounds.
diagram_description: Iron nails dipped in copper sulphate solution showing colour change of nails and fading of blue solution.
subject: chemistry

summary: In a double displacement reaction, two ionic compounds exchange their ions to form two new compounds. One product is often an insoluble precipitate. Example: Na2SO4(aq) + BaCl2(aq) → BaSO4(s)↓ + 2NaCl(aq). The white BaSO4 precipitate forms due to exchange of SO4²⁻ and Ba²⁺ ions.
diagram_description: Test tube showing mixing of sodium sulphate and barium chloride solutions forming a white precipitate of barium sulphate.
subject: chemistry

summary: Oxidation is the gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen; reduction is the loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen. These processes always occur simultaneously in a redox reaction. Example: CuO + H2 → Cu + H2O — CuO is reduced (loses O) while H2 is oxidised (gains O).
subject: chemistry

summary: In a redox reaction, one reactant is oxidised (gains O or loses H) while another is simultaneously reduced (loses O or gains H). Example: ZnO + C → Zn + CO — carbon is oxidised to CO and ZnO is reduced to Zn. Another example is MnO2 + 4HCl → MnCl2 + 2H2O + Cl2.
diagram_description: China dish with copper powder being heated, showing oxidation of copper to black copper oxide.
subject: chemistry

summary: Corrosion is the slow oxidative attack on metals by substances in their environment such as moisture and acids, degrading them over time. Rusting of iron produces reddish-brown iron oxide coating; silver tarnishes black and copper turns green. Corrosion causes significant economic damage to metal structures and is prevented by painting or coating.
subject: chemistry

summary: Rancidity occurs when fats and oils in food are oxidised, causing unpleasant smell and taste. It is prevented by adding antioxidants, storing food in airtight containers, refrigerating, or flushing packaging with inert nitrogen gas to exclude oxygen. Chips packets are flushed with nitrogen for this reason.
subject: chemistry

summary: Chemical reactions can be classified by their energy exchange with surroundings. Exothermic reactions release heat (e.g. combustion, respiration, slaking of lime). Endothermic reactions absorb energy from surroundings (e.g.
subject: chemistry

summary: Exothermic reactions release heat energy along with the products, raising the temperature of the surroundings. Examples include combustion of natural gas (CH4 + 2O2 → CO2 + 2H2O + heat), burning of coal, slaking of lime (CaO + H2O → Ca(OH)2 + heat), and cellular respiration (C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O + energy).
subject: chemistry

summary: Endothermic reactions absorb energy from the surroundings in the form of heat, light, or electricity. Decomposition reactions are typically endothermic; for example, electrolysis of water requires electrical energy, and photodecomposition of AgCl requires light energy. The surroundings cool during endothermic reactions.
subject: chemistry

summary: Slaked lime Ca(OH)2 applied to walls reacts slowly with atmospheric CO2 to form calcium carbonate CaCO3, giving a shiny white finish. The same formula CaCO3 is shared by marble. The reaction is: Ca(OH)2(aq) + CO2(g) → CaCO3(s) + H2O(l).
subject: chemistry

summary: Silver chloride and silver bromide decompose in sunlight to produce silver metal and the corresponding halogen gas. This photodecomposition is the chemical basis of black-and-white photography. 2AgCl(s) → 2Ag(s) + Cl2(g); 2AgBr(s) → 2Ag(s) + Br2(g).
diagram_description: China dish with silver chloride placed in sunlight turning grey due to photodecomposition to silver metal.
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases
Reactions of Metal Carbonates and Hydrogencarbonates with Acids
Reactions of Metal Oxides and Non-Metal Oxides
summary: Acids are substances that taste sour, turn blue litmus red, and produce H⁺(aq) ions in solution. Bases are bitter, turn red litmus blue, and produce OH⁻(aq) ions in solution. Acids and bases react with metals, carbonates, and each other in characteristic ways.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

frequently_confused_with: pH Scale and Strength of Acids and Bases
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases
summary: Acids are sour, turn blue litmus red, and give red/pink colour with methyl orange; bases are bitter, turn red litmus blue, and give pink with phenolphthalein. Natural indicators include litmus (from lichen), turmeric, and flower petals; olfactory indicators like onion change odour in acidic or basic media. Synthetic indicators such as methyl orange and phenolphthalein are also used.
frequently_confused_with: pH Scale and Strength of Acids and Bases
subject: chemistry

summary: Acids react with metals to produce hydrogen gas and a salt: Acid + Metal → Salt + Hydrogen gas. Bases like NaOH also react with certain metals (e.g., zinc) to produce hydrogen gas and a salt with a negative ion composed of the metal and oxygen, such as sodium zincate (Na₂ZnO₂). The hydrogen gas evolved burns with a pop sound.
diagram_description: Apparatus showing zinc granules reacting with dilute sulphuric acid, hydrogen gas passing through soap solution to form bubbles, which burn with a candle.

builds_upon.concept_name: Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases

frequently_confused_with: Reactions of Metal Carbonates and Hydrogencarbonates with Acids
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Reactions of Metal Carbonates and Hydrogencarbonates with Acids
summary: Metal carbonates and hydrogencarbonates react with acids to give a salt, carbon dioxide gas, and water. The CO₂ produced turns lime water milky due to formation of calcium carbonate precipitate. Limestone, chalk, and marble are all forms of calcium carbonate.
diagram_description: Apparatus showing CO₂ gas evolved from reaction of Na₂CO₃ with HCl being passed through lime water (calcium hydroxide solution) in a separate test tube.

builds_upon.concept_name: Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

summary: A neutralisation reaction occurs when an acid reacts with a base to produce a salt and water. At the ionic level, H⁺(aq) from the acid combines with OH⁻(aq) from the base to form water. For example, NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O; the reaction is confirmed by phenolphthalein losing its pink colour on acid addition.

builds_upon.concept_name: Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases

frequently_confused_with: Reactions of Metal Carbonates and Hydrogencarbonates with Acids
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Reactions of Metal Oxides and Non-Metal Oxides
summary: Metal oxides react with acids to give salt and water (Metal oxide + Acid → Salt + Water), so they are called basic oxides; e.g., CuO + HCl gives blue-green copper(II) chloride solution. Non-metal oxides (e.g., CO₂) react with bases to give salt and water, so they are acidic in nature. This parallels the reactions of bases and acids respectively.

builds_upon.concept_name: Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
summary: All acids produce H⁺(aq) or hydronium ions (H₃O⁺) in aqueous solution, and all bases produce OH⁻(aq) ions. The presence of these ions makes acidic and basic solutions conductors of electricity. Water is essential for ionisation; dry HCl gas does not show acidic properties.
frequently_confused_with: pH Scale and Strength of Acids and Bases
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
summary: Acids ionise in water to release hydrogen ions (H⁺), which cannot exist alone and instead combine with water to form hydronium ions (H₃O⁺). These ions are responsible for the acidic properties of acid solutions and enable the conduction of electricity. Compounds like glucose and alcohol also contain hydrogen but do not form H⁺(aq) ions, so they are not acids.
diagram_description: Circuit diagram with two nails in a beaker of acid solution connected to a 6V battery and bulb, showing the bulb glowing when HCl is present but not when glucose solution is used.

builds_upon.concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
summary: Bases produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. Bases that are soluble in water are called alkalis; they are soapy to touch, bitter, and corrosive. Examples include NaOH, KOH, and Ca(OH)₂ which dissociate to give OH⁻(aq) ions.

builds_upon.concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
Properties and Indicators of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
summary: Water is essential for acids to exhibit acidic properties; dry HCl gas does not change dry litmus paper colour because H⁺ ions are only produced in the presence of water. When HCl dissolves in water, it ionises to give H₃O⁺ and Cl⁻. Moist litmus paper changes colour because water enables ionisation.
diagram_description: Apparatus for preparation of HCl gas from NaCl and concentrated H₂SO₄, with dry and moist blue litmus paper tested successively at the delivery tube outlet.
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Ionic Nature of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Solution
summary: Dissolving a concentrated acid or base in water is highly exothermic; concentrated acid must always be added slowly to water (never the reverse) to avoid dangerous splashing. Dilution decreases the concentration of H₃O⁺ or OH⁻ ions per unit volume. Adding water to a concentrated acid can cause violent spattering and burns.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: pH Scale and Strength of Acids and Bases
subtopic_names: Strong and Weak Acids and Bases
Importance of pH in Everyday Life
