chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

subtopic_names: Diagonal Relationships in the Periodic Table
summary: Chemical properties vary periodically with atomic number. Valence of representative elements typically equals the number of valence electrons or eight minus this number. Reactivity is highest at period extremes (alkali metals lose electrons; halogens gain electrons) and lowest in the center. Chemical behavior is fundamentally determined by electronic configuration.
diagram_description: Periodic table highlighting chemical behavior regions: reactive metals left, reactive non-metals right, least reactive center
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Valence (combining capacity) of representative elements equals the number of valence electrons (Group 1–2) or eight minus this number (Groups 13–17). Oxidation state is the charge acquired by an atom in a compound based on electronegativity differences. For example, in Na₂O, Na has +1 oxidation state; in OF₂, O has +2 (higher electronegativity element gets negative oxidation state).
diagram_description: Table showing formulas of hydrides and oxides for Groups 1–17, illustrating valence periodicity
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

concept_name: Diagonal Relationships in the Periodic Table
summary: First members of s- and p-block groups (Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F) show anomalous properties and resemble the second member of the next group diagonally: Li resembles Mg; Be resembles Al. This is due to small atomic size, large charge/radius ratio, and high electronegativity. First-period elements form stronger pπ–pπ multiple bonds (C≡C, N≡N) than heavier congeners.
diagram_description: Periodic table with diagonal arrows connecting Li to Mg, Be to Al, B to Si, etc., showing diagonal relationships
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Over 78% of elements are metals (left side of periodic table): solids at room temperature with high melting points, malleable, ductile, good conductors. Non-metals (top right): poor conductors, low melting points, brittle. Metalloids (Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te) lie along the diagonal zig-zag line and display both metallic and non-metallic properties.
diagram_description: Periodic table showing metal region (left/blue), non-metal region (top-right/green), and metalloid staircase line
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Chemical reactivity is highest at period extremes (alkali metals readily lose electrons; halogens gain electrons) and lowest in the center. Basic oxides form from extreme-left elements (Na₂O); acidic oxides from extreme-right elements (Cl₂O₇). Center elements form amphoteric oxides (Al₂O₃, As₂O₃) or neutral oxides (CO, NO). Reactivity within a group increases downward for metals; decreases for non-metals.
diagram_description: Periodic table showing reactivity gradient with arrows and oxide type regions labeled
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Effective nuclear charge (Zeff) is the net positive charge experienced by a valence electron, reduced by shielding from inner electrons. As we move across a period, Zeff increases (same shell, more protons), while moving down a group, Zeff remains relatively constant but is experienced at greater distance. Shielding is most effective for completely filled inner orbitals; penetration explains why 2s electrons experience stronger attraction than 2p electrons.
diagram_description: Schematic showing inner electrons shielding valence electron from nucleus; comparison of 2s vs. 2p orbital penetration
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Effective nuclear charge is the net positive charge felt by an electron in an atom, equal to actual nuclear charge (Z) minus shielding constant (S). Zeff increases across a period (more protons, same inner electrons) and remains relatively stable down a group (additional protons offset by additional inner shells). It explains periodic trends in ionization enthalpy and electronegativity.
diagram_description: Comparison showing Zeff values increasing left-to-right across period II and relatively constant down Group 1
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Shielding (screening) is the reduction in effective nuclear charge due to repulsion by inner electrons. s-electrons penetrate closer to the nucleus than p-electrons in the same shell, experiencing stronger attraction (e.g., 2s electrons are more tightly bound than 2p electrons). This explains why removing a 2p-electron from boron is easier than removing a 2s-electron from beryllium, despite beryllium having lower atomic number.
diagram_description: Orbital diagrams comparing 2s and 2p orbital density near nucleus; arrow showing relative penetration
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

subtopic_names: Occurrence of Reactive Elements in Nature
summary: Elements are classified by nature into metals, non-metals, and metalloids. Metals comprise >78% of known elements, occupy the left side of the periodic table, and are good conductors of heat and electricity. Non-metals occupy the top right, are poor conductors, and are more electronegative. Representative elements (s- and p-blocks) show clear periodic trends; transition elements (d- and f-blocks) display intermediate properties.
diagram_description: Periodic table with regions color-coded for metals, non-metals, and metalloids
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Metals are elements with low ionization enthalpies that readily lose electrons to form cations. They are typically solids at room temperature (mercury is an exception), with high melting and boiling points. Metals are malleable (can be hammered into sheets), ductile (drawn into wires), and excellent conductors of heat and electricity due to delocalized electrons in metallic bonding.
diagram_description: Representation of metallic structure showing electron sea model and atomic cores
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals

summary: Non-metals have high ionization enthalpies and electronegativities; they tend to gain or share electrons. Most are gases or solids at room temperature with low melting and boiling points (C and B are exceptions). Non-metals are poor conductors of heat and electricity; non-metallic solids are brittle and neither malleable nor ductile. Non-metallic character increases from left to right across a period.
diagram_description: Examples of non-metallic elements (O₂, N₂, P₄, S₈) showing gaseous and solid allotropes
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals

summary: Representative (main group) elements occupy s- and p-blocks with clear, predictable chemical properties tied to valence electrons. Transition elements (d-block) bridge chemically active s-block metals and less reactive p-block elements. Transition metals exhibit variable oxidation states, form colored ions, are paramagnetic, and often act as catalysts. Most man-made elements are transition metals and actinoids.
diagram_description: Periodic table highlighting representative elements (s + p blocks) in one color and transition elements (d + f blocks) in another
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

concept_name: Isoelectronic Species and Ionic Size Comparison
summary: Isoelectronic species are atoms or ions with the same number of electrons. Examples: O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, and Mg²⁺ all have 10 electrons. Among isoelectronic species, size decreases with increasing nuclear charge: the cation with greater positive charge (smaller radius) < anion with greater negative charge (larger radius). Relative radii: O²⁻ > F⁻ > Na⁺ > Mg²⁺.
diagram_description: Comparison diagram showing relative sizes of O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺ with nuclear charges indicated
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Two notable deviations occur in period II ionization enthalpies: (1) Be has higher ΔiH than B because 2s electrons penetrate closer to nucleus than 2p electrons, (2) N has higher ΔiH than O because N has three unpaired 2p electrons (half-filled 2p³ is stable) while O has electron-pairing repulsion (2p⁴). These exceptions reveal importance of orbital type and electron configuration stability beyond simple nuclear charge trends.
diagram_description: Electron configuration diagrams for Be (2s²), B (2s²2p¹), N (2s²2p³), O (2s²2p⁴) showing orbital filling and repulsion effects
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

concept_name: Occurrence of Reactive Elements in Nature
summary: Highly reactive elements (low ionization enthalpy) do not occur naturally in free/elemental state; they exist in combined form as compounds. For example, alkali metals (Group 1, very reactive) are found as salts; halogens (Group 17, reactive non-metals) as halides. Less reactive elements like noble gases occur as free elements. This principle reflects the tendency of reactive elements to lose or gain electrons and form stable ionic/covalent compounds.
diagram_description: Periodic table with reactivity zones highlighted and examples of natural occurrence (free vs. combined forms)
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Lanthanoids (Ce–Lu, Z=58–71) and actinoids (Th–Lr, Z=90–103) comprise the f-block inner-transition series. Lanthanoids show similar properties within the series due to f-orbital filling; actinoids display more variable oxidation states. Actinoids are all radioactive; elements after uranium (transuranium elements) occur only in laboratories. Both series are placed separately below the main table to preserve periodic organization.
diagram_description: Lanthanoid and actinoid series shown in separate rows with electron configurations and radioactivity indicators
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

summary: Kössel and Lewis developed the electronic theory of chemical bonding, explaining how atoms achieve stable noble gas configurations through electron transfer (ionic bonding) or electron pair sharing (covalent bonding). Lewis introduced symbols to represent valence electrons and postulated that atoms form bonds to attain a stable outer octet of electrons.
diagram_description: Progression from isolated atoms to bonded molecules showing electron transfer and electron pair sharing
subject: chemistry

summary: Lewis symbols represent the number of valence electrons in an atom using dots placed around the element symbol. The number of dots indicates the number of valence electrons, helping determine group valence (either equal to dots or 8 minus dots). These symbols simplify visualization of bonding in molecules.
diagram_description: Lewis symbols for second period elements showing dot placement for Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F, Ne
subject: chemistry

summary: The octet rule states that atoms combine by transferring or sharing valence electrons to achieve a stable outer shell of eight electrons (or two for hydrogen). This rule explains chemical bonding and was developed by Kössel and Lewis based on the inertness of noble gases. It applies mainly to second-period elements.
diagram_description: Comparison of electron configurations before and after bond formation showing octet achievement
subject: chemistry

summary: The octet rule has three major exceptions: incomplete octet (BF₃, BCl₃, BeH₂), odd-electron molecules (NO, NO₂), and expanded octet in third-period and beyond elements (PF₅, SF₆). These exceptions show the rule's limitations and indicate the need for more sophisticated bonding theories.
diagram_description: Examples of octet rule exceptions showing electron configurations of exceptional molecules
subject: chemistry

summary: Covalent bonding occurs when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons to achieve stable configurations. Single bonds involve one shared pair, double bonds two pairs, and triple bonds three pairs. This sharing results in Lewis structures showing bonded and non-bonded electron pairs around each atom.
diagram_description: Lewis dot structures showing single, double, and triple bonds with shared electron pairs
subject: chemistry

summary: Electrovalent (ionic) bonds form through electrostatic attraction between cations and anions formed by complete electron transfer from one atom to another. The electrovalence equals the number of unit charges on the ion. Noble gas configurations are achieved by the resulting ions, as seen in NaCl and CaF₂.
diagram_description: Electron transfer diagrams showing formation of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions and their electrostatic attraction
subject: chemistry

summary: Ionic bonding involves electrostatic attraction between cations and anions arranged in a three-dimensional crystal lattice. The stability of ionic compounds depends on lattice enthalpy—the energy required to completely separate one mole of solid ionic compound into gaseous ions. Ionization and electron gain enthalpies determine the ease of ion formation.
diagram_description: Rock salt crystal structure showing 3D arrangement of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions
subject: chemistry

summary: Lattice enthalpy is the energy required to completely separate one mole of a solid ionic compound into gaseous constituent ions at infinite distance. For example, NaCl has a lattice enthalpy of 788 kJ mol⁻¹. It represents the strength of electrostatic forces in the crystal and is crucial for ionic compound stability.
diagram_description: Energy diagram showing separation of ionic solid into gaseous ions
subject: chemistry

summary: Ionic bonds form more readily between elements with low ionization enthalpy (metals) and high negative electron gain enthalpy (nonmetals). Small cation size and large anion size increase ionic character, as do high cation charges. These factors maximize electrostatic attraction and overall compound stability.
diagram_description: Trends in ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy across periodic table

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

subject: chemistry

summary: Ionic compounds crystallize in three-dimensional ordered arrangements called crystal lattices determined by ion sizes, charges, and packing efficiency. The rock salt (NaCl) structure is a common example with each Na⁺ surrounded by six Cl⁻ ions and vice versa. Crystal geometry directly affects lattice enthalpy and compound properties.
diagram_description: 3D representation of rock salt crystal structure with cation and anion positions
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond parameters describe the properties of covalent bonds including length, angle, energy, order, and polarity. These measurable quantities characterize how atoms are bonded, determine molecular geometry, and influence chemical reactivity and physical properties of compounds.
diagram_description: Molecular structure showing bond length, bond angle, and dipole moment vector
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond length is the equilibrium distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms, determined by spectroscopic and X-ray diffraction techniques. It equals the sum of covalent radii of the atoms. Shorter bonds indicate stronger bonds; bond length decreases with increasing bond order.
diagram_description: Diagram showing covalent and van der Waals radii in a diatomic molecule
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond angle is the angle between orbitals containing bonding electron pairs around the central atom in a molecule. It is measured in degrees using spectroscopic methods and indicates the spatial distribution and geometry of bonds. Bond angles vary with molecular geometry (e.g., 104.5° in H₂O, 107° in NH₃).
diagram_description: Water molecule showing H-O-H bond angle of 104.5°
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond enthalpy is the energy required to break one mole of bonds of a particular type in gaseous state, measured in kJ mol⁻¹. For heteronuclear molecules and polyatomic species, mean bond enthalpy is used. Stronger bonds have higher bond enthalpies, and bond strength increases with bond order.
diagram_description: Energy diagram showing bond dissociation for H₂, O₂, and N₂ molecules
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond order in Lewis structures equals the number of shared electron pairs between two atoms (1, 2, or 3 for single, double, triple bonds). Higher bond order correlates with shorter bond length and greater bond strength. Isoelectronic molecules/ions have identical bond orders.
diagram_description: Comparison of single, double, and triple bonds showing electron pair arrangement
subject: chemistry

summary: Dipole moment is the product of charge magnitude and separation distance, expressing bond polarity and molecular asymmetry. Nonpolar bonds have zero dipole moment (equal atoms), while polar bonds have asymmetric electron distribution toward more electronegative atoms. Dipole moments are measured in Debye units (D).
diagram_description: Vector diagram showing individual bond dipoles and resultant net dipole moment for water and carbon dioxide

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

subject: chemistry

summary: Resonance occurs when a single Lewis structure cannot accurately describe a molecule, so multiple structures with similar energy and same atomic positions are drawn. The actual molecule is represented as a resonance hybrid averaging these canonical forms. Examples include O₃, CO₃²⁻, and CO₂.
diagram_description: Resonance structures of ozone showing two canonical forms and resonance hybrid with intermediate bonds
subject: chemistry

summary: Canonical forms are individual Lewis structures with the same skeletal arrangement but different electron placement. The resonance hybrid represents the actual molecule, averaging the electron distribution across all canonical forms. No interconversion between forms occurs; the molecule exists as a single hybrid.
diagram_description: Carbonate ion showing three canonical forms and resulting resonance hybrid with equivalent C-O bonds
subject: chemistry

summary: Resonance stabilizes molecules because the energy of the resonance hybrid is lower than any individual canonical form. This stabilization results from averaging bond characteristics across all contributing structures. Resonance also explains intermediate bond properties between single and double bonds.
diagram_description: Energy diagram comparing canonical form energies with resonance hybrid energy
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Geometry of Molecules Without Lone Pairs
Geometry of Molecules With Lone Pairs
aliases: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory
summary: VSEPR theory predicts molecular shapes by minimizing repulsion between valence shell electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs). Electron pairs position themselves as far apart as possible on a spherical surface around the central atom. Multiple bonds are treated as single super-pairs.
diagram_description: Comparison of linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, and octahedral geometries
subject: chemistry

summary: VSEPR is based on key postulates: molecular shape depends on valence shell electron pairs; electrons repel each other; pairs occupy maximum-distance positions on a spherical surface; valence shell is spherical; multiple bonds act as single pairs. These assumptions accurately predict geometries of most molecules.
diagram_description: Spherical representation showing electron pair distribution around central atom
subject: chemistry

summary: Electron pair repulsions decrease in order: lp-lp > lp-bp > bp-bp. Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, causing stronger repulsion. This explains bond angle deviations from ideal geometries (e.g., H₂O: 104.5° instead of 109.5°, NH₃: 107° instead of 109.5°).
diagram_description: Comparative diagram showing spatial extent of lone pairs versus bonding pairs
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Geometry of Molecules Without Lone Pairs
summary: Molecules with only bonding pairs adopt regular geometries: AB₂ (linear, 180°), AB₃ (trigonal planar, 120°), AB₄ (tetrahedral, 109.5°), AB₅ (trigonal bipyramidal), and AB₆ (octahedral, 90°). Examples include BeCl₂, BF₃, CH₄, PCl₅, and SF₆.
diagram_description: Ball-and-stick models of BeCl₂, BF₃, CH₄, PCl₅ showing linear, planar, tetrahedral, and bipyramidal geometries
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Geometry of Molecules With Lone Pairs
summary: Lone pairs distort ideal geometries: AB₂E (bent), AB₃E (pyramidal), AB₂E₂ (bent), AB₄E (see-saw), AB₃E₂ (T-shaped), AB₅E (square pyramidal), AB₄E₂ (square planar). Bond angles are compressed from ideal values due to increased lp-bp and lp-lp repulsions compared to bp-bp repulsion.
diagram_description: Comparison of bent water, pyramidal ammonia, and see-saw sulfur tetrafluoride geometries
subject: chemistry

summary: Valence Bond Theory explains covalent bonding through orbital overlap and quantifies bond formation energetics. It describes H₂ formation as overlap of 1s orbitals with minimum potential energy at equilibrium bond distance. VB theory provides deeper insight than Lewis theory into bond strength, directional properties, and molecular geometry.
diagram_description: Potential energy curve for H₂ molecule formation showing energy minimum at equilibrium distance
subject: chemistry

summary: Covalent bonds form when atomic orbitals of two atoms partially overlap, allowing electron pairing with opposite spins. Greater overlap produces stronger bonds with lower potential energy. Overlap minimizes energy when atoms reach equilibrium bond distance; closer approach increases repulsion.
diagram_description: Diagram showing 1s orbital overlap in hydrogen molecule at various internuclear distances
subject: chemistry

summary: Covalent bonds have directional properties determined by atomic orbital orientation. P orbitals are oriented at 90° to each other, but molecular geometries like CH₄ (109.5°) and NH₃ (107°) cannot be explained by simple orbital overlap alone. This discrepancy motivates the hybridization concept.
diagram_description: Comparison of expected 90° p-orbital geometry with observed 109.5° tetrahedral geometry in methane
subject: chemistry

summary: Orbital overlap depends on orbital phase and orientation. Positive overlap (same-phase orbitals aligned along bonding axis) produces bonding MOs. Negative overlap (opposite phases) produces antibonding MOs. Zero overlap (perpendicular orientations) produces no bond, demonstrated with s-p and p-p orbital combinations.
diagram_description: Diagrams showing positive, negative, and zero overlap for s-s, s-p, and p-p orbital combinations
subject: chemistry

summary: Sigma (σ) bonds form by head-on overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis (s-s, s-p, p-p overlaps). Pi (π) bonds form by sidewise overlap with parallel orbital axes. Multiple bonds consist of one σ bond plus one or more π bonds. σ bonds are stronger than π bonds.
diagram_description: Diagrams showing σ bond formation by end-to-end overlap and π bond by sidewise overlap
subject: chemistry

summary: Hybridisation is the mixing of atomic orbitals of slightly different energies to form equivalent hybrid orbitals used in bonding. The number of hybrid orbitals equals combining atomic orbitals. Hybridisation explains molecular shapes: sp (linear), sp² (planar), sp³ (tetrahedral), sp³d (bipyramidal), sp³d² (octahedral).
diagram_description: Progressive mixing of s and p orbitals to form sp, sp2, and sp3 hybrids with characteristic geometries
subject: chemistry

summary: sp hybridisation combines one s and one p orbital to form two equivalent linear hybrid orbitals at 180°. Each has 50% s and 50% p character. Examples include BeCl₂ (linear) where beryllium uses sp hybrids. The linear orientation provides strong bonding with projecting lobes.
diagram_description: Formation of sp hybrid orbitals from s and p orbitals showing linear arrangement at 180°
subject: chemistry

summary: sp² hybridisation combines one s and two p orbitals to form three equivalent trigonal planar hybrids at 120°. Each has 33% s and 67% p character. BCl₃ and C₂H₄ exemplify this type. One unhybridised p orbital remains for π bonding in molecules like ethene.
diagram_description: Formation of sp2 hybrid orbitals showing trigonal planar arrangement with 120° angles
subject: chemistry

summary: sp³ hybridisation combines one s and three p orbitals to form four equivalent tetrahedral hybrids at 109.5°. Each has 25% s and 75% p character. CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O exemplify this type. All four hybrid orbitals are involved in bonding (CH₄) or bonding plus lone pairs (NH₃, H₂O).
diagram_description: Formation of sp3 hybrid orbitals showing tetrahedral arrangement with 109.5° angles
subject: chemistry

summary: sp³d hybridisation uses one s, three p, and one d orbital to form five hybrid orbitals with trigonal bipyramidal geometry. sp³d² uses one s, three p, and two d orbitals to form six octahedral hybrids. These accommodate expanded octets in compounds like PCl₅ and SF₆ found in third period and beyond.
diagram_description: Comparison of sp3d trigonal bipyramidal and sp3d2 octahedral geometries
subject: chemistry

summary: Molecular Orbital Theory describes molecules using molecular orbitals (MOs) formed by combining atomic orbitals of comparable energy. Electrons occupy MOs following aufbau principle and Pauli exclusion. MOs are classified as bonding (lower energy) or antibonding (higher energy) based on electron density between nuclei.
diagram_description: Energy level diagram showing atomic orbitals combining to form bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals
subject: chemistry

summary: Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO) approximates molecular orbitals as sums and differences of atomic orbital wave functions. For H₂, σ = ψₐ + ψᵦ (bonding) and σ* = ψₐ - ψᵦ (antibonding). This method overcomes the inability to solve Schrödinger equation for multi-electron systems.
diagram_description: Constructive and destructive interference of atomic orbitals forming bonding and antibonding MOs
subject: chemistry

summary: Bonding MOs have electron density concentrated between nuclei, are lower in energy, and stabilize molecules. Antibonding MOs have a nodal plane (zero density) between nuclei, are higher in energy, and destabilize molecules. σ/π notation refers to symmetry; */no asterisk distinguishes bonding from antibonding.
diagram_description: Orbital contours showing electron density between nuclei in bonding MO and nodal plane in antibonding MO
subject: chemistry

summary: Three conditions must be met for successful atomic orbital combination: orbitals have similar or nearly equal energy; orbitals have same symmetry about molecular axis; orbitals overlap significantly. For example, 2pz orbitals combine but 2pz with 2px do not combine due to different symmetries.
diagram_description: Comparison of successful and unsuccessful orbital combinations showing symmetry requirements
subject: chemistry

summary: MO energy diagrams show atomic orbitals on sides, resulting molecular orbitals in center, arranged by energy. Different orderings apply to different molecules: for O₂/F₂, σ2p is above π2p; for B₂/C₂/N₂, π2p is above σ2p. Electrons fill by aufbau principle giving electronic configurations and bond orders.
diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison of MO diagrams for N₂ and O₂ showing different orbital orderings
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond order = ½(Nᵦ - Nₐ) where Nᵦ and Nₐ are bonding and antibonding electrons. Positive bond orders indicate stability; molecules with more antibonding than bonding electrons are unstable. MO theory successfully predicts O₂ paramagnetism and explains why He₂ and Be₂ don't exist.
diagram_description: Bond order calculations for H₂, O₂, and N₂ with electron configurations
subject: chemistry

summary: Molecular orbitals are classified by symmetry: σ (sigma) orbitals are symmetric about the molecular axis (1s, 2pz overlaps); π (pi) orbitals are asymmetric with lobes above/below the axis (2px, 2py overlaps); δ (delta) orbitals are rarer. Each type has bonding and antibonding versions.
diagram_description: Contour diagrams showing σ, π, and their antibonding counterparts formed from different atomic orbital combinations
subject: chemistry

summary: Homonuclear diatomic molecules consist of two identical atoms. Their properties (bond length, strength, stability, magnetism) are determined by molecular orbital occupancy and bond order. Examples include H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, and halogens, each with characteristic electronic configurations and properties.
diagram_description: Comparison of MO energy diagrams and molecular properties for H₂, N₂, O₂, and F₂
subject: chemistry

summary: H₂ has electronic configuration (σ1s)² with bond order = 1 (single bond). Bond dissociation energy is 435.8 kJ mol⁻¹ and bond length is 74 pm. The molecule is diamagnetic (no unpaired electrons) and highly stable, forming the basis for understanding molecular bonding.
diagram_description: MO diagram for H₂ showing 1s atomic orbitals forming σ1s bonding and σ*1s antibonding MOs
subject: chemistry

summary: O₂ has two unpaired electrons in π*2p orbitals making it paramagnetic (attracted by magnetic fields). Electronic configuration gives bond order = 2 (double bond). This explains O₂'s unexpected paramagnetism, a major success of MO theory over Lewis theory. Bond energy is 498 kJ mol⁻¹.
diagram_description: MO diagram for O₂ showing unpaired electrons in π*2p orbitals (without 2s-2p mixing)
subject: chemistry

summary: N₂ has bond order = 3 (triple bond consisting of one σ and two π bonds). Bond dissociation energy of 946.0 kJ mol⁻¹ is the highest among diatomic molecules. The molecule is diamagnetic (no unpaired electrons) and extremely stable, explaining nitrogen's low reactivity in atmosphere.
diagram_description: MO diagram for N₂ showing filled bonding orbitals and empty antibonding orbitals, with 2s-2p mixing
subject: chemistry

summary: F₂ has bond order = 1 (single bond) and is diamagnetic. Cl₂ and other halogens similarly have single bonds. B₂ and C₂ have double bonds with π electrons and are diamagnetic. Li₂ and Be₂ are unstable or nonexistent (bond order ≤ 0).
diagram_description: Comparison of bond properties for F₂, Cl₂, B₂, C₂, and Li₂
subject: chemistry

summary: Hydrogen bonding is an attractive force between a partially positive hydrogen atom bonded to highly electronegative elements (F, O, N) and a lone pair on another electronegative atom. Weaker than covalent bonds but stronger than van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds significantly affect molecular properties and structures.
diagram_description: Hydrogen bonding in water and HF molecules showing dotted H-bond representation
subject: chemistry

summary: When hydrogen bonds to highly electronegative atom X (F, O, N), electrons shift toward X, creating δ+ on H and δ- on X. The partially positive hydrogen is attracted to lone pairs on another X atom of the same or different molecule. This electrostatic attraction constitutes the hydrogen bond.
diagram_description: Electron displacement in H-X bond showing δ+ and δ- charges leading to hydrogen bonding
subject: chemistry

summary: Intermolecular hydrogen bonds form between H atoms and electronegative atoms of different molecules (e.g., HF, H₂O, alcohols). Intramolecular hydrogen bonds form within a single molecule when H is positioned between two electronegative atoms like in o-nitrophenol. Both types significantly affect molecular properties.
diagram_description: Comparison of intermolecular H-bonding in water and intramolecular H-bonding in o-nitrophenol
subject: chemistry

summary: Hydrogen bonding profoundly affects physical properties: causes abnormally high boiling/melting points in H₂O and alcohols; increases solubility in polar solvents; stabilizes protein and DNA structures. Magnitude varies with physical state (maximum in solids, minimum in gases), explaining state-dependent properties of hydrogen-bonding compounds.
diagram_description: Comparison of boiling points showing anomaly for water and hydrogen-bonding compounds versus related nonpolar molecules
subject: chemistry

summary: Fajans rules explain partial covalent character in ionic bonds: smaller cations and larger anions increase covalency; higher cation charge increases covalency; transition metal cations (nd configuration) are more polarizing than alkali metals. These rules bridge purely ionic and purely covalent bonding concepts.
diagram_description: Periodic trends showing how cation size and anion size affect covalent character of ionic bonds
subject: chemistry

summary: Formal charge quantifies electron distribution in Lewis structures: F.C. = valence electrons - lone pair electrons - ½(bonding electrons). The sum of formal charges equals overall charge. This helps select most probable Lewis structures: lowest formal charges and charges on most electronegative atoms are favored.
diagram_description: Ozone resonance structures with formal charges calculated for each atom
subject: chemistry

summary: Lewis dot structures show all valence electrons in molecules/ions as dots or lines, representing bonding pairs (lines) and lone pairs (dots). Construction follows steps: count valence electrons, arrange skeletal structure (least electronegative central), distribute electrons ensuring octet satisfaction. These structures are essential for understanding molecular properties.
diagram_description: Step-by-step Lewis structure construction for water, carbon dioxide, and carbonate ion
subject: chemistry

summary: A thermodynamic system is the part of the universe under observation, separated from surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary. The universe equals system plus surroundings. The boundary controls exchange of matter and energy between system and surroundings.
diagram_description: Diagram showing system enclosed by boundary within surroundings; arrows indicating matter and energy exchange
subject: chemistry

summary: Open systems exchange both matter and energy with surroundings. Closed systems exchange energy but not matter. Isolated systems exchange neither matter nor energy (adiabatic walls). Classification depends on boundary permeability.
diagram_description: Three diagrams side-by-side: open system with bidirectional arrows for matter and energy; closed system with energy arrows only; isolated system with no arrows
subject: chemistry

summary: System state is defined by measurable macroscopic properties: pressure (p), volume (V), temperature (T), and composition. State variables depend only on current state, not path taken. Once minimum macroscopic properties are fixed, others are determined.
diagram_description: PV diagram showing initial and final states; path-independent property illustration
subject: chemistry

summary: Boundaries separate system from surroundings and may be real (beaker walls) or imaginary. Adiabatic walls prevent heat transfer (insulated). Thermally conducting walls allow heat exchange. Boundary design controls energy and matter movement.
diagram_description: Cross-section of adiabatic system and conducting system showing wall structure and thermal properties
subject: chemistry

summary: Internal energy (U) is the total energy of a system (chemical, electrical, mechanical). It changes when heat passes into/out or work is done on/by the system. U is a state function dependent only on initial and final states, not path. The first law: ΔU = q + w.
diagram_description: Energy diagram showing internal energy levels; arrows representing heat and work contributions
subject: chemistry

summary: Joule's experiments (1840–50) showed that identical work on a system (mechanical or electrical) produces the same internal energy change, regardless of path. This proves U is a state function. For adiabatic work: ΔU = w_ad.
diagram_description: Joule's paddle experiment apparatus showing mechanical and electrical work producing same temperature rise
subject: chemistry

summary: Mechanical work during gas expansion/compression: w = -p_ex·ΔV. Negative sign indicates positive w for compression. At constant pressure: w = -p·ΔV. For reversible isothermal processes: w_rev = -nRT ln(V_f/V_i).
diagram_description: PV diagram showing work as area under curve; separate curves for constant pressure, irreversible, and reversible processes
subject: chemistry

summary: Heat (q) is energy transfer resulting from temperature difference. At constant volume: ΔU = q_V. Heat capacity C relates heat to temperature change: q = CΔT. Specific heat is heat per unit mass; molar heat capacity is per mole. q is positive when absorbed by system.
diagram_description: Temperature vs heat supplied graph showing linear relationship through heat capacity slope
subject: chemistry

summary: The first law states: ΔU = q + w. For isolated systems (q=0, w=0): ΔU=0. Energy is conserved—cannot be created or destroyed. The law applies to systems moving between equilibrium states.
diagram_description: Energy flow diagram showing heat (q) and work (w) inputs/outputs and internal energy change
subject: chemistry

summary: Enthalpy H = U + pV is a state function. At constant pressure: ΔH = q_p. Exothermic reactions (ΔH < 0) release heat; endothermic (ΔH > 0) absorb heat. Standard enthalpy (ΔH°) refers to pure substances at 1 bar and specified temperature (usually 298 K).
diagram_description: Enthalpy diagram showing reactants and products with ΔH as energy difference; separate diagrams for exothermic and endothermic
subject: chemistry

summary: Enthalpy H = U + pV is a useful state function for reactions at constant pressure. ΔH = ΔU + Δ(pV). For constant pressure: ΔH = ΔU + pΔV. Enthalpy equals heat absorbed at constant pressure (q_p).
diagram_description: Derivation diagram showing transition from ΔU equation to enthalpy definition
subject: chemistry

summary: Reaction enthalpy ΔrH = Σ(a_i·ΔfH products) - Σ(b_i·ΔfH reactants), where a_i and b_i are stoichiometric coefficients. Exothermic reactions have ΔrH < 0; endothermic have ΔrH > 0. For gaseous systems: ΔH = ΔU + Δn_g·RT.
diagram_description: Table showing exothermic and endothermic reactions with enthalpy values and diagrams
subject: chemistry

summary: Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔfH°) is the enthalpy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in most stable states at 1 bar and 298 K. By convention, ΔfH° of elements in reference states = 0. Example: H₂(g) + ½O₂(g) → H₂O(l); ΔfH° = –285.8 kJ mol⁻¹.
diagram_description: Table of standard molar enthalpies of formation for common substances at 298 K
subject: chemistry

summary: Combustion enthalpy (ΔcH) is heat released per mole of substance burning completely in O₂. All combustion is exothermic (ΔcH < 0). Atomization enthalpy (ΔaH) is heat required to break all bonds and form gaseous atoms. For diatomic molecules: ΔaH = bond dissociation enthalpy.
diagram_description: Combustion reaction equations showing products CO₂ and H₂O; atomization showing molecular breakdown to atoms
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation
summary: Hess's law: Enthalpy change for a reaction is the same whether it occurs in one step or multiple steps. Since H is a state function, ΔH_total = ΔH₁ + ΔH₂ + ... This allows calculating ΔH for reactions that cannot be measured directly.
diagram_description: Energy diagram showing multiple reaction pathways to same final state; Born-Haber cycle example
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation
summary: Hess's law states: if a reaction occurs in multiple steps, total ΔrH equals sum of individual step enthalpies. Allows calculation of ΔrH for reactions where direct measurement is impossible. Derived from H being a state function.
diagram_description: Reaction diagram showing CO formation via two different pathways; enthalpy values summing to same result
subject: chemistry

summary: Bond dissociation enthalpy is energy to break one mole of bonds (endothermic, positive). Mean bond enthalpy is average for bonds in polyatomic molecules. ΔrH = Σ(bond enthalpies of reactants) - Σ(bond enthalpies of products). Valid for gas-phase reactions only.
diagram_description: Table of mean single and multiple bond enthalpies; reaction equation showing energy balance
subject: chemistry

summary: Calorimetry measures energy changes by monitoring temperature shifts in a known-mass liquid. Bomb calorimeter measures ΔU at constant volume (combustion). Constant pressure calorimeter measures ΔH = q_p. Heat absorbed: q = C·ΔT, where C is heat capacity.
diagram_description: Cross-section diagrams of bomb calorimeter and open calorimeter showing thermometer, sample, water bath
subject: chemistry

summary: Sealed steel vessel burns combustible substance in pure O₂. Volume constant (ΔV=0), so ΔU = q_V. Vessel immersed in water bath; heat released raises water temperature. ΔU calculated from temperature change and calorimeter heat capacity.
diagram_description: Detailed cross-section of bomb calorimeter with sample chamber, oxygen inlet, thermometer, and water bath
subject: chemistry

summary: Open calorimeter cup at atmospheric pressure. ΔH = q_p measured directly from temperature rise of water or solution. Heat of reaction equals heat absorbed by calorimeter solution: q_p = C_p·ΔT.
diagram_description: Simple foam cup calorimeter showing thermometer and reaction mixture in water; temperature measurement
subject: chemistry

summary: Extensive properties (mass, volume, internal energy, enthalpy) depend on quantity of matter. Intensive properties (temperature, density, pressure) are independent of amount. Molar properties (e.g., C_m, V_m) are extensive divided by moles, yielding intensive values.
diagram_description: Illustration of gas sample partitioned into half volumes; extensive property halves, intensive property unchanged
subject: chemistry

summary: Entropy (S) measures disorder/randomness in a system. Spontaneous processes increase total entropy (ΔS_total > 0). Entropy is a state function: ΔS = q_rev/T. Second law: entropy of isolated systems increases. Driving force for spontaneity combines enthalpy and entropy effects.
diagram_description: Gas diffusion diagram showing ordered initial state becoming disordered mixed state; entropy increase visual
subject: chemistry

summary: Entropy (S) is a state function dependent only on initial and final states. For reversible processes: ΔS = q_rev/T. Greater disorder (liquid > solid, gas > liquid) means higher entropy. Entropy change is path-independent but calculation requires reversible process.
diagram_description: PV diagram showing reversible and irreversible paths; entropy calculation for reversible path only
subject: chemistry

summary: Spontaneous process proceeds without external agency and is irreversible (cannot reverse on its own). Spontaneity depends on both ΔH and ΔS: not solely on energy decrease. Exothermic reactions are favored, but endothermic ones can occur if entropy increase is large.
diagram_description: Examples of spontaneous processes (gas diffusion, combustion) vs non-spontaneous (heat flow uphill, decomposition)
subject: chemistry

summary: Gibbs energy: G = H - TS. ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. For spontaneity at constant T,p: ΔG < 0 (spontaneous), ΔG = 0 (equilibrium), ΔG > 0 (non-spontaneous). ΔG combines both energy and entropy effects, unifying spontaneity criterion for all system types.
diagram_description: Graph showing ΔG vs temperature for reactions with various ΔH and ΔS sign combinations
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Standard Gibbs Energy and Equilibrium Constant
summary: At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and free energy is minimum. Standard Gibbs energy (ΔG°) relates to equilibrium constant: ΔG° = –RT ln K or ΔG° = –2.303 RT log K. Large K (ΔG° << 0) favors products; small K (ΔG° >> 0) favors reactants.
diagram_description: Free energy curve showing minimum at equilibrium; relationship between ΔG° and K values
subject: chemistry

summary: At equilibrium, free energy is at minimum and ΔG = 0. This is the criterion for equilibrium: system will spontaneously shift toward lower G until ΔG = 0. Before equilibrium: ΔG < 0 (forward); after: ΔG < 0 (reverse) if disturbed.
diagram_description: Free energy vs reaction progress curve showing minimum at equilibrium point
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Standard Gibbs Energy and Equilibrium Constant
summary: ΔG° = –RT ln K = –2.303 RT log K relates standard free energy to equilibrium constant K. When ΔG° < 0: K > 1 (products favored). When ΔG° > 0: K < 1 (reactants favored). Allows prediction of reaction extent and K from ΔH° and ΔS°.
diagram_description: Table showing relationships between ΔG° signs and K values with interpretation
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Second and Third Laws of Thermodynamics
summary: ΔG = ΔH – TΔS shows temperature dependence. Reactions with ΔH<0, ΔS>0 always spontaneous. ΔH>0, ΔS>0 spontaneous only at high T. ΔH<0, ΔS<0 spontaneous only at low T. ΔH>0, ΔS<0 never spontaneous. Table 5.4 summarizes all cases.
diagram_description: ΔG vs temperature plot showing effects of different ΔH and ΔS sign combinations
subject: chemistry

summary: ΔG = ΔH – TΔS shows linear dependence on T with slope = –ΔS. As T increases, TΔS term becomes dominant. Reactions non-spontaneous at low T can become spontaneous at high T if ΔS > 0. Critical temperature: T = ΔH/ΔS where ΔG changes sign.
diagram_description: Linear ΔG vs T graph showing T-intercept and slope = –ΔS; identification of spontaneity regions
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Second and Third Laws of Thermodynamics
summary: Second law: entropy of isolated systems increases (ΔS_total > 0 for spontaneous processes). Third law: entropy of pure crystalline solid approaches zero at absolute zero (0 K). Permits calculation of absolute entropy from thermal data. Applies to pure crystalline solids only.
diagram_description: Entropy vs temperature graph for crystalline solid showing approach to zero at 0 K
subject: chemistry

summary: Lattice enthalpy is energy to convert 1 mole ionic solid to gaseous ions. Cannot be measured directly; calculated via Born-Haber cycle using ΔH_solution = ΔH_lattice + ΔH_hydration. Large lattice enthalpies reduce solubility. Related to ionic charge density.
diagram_description: Born-Haber cycle diagram showing atomization, ionization, dissociation, electron gain, and formation steps
subject: chemistry

summary: Born-Haber cycle calculates lattice enthalpy by summing ΔH for: (1) metal sublimation, (2) ionization, (3) nonmetal dissociation, (4) electron gain, (5) crystal formation. Uses Hess's law: Σ ΔH = 0. Result: ΔH_lattice = sum of individual steps.
diagram_description: Complete Born-Haber cycle for NaCl showing all five energy steps with enthalpy values
subject: chemistry

summary: Solution enthalpy ΔsolH is heat change dissolving 1 mole substance in specified solvent. ΔsolH = ΔH_lattice + ΔH_hydration. Most ionic compounds show ΔsolH > 0 (endothermic). Highly charged ions release large hydration enthalpy (very negative), promoting dissolution.
diagram_description: Diagram showing ionic solid dissociating into hydrated ions; enthalpy relationships shown schematically
subject: chemistry

summary: Thermochemical equation: balanced chemical equation + ΔrH value. Coefficients represent moles (not molecules). ΔrH refers to number of moles specified. Physical states must be included. When equation reversed, ΔrH changes sign.
diagram_description: Examples of thermochemical equations showing different physical states and ΔH values; effect of reversing equation
subject: chemistry

summary: For ideal gas: Cp – Cv = R. Cp > Cv because work done at constant p increases total energy needed. ΔH = ΔU + Δ(pV) = ΔU + RΔT (for ideal gas). This relates molar heat capacities: C_p - C_V = R.
diagram_description: Graph comparing temperature rise under constant pressure vs constant volume for same heat input
subject: chemistry

summary: Phase transitions occur at constant T and p. Fusion (solid→liquid): ΔfusH. Vaporization (liquid→gas): ΔvapH. Sublimation (solid→gas): ΔsubH = ΔfusH + ΔvapH. All endothermic (positive). Magnitude depends on intermolecular force strength.
diagram_description: Energy diagram showing enthalpy changes for melting, vaporization, and sublimation; temperature constant during transition
subject: chemistry

summary: Melting/fusion: ΔfusH positive, solid becomes liquid at T_f. Boiling/vaporization: ΔvapH positive, liquid becomes gas at T_b. Sublimation: ΔsubH positive, solid directly becomes gas. Standard values (at 1 bar) given in tables.
diagram_description: Table of standard enthalpy changes for fusion and vaporization; molecular-level diagram showing increased disorder
subject: chemistry

summary: Physical equilibrium occurs when opposing phase transformations occur at equal rates in a closed system at fixed temperature. Examples include solid-liquid, liquid-vapour, and gas-liquid equilibria where measurable properties remain constant at equilibrium.
diagram_description: Phase diagram showing equilibrium between solid-liquid-vapour phases at different temperatures and pressures
subject: chemistry

summary: At the normal melting point, solid and liquid phases coexist in equilibrium with equal rates of melting and freezing at constant temperature and atmospheric pressure. Ice and water at 273 K exemplify this dynamic equilibrium where molecular activity continues at the phase boundary.
diagram_description: Insulated flask showing ice-water system with arrows indicating equal rates of melting and freezing
subject: chemistry

summary: Liquid-vapour equilibrium is attained in a closed container when the rate of evaporation equals the rate of condensation, resulting in constant vapour pressure. The normal boiling point is the temperature at 1.013 bar where liquid and vapour are in equilibrium.
diagram_description: Sealed container with manometer showing water surface, vapour space, and mercury level measuring equilibrium vapour pressure
subject: chemistry

summary: Solid-vapour equilibrium occurs when a solid sublimes to vapour and simultaneously the vapour condenses back to solid at equal rates. Classic examples include iodine, camphor, and ammonium chloride reaching violet/colourless colour equilibrium in sealed containers.
diagram_description: Sealed flask containing solid iodine with violet vapour showing colour intensity becoming constant at equilibrium
subject: chemistry

summary: Dissolution equilibrium exists between solid solute and dissolved solute in a saturated solution, or between dissolved gas and gaseous phase. Henry's law states that gas solubility is proportional to partial pressure; soda water demonstrates this by fizzing when pressure decreases.
diagram_description: Saturated sugar solution showing equilibrium between solid and dissolved state with dynamic exchange of molecules
subject: chemistry

summary: Dynamic equilibrium means opposing processes occur simultaneously at equal rates, though macroscopic properties appear static. Radioactive isotope experiments prove continuous molecular exchange occurs: radioactive sugar in saturated solution becomes distributed in both solid and liquid phases.
diagram_description: Timeline showing radioactive sugar molecules gradually distributing between solid and saturated solution phases
subject: chemistry

summary: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, causing constant concentrations of reactants and products. The equilibrium constant Kc relates the concentrations of products to reactants, each raised to stoichiometric coefficients.
diagram_description: Concentration vs time graph showing reactants decreasing and products increasing until both plateau at equilibrium
subject: chemistry

summary: Reversible reactions proceed in both directions and can reach equilibrium from either direction. The double arrow notation (⇌) indicates both reactants and products form in a closed system until rates equalize.
diagram_description: Chemical equation showing double arrow with arrows of varying sizes indicating net direction and relative rates
subject: chemistry

summary: The equilibrium constant expression relates product concentrations (numerator) to reactant concentrations (denominator), each raised to stoichiometric coefficients. This mathematical relationship, Kc = [products]/[reactants], applies at a specific temperature.
diagram_description: Balanced equation with stoichiometric coefficients highlighted, showing their correspondence to exponents in Kc expression
subject: chemistry

summary: Kc is the equilibrium constant expressed using molar concentrations (mol/L). It has a constant value for a given reaction at a specific temperature and is independent of initial concentrations. For H2(g) + I2(g) ⇌ 2HI(g), Kc = [HI]²/([H2][I2]).
diagram_description: Table showing different initial conditions and equilibrium mixtures all yielding the same Kc value
subject: chemistry

summary: Kp is the equilibrium constant expressed using partial pressures of gases in bar. The relationship Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn connects Kp and Kc, where Δn = moles of gaseous products minus moles of gaseous reactants. Kp = Kc only when Δn = 0.
diagram_description: Comparison table showing Kc and Kp expressions for gaseous reactions with different Δn values
subject: chemistry

summary: The extent of a reaction is classified by comparing Kc values: K > 10³ (products dominate), 10⁻³ < K < 10³ (both present), K < 10⁻³ (reactants dominate). The reaction quotient Qc predicts reaction direction: if Qc < Kc, reaction proceeds forward; if Qc > Kc, it proceeds backward.
diagram_description: Diagram showing Kc ranges with examples and corresponding equilibrium compositions, plus Qc comparison logic
subject: chemistry

summary: Reactions are classified into three categories based on extent: (1) nearly complete with negligible reactants remaining; (2) small product formation with mostly unchanged reactants; (3) comparable concentrations of reactants and products at equilibrium. Classification depends on Kc magnitude.
diagram_description: Three pie charts showing relative proportions of reactants vs products for each reaction extent category
subject: chemistry

summary: The reaction quotient Q is calculated identically to Kc but using non-equilibrium concentrations. It predicts the direction of net reaction: Q < Kc indicates forward progression, Q > Kc indicates reverse progression, and Q = Kc means the system is at equilibrium.
diagram_description: Number line showing Kc position with Q on either side, indicating reaction direction with arrows
subject: chemistry

summary: Reaction direction is determined by comparing Qc to Kc: if Qc < Kc, the net reaction proceeds forward (left to right); if Qc > Kc, the net reaction proceeds backward (right to left); if Qc = Kc, no net reaction occurs. This follows Le Chatelier's principle.
diagram_description: Equilibrium diagram showing Qc positions relative to Kc with arrows indicating net forward or reverse reaction
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts
subtopic_names: Effect of Pressure and Volume Change
summary: Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in conditions, it shifts to counteract that change and restore equilibrium. Applicable to concentration, pressure, temperature, and catalyst changes in closed systems.
diagram_description: Flowchart showing perturbation (stress) applied to equilibrium system and resulting shift direction to counteract change
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts
summary: Addition of a reactant or removal of a product shifts equilibrium rightward (forward). Removal of a reactant or addition of a product shifts equilibrium leftward (reverse). The system adjusts to minimize the concentration change while maintaining Kc constant.
diagram_description: Equilibrium diagram for H2(g) + I2(g) ⇌ 2HI(g) showing concentration changes upon addition of H2 and resulting rightward shift

builds_upon.concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Effect of Pressure and Volume Change
parent_concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts
summary: Pressure increase (volume decrease) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas (Δn negative). Pressure decrease shifts equilibrium toward the side with more moles of gas. If Δn = 0 (equal moles on both sides), pressure change has no effect on equilibrium position.
diagram_description: Three reactions with different Δn values showing equilibrium shift direction upon pressure increase

builds_upon.concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts
summary: Temperature is the only factor that changes Kc. For exothermic reactions (ΔH < 0), increasing temperature shifts equilibrium left and decreases Kc. For endothermic reactions (ΔH > 0), increasing temperature shifts equilibrium right and increases Kc. Low temperature favours high yield for exothermic reactions but slows rate.
diagram_description: Two graphs: one showing Kc decrease for exothermic reaction with temperature; one for endothermic showing increase

builds_upon.concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts
summary: Catalysts increase the rates of both forward and reverse reactions equally by lowering activation energy through an alternative pathway. They do not change the equilibrium position, extent of reaction, or Kc value—they only accelerate the approach to equilibrium.
diagram_description: Reaction coordinate diagram showing catalyst providing lower-energy alternative pathway for forward and reverse reactions

builds_upon.concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts
summary: Addition of an inert gas at constant volume has no effect on equilibrium because partial pressures and molar concentrations of reactants and products remain unchanged. The inert gas increases total pressure but does not affect the reaction quotient or equilibrium.
diagram_description: Container with equilibrium mixture showing inert gas bubble addition at constant volume with unchanged partial pressures

builds_upon.concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts

subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases
Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases
Lewis Concept of Acids and Bases
summary: Ionic equilibrium involves equilibria of ions in aqueous solutions. Electrolytes conduct electricity by dissociating into ions; strong electrolytes dissociate completely, weak electrolytes partially. Acids, bases, and salts are electrolytes establishing dynamic equilibria between ions and unionized molecules.
diagram_description: Comparison table showing strong and weak electrolytes with conductivity and ionization percentages
subject: chemistry

summary: Electrolytes are substances that conduct electricity in aqueous solution due to ion production. Strong electrolytes (HCl, NaCl, NaOH) completely dissociate; weak electrolytes (acetic acid, ammonia) partially dissociate. Non-electrolytes (sugar) do not dissociate and do not conduct electricity.
diagram_description: Experimental setup showing electrical conductivity comparing strong electrolyte, weak electrolyte, and non-electrolyte solutions
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases
summary: According to Arrhenius, acids are substances producing H⁺ (hydronium H₃O⁺) ions in water; bases produce OH⁻ ions. This definition applies only to aqueous solutions and does not account for basicity of substances like ammonia lacking OH⁻ groups.
diagram_description: Two equations showing acid ionization producing H₃O⁺ and base ionization producing OH⁻ in water
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases
summary: Brönsted-Lowry theory defines acids as proton (H⁺) donors and bases as proton acceptors. This broader definition applies beyond aqueous solutions and accounts for amphoteric substances like water and ammonia. Water acts as both acid (donating H⁺) and base (accepting H⁺).
diagram_description: Equation showing water as acid and ammonia as base, with H⁺ transfer and roles labelled

builds_upon.concept_name: Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Lewis Concept of Acids and Bases
summary: Lewis theory defines acids as electron-pair acceptors and bases as electron-pair donors. This encompasses species without protons or hydroxyl groups, such as BF₃ (acid) accepting electron pairs from NH₃ (base), expanding acid-base chemistry beyond aqueous systems.
diagram_description: Lewis structure of BF₃·NH₃ complex showing electron-pair donation from nitrogen lone pair to boron

builds_upon.concept_name: Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

summary: A conjugate acid-base pair consists of two species differing by one proton. If HA is a strong acid, its conjugate base A⁻ is very weak; if HA is a weak acid, its conjugate base is strong. Examples: HCl/Cl⁻, H₂O/OH⁻, NH₄⁺/NH₃.
diagram_description: Table showing conjugate pairs with their conjugate acids and bases labelled with proton transfer arrows

builds_upon.concept_name: Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

summary: Water undergoes self-ionization: H₂O ⇌ H⁺ + OH⁻, with ionic product Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴ at 298K. The pH scale (pH = −log[H⁺]) measures hydrogen ion concentration. At equilibrium: pH < 7 (acidic), pH = 7 (neutral), pH > 7 (basic).
diagram_description: pH scale from 0-14 with common substances positioned showing acidic, neutral, and basic regions
subject: chemistry

summary: The ionic product of water, Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻], is a constant equal to 10⁻¹⁴ mol² L⁻² at 298K (25°C). In pure water, [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ M. Kw changes with temperature but remains applicable to all aqueous solutions.
diagram_description: Equation showing water self-ionization equilibrium with Kw expression
subject: chemistry

summary: The pH scale (0–14) measures hydrogen ion concentration logarithmically: pH = −log[H⁺]. A one-unit pH change corresponds to a 10-fold change in [H⁺]. pH < 7 indicates acidity, pH = 7 indicates neutrality, pH > 7 indicates basicity. pH paper and pH meters measure solution acidity.
diagram_description: pH scale showing 0-14 range with common substances (vinegar, blood, baking soda) at their typical pH values
subject: chemistry

summary: pOH is defined as −log[OH⁻]. The relationship pH + pOH = pKw = 14 (at 298K) connects pH and pOH for all aqueous solutions. From [OH⁻], one can calculate [H⁺], pH, and vice versa using these interconnected equations.
diagram_description: Table showing corresponding pH and pOH values at different acidities demonstrating pH + pOH = 14
subject: chemistry

summary: Solutions are classified by ion concentrations: acidic ([H⁺] > 10⁻⁷ M, pH < 7), neutral ([H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ M, pH = 7), or basic ([H⁺] < 10⁻⁷ M, pH > 7). In acidic solutions [H⁺] > [OH⁻]; in basic solutions [H⁺] < [OH⁻]; in neutral solutions [H⁺] = [OH⁻].
diagram_description: Three beakers showing acidic, neutral, and basic solutions with H+ and OH− ion distributions
subject: chemistry

summary: Weak acids and bases establish equilibrium between unionized and ionized forms. The ionization constant Ka (for acids) or Kb (for bases) quantifies strength. Degree of ionization (α) indicates the fraction dissociated and depends on initial concentration and common ions.
diagram_description: Equilibrium expressions for weak acid HA and weak base B showing Ka and Kb relationships
subject: chemistry

summary: Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA] quantifies weak acid strength; larger Ka indicates stronger acid. At a given temperature, Ka is constant and independent of concentration. pKa = −logKa provides a logarithmic scale; pKa < 7 for weak acids. Examples: acetic acid Ka = 1.74 × 10⁻⁵.
diagram_description: Table of weak acids with Ka and pKa values showing inverse relationship between Ka magnitude and acid strength
subject: chemistry

summary: Kb = [BH⁺][OH⁻]/[B] quantifies weak base strength; larger Kb indicates stronger base. At a given temperature, Kb is constant and independent of concentration. pKb = −logKb; stronger bases have larger Kb and smaller pKb. Example: ammonia Kb = 1.77 × 10⁻⁵.
diagram_description: Table of weak bases with Kb and pKb values showing inverse relationship between Kb magnitude and base strength
subject: chemistry

summary: Degree of ionization (α) is the fraction of weak electrolyte molecules ionized at equilibrium: α = [ionized]/[initial]. As concentration decreases, α increases (dilution effect). Percent ionization = α × 100%. Weak acids typically have α < 5%, strong electrolytes have α ≈ 100%.
diagram_description: Graph showing degree of ionization increasing as weak acid concentration decreases
subject: chemistry

summary: For a conjugate acid-base pair, Ka × Kb = Kw = 10⁻¹⁴ at 298K. Therefore, pKa + pKb = 14. A strong acid has a very weak conjugate base (small Kb); a weak acid has a stronger conjugate base (larger Kb). This relationship allows calculation of one constant from the other.
diagram_description: Table showing conjugate pairs with Ka and Kb values demonstrating Ka × Kb = 10−14
subject: chemistry

summary: Addition of a common ion (e.g., acetate to acetic acid solution) suppresses ionization of the weak acid/base by shifting equilibrium toward unionized form. This decreases [H⁺] or [OH⁻] and increases pH or pOH. The common ion effect follows Le Chatelier's principle.
diagram_description: Equilibrium diagram for acetic acid showing rightward shift (less ionization) upon acetate ion addition
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Hydrolysis of Salt of Weak Acid and Strong Base
Hydrolysis of Salt of Strong Acid and Weak Base
Hydrolysis of Salt of Weak Acid and Weak Base
summary: Salts undergo hydrolysis in water where ions interact with water molecules. Salts of weak acids/strong bases produce basic solutions; salts of strong acids/weak bases produce acidic solutions; salts of strong acids/strong bases produce neutral solutions. pH depends on relative Ka and Kb values.
diagram_description: Table categorizing salts by acid-base nature with resulting solution pH and hydrolysis reactions
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Hydrolysis of Salt of Weak Acid and Strong Base
summary: Salts of weak acids with strong bases (e.g., sodium acetate) produce basic solutions. The anion hydrolyzes: A⁻ + H₂O ⇌ HA + OH⁻, generating OH⁻ ions. pH > 7. The stronger the weak acid (higher Ka), the less basic the salt solution.
diagram_description: Hydrolysis equilibrium equation for acetate ion showing OH− generation and resulting basic pH
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Hydrolysis of Salt of Strong Acid and Weak Base
summary: Salts of weak bases with strong acids (e.g., ammonium chloride) produce acidic solutions. The cation hydrolyzes: BH⁺ + H₂O ⇌ B + H₃O⁺, generating H⁺ ions. pH < 7. The stronger the weak base (higher Kb), the more acidic the salt solution.
diagram_description: Hydrolysis equilibrium equation for ammonium ion showing H₃O⁺ generation and resulting acidic pH
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Hydrolysis of Salt of Weak Acid and Weak Base
summary: Salts of weak acids and weak bases (e.g., ammonium acetate) undergo simultaneous cation and anion hydrolysis. pH = 7 + ½(pKa − pKb). If pKa > pKb, solution is basic; if pKa < pKb, solution is acidic. Both hydrolysis equilibria must be considered.
diagram_description: Dual hydrolysis equations for ammonium acetate showing both cation and anion reactions
subject: chemistry

summary: Buffer solutions resist pH changes upon addition of small amounts of acid or base. Composed of a weak acid and its salt (e.g., acetic acid + sodium acetate) or weak base and its salt. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation relates pH to pKa and the ratio of salt to acid concentrations.
diagram_description: Acetic acid-acetate buffer equilibrium showing how added H+ and OH− are neutralized by buffer components
subject: chemistry

summary: Buffers consist of a weak acid and its conjugate base (salt), or a weak base and its conjugate acid salt. Example: acetic acid (CH₃COOH) and sodium acetate (CH₃COONa) form an acidic buffer at pH ≈ 4.76. The ratio of components determines the pH.
diagram_description: Representation of acidic and basic buffer compositions showing weak acid/base with their salt forms
subject: chemistry

summary: Buffers resist pH change by neutralizing added acid or base. Added H⁺ is consumed by the anion: H⁺ + A⁻ → HA. Added OH⁻ is consumed by the acid: HA + OH⁻ → A⁻ + H₂O. Equilibrium shifts maintain relatively constant pH.
diagram_description: Equilibrium diagram showing buffer responding to added acid and base with arrows indicating equilibrium shifts
subject: chemistry

summary: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation pH = pKa + log([Salt]/[Acid]) calculates buffer pH from pKa and component concentration ratio. When [Salt] = [Acid], pH = pKa. Buffers work best within ±1 unit of pKa. This equation is essential for designing buffers of desired pH.
diagram_description: Graph showing pH vs log([Salt]/[Acid]) demonstrating linear relationship with pKa as y-intercept
subject: chemistry

summary: Buffer capacity is the amount of acid or base a buffer can neutralize before significant pH change occurs. It increases with higher concentrations of buffer components and is maximized when [Salt] = [Acid]. A buffer with 1 M components has greater capacity than one with 0.1 M components.
diagram_description: Graph comparing pH change for different buffer concentrations upon addition of strong acid/base
subject: chemistry

summary: Sparingly soluble salts establish equilibrium between solid and dissolved ions in saturated solution. The solubility product Ksp = [cation]^x[anion]^y (where x, y are stoichiometric coefficients) is constant at given temperature. If ion product Q > Ksp, precipitation occurs; if Q < Ksp, dissolution occurs.
diagram_description: Table showing Ksp expressions for different salt stoichiometries and precipitation conditions
subject: chemistry

summary: Ksp is the equilibrium constant for a saturated solution of a sparingly soluble salt. For a salt MxXy dissolving as MxXy(s) ⇌ xM^p+(aq) + yX^q−(aq), Ksp = [M^p+]^x[X^q−]^y. The pure solid concentration is omitted (constant). Larger Ksp indicates higher solubility.
diagram_description: Comparison table of Ksp values for different salts showing relationship to molar solubility
subject: chemistry

summary: Molar solubility S is calculated from Ksp using stoichiometric relationships. For BaSO₄: Ksp = S² = 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰, so S = 1.05 × 10⁻⁵ M. For A₂X₃: Ksp = (2S)²(3S)³ = 108S⁵. Rearrangement gives S = (Ksp/108)^(1/5).
diagram_description: Worked examples showing solubility calculation from Ksp for 1:1 and complex stoichiometry salts
subject: chemistry

summary: Addition of a common ion (e.g., Cl⁻ to AgCl solution) increases ion product Q, causing Q > Ksp and precipitation of the salt. This decreases solubility. Higher common ion concentration causes greater suppression. Example: solubility of AgCl in 0.1M NaCl is lower than in pure water.
diagram_description: Graph showing solubility of AgCl decreasing with increasing [Cl−] concentration from external source
subject: chemistry

summary: Solubility of salts of weak acids increases at lower pH. Addition of H⁺ protonates the anion (e.g., CO₃²⁻ → HCO₃⁻ → H₂CO₃), decreasing its concentration and shifting dissolution equilibrium rightward. Relationship: S = (Ksp([H⁺] + Ka)/Ka)^(1/2) for salts like CaCO₃.
diagram_description: Graph showing solubility of CaCO₃ increasing dramatically at lower pH due to carbonate ion protonation
subject: chemistry

summary: The Haber process synthesises ammonia: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g). Isotope studies (using D₂ instead of H₂) proved dynamic equilibrium by showing scrambling of H and D atoms. Industrial conditions (~500°C, 200 atm, iron catalyst) balance rate and equilibrium yield, demonstrating practical application of equilibrium principles.
diagram_description: Industrial Haber reactor diagram with temperature, pressure, and catalyst conditions annotated

builds_upon.concept_name: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Shifts

subject: chemistry

summary: Heterogeneous equilibrium involves reactants and products in different phases. Pure solids and liquids have constant concentration and are omitted from equilibrium expressions. Example: CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g); Kc = [CO₂] because solid concentrations are constant.
diagram_description: Thermal decomposition of CaCO₃ showing solid, solid, and gas phases with Kc expression excluding solids
subject: chemistry

summary: Polyprotic acids ionise sequentially with decreasing ionization constants: H₂X ⇌ H⁺ + HX⁻ (Ka₁), then HX⁻ ⇌ H⁺ + X²⁻ (Ka₂). Ka₁ >> Ka₂ because removing a proton from a negative ion is difficult. Most H⁺ comes from the first ionisation. Example: H₂SO₄ has Ka₁ very large and Ka₂ = 0.012.
diagram_description: Successive ionization equations for dibasic acid with Ka1 >> Ka2 relationship illustrated
subject: chemistry

summary: Redox reactions involve simultaneous oxidation and reduction. Classically, oxidation was the addition of oxygen or removal of hydrogen; reduction was removal of oxygen or addition of hydrogen. The definition was broadened to include transfer of electronegative elements (oxidation) and electropositive elements (reduction).
diagram_description: Timeline showing evolution from oxygen-based definition to modern electron-transfer model
subject: chemistry

summary: Addition of oxygen to an element/compound, removal of hydrogen, or removal of electropositive element. Examples: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO; 2H₂S + O₂ → 2S + 2H₂O; Mg + Cl₂ → MgCl₂.
diagram_description: Examples showing oxidation by oxygen addition, hydrogen removal, and halogen addition
subject: chemistry

summary: Removal of oxygen from a compound, addition of hydrogen, or addition of electropositive element. Examples: 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂; 2FeCl₃ + H₂ → 2FeCl₂ + 2HCl; CH₂=CH₂ + H₂ → CH₃-CH₃.
diagram_description: Examples showing reduction by oxygen removal, hydrogen addition, and electropositive element addition
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Oxidation and Reduction as Electron Transfer
aliases: Redox Reactions in Terms of Electron Transfer
summary: Redox reactions are fundamentally electron-transfer processes. Oxidation is loss of electrons; reduction is gain. Half-reactions explicitly show electron involvement, and the sum of half-reactions gives the overall reaction. Ions Na⁺Cl⁻, (Na⁺)₂O²⁻ show charge development from electron transfer.
diagram_description: Electron flow diagram showing oxidation and reduction half-reactions in sodium chloride formation
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Oxidation and Reduction as Electron Transfer
summary: Oxidation is the loss of one or more electrons by any species. Reduction is the gain of one or more electrons by any species. In 2Na(s) → 2Na⁺(g) + 2e⁻, sodium is oxidised; in Cl₂(g) + 2e⁻ → 2Cl⁻(g), chlorine is reduced.
diagram_description: Half-reaction equations showing electron loss in oxidation and electron gain in reduction
subject: chemistry

summary: A half-reaction explicitly shows electron involvement in oxidation or reduction. A redox couple consists of the oxidised and reduced forms of a substance, written as Oxidised/Reduced (e.g., Zn²⁺/Zn, Cu²⁺/Cu). Half-reactions are separated by | or / representing an interface.
diagram_description: Representation of redox couple notation and half-reaction writing in galvanic cell context
subject: chemistry

summary: An oxidising agent (oxidant) accepts electrons and is itself reduced. A reducing agent (reductant) donates electrons and is itself oxidised. In 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl, Na is the reducing agent (donates e⁻); Cl₂ is the oxidising agent (accepts e⁻).
diagram_description: Electron flow showing oxidising agent accepting electrons and reducing agent donating electrons
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Identifying Oxidation and Reduction by Oxidation Number
summary: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound, assigned by assuming complete electron transfer to the more electronegative atom. It is a bookkeeping tool for tracking electron shifts in reactions. Rules assign oxidation numbers systematically based on electronegativity and bonding patterns.
diagram_description: Table showing oxidation numbers assigned to atoms in H₂O, CO₂, H₂S, and other compounds
subject: chemistry

summary: Six rules: (1) Uncombined elements = 0; (2) Monatomic ions = charge on ion; (3) O usually –2 (exceptions: peroxides –1, superoxides –½, with F +2); (4) H usually +1 (except metal hydrides –1); (5) F always –1, other halogens –1 in halides, positive with O; (6) Sum = 0 in compounds, equals charge in polyatomic ions.
diagram_description: Step-by-step guide showing application of six oxidation number assignment rules with examples
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Identifying Oxidation and Reduction by Oxidation Number
summary: Oxidation is an increase in oxidation number; reduction is a decrease. An oxidising agent increases the oxidation number of another element; a reducing agent lowers it. By comparing oxidation numbers before and after a reaction, redox reactions are easily identified and classified.
diagram_description: Reaction showing oxidation number changes identifying oxidised/reduced species and agents
subject: chemistry
