summary: Stock notation expresses the oxidation number of a metal as a Roman numeral in parentheses after the metal symbol. Examples: Au(I)Cl for aurous chloride, Au(III)Cl₃ for auric chloride, Sn(II)Cl₂ for stannous chloride, Sn(IV)Cl₄ for stannic chloride.
diagram_description: Examples of Stock notation for common metal compounds with varying oxidation states
subject: chemistry

summary: Redox reactions are classified into four types: combination (A + B → C, both/either elemental), decomposition (compound → components, one elemental), displacement (X + YZ → XZ + Y), and disproportionation (one element simultaneously oxidised and reduced). Each involves change in oxidation numbers.
diagram_description: Flowchart categorising redox reactions with examples of each type
subject: chemistry

summary: A + B → C where at least one reactant is elemental. Includes all combustion reactions using O₂ and reactions with other elements. Examples: C(s) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g); 3Mg(s) + N₂(g) → Mg₃N₂(s); CH₄(g) + 2O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2H₂O(l).
diagram_description: Examples of combination reactions showing two elements combining to form a compound
subject: chemistry

summary: A compound breaks down into two or more components, at least one elemental. Not all decomposition is redox (e.g., CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ is not). Examples: 2H₂O(l) → 2H₂(g) + O₂(g); 2NaH(s) → 2Na(s) + H₂(g); 2KClO₃(s) → 2KCl(s) + 3O₂(g).
diagram_description: Examples of decomposition reactions with oxidation number changes clearly marked
subject: chemistry

summary: X + YZ → XZ + Y; an ion/atom in a compound is replaced by another element's ion/atom. Metal displacement: Zn(s) + Cu²⁺(aq) → Zn²⁺(aq) + Cu(s). Non-metal displacement includes hydrogen displacement: Zn(s) + 2HCl(aq) → ZnCl₂(aq) + H₂(g).
diagram_description: Metal activity series showing displacement reactions with decreasing reactivity from Zn to Ag
subject: chemistry

summary: A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive one from its salt solution. The reducing metal is a better reducing agent. Examples: Zn(s) + CuSO₄(aq) → Cu(s) + ZnSO₄(aq); V₂O₅(s) + 5Ca(s) → 2V(s) + 5CaO(s). Order: Zn > Cu > Ag.
diagram_description: Metal displacement reaction showing zinc displacing copper from copper nitrate solution
subject: chemistry

summary: Non-metals displace hydrogen from water/acids or displace halides from solutions. Active metals displace H₂ from cold water: 2Na(s) + 2H₂O(l) → 2NaOH(aq) + H₂(g). Halogens displace less reactive ones: Cl₂(g) + 2KBr(aq) → 2KCl(aq) + Br₂(l). Halogen reactivity: F > Cl > Br > I.
diagram_description: Halogen displacement reactions showing colour changes and activity series F > Cl > Br > I
subject: chemistry

summary: An element in one oxidation state is simultaneously oxidised and reduced to higher and lower states. The reacting substance contains an element in an intermediate oxidation state that can exist in at least three states. Example: 2H₂O₂(aq) → 2H₂O(l) + O₂(g); oxygen in –1 state becomes 0 and –2.
diagram_description: Disproportionation of chlorine in alkali showing Cl₂ forming ClO⁻ and Cl⁻
subject: chemistry

summary: Two systematic methods balance redox equations: (1) Oxidation number method using electron gain/loss; (2) Half-reaction method splitting into oxidation and reduction halves. Both account for electron transfer and produce balanced equations respecting mass and charge.
diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison of oxidation number and half-reaction methods for balancing
subject: chemistry

summary: Five steps: (1) Write correct formulas; (2) Assign oxidation numbers and identify changes; (3) Calculate total increase/decrease and multiply to equalise; (4) Add H⁺ or OH⁻ for charge balance (acidic/basic); (5) Add H₂O to balance H and O atoms. Works well for simple reactions.
diagram_description: Step-by-step oxidation number method example balancing dichromate-sulphite reaction

builds_upon.concept_name: Identifying Oxidation and Reduction by Oxidation Number

subject: chemistry

summary: Seven steps: (1) Write unbalanced ionic equation; (2) Separate into oxidation and reduction halves; (3) Balance atoms other than O/H; (4) Balance O with H₂O, H with H⁺ (or OH⁻ for basic); (5) Add electrons to balance charge; (6) Equalise electrons in both halves; (7) Add halves and cancel electrons. Precise and systematic.
diagram_description: Detailed walkthrough of half-reaction method for permanganate oxidising iodide
subject: chemistry

summary: Redox reactions reach different equilibrium states based on each species' tendency to lose electrons. Zn releases electrons to Cu²⁺ and Cu releases to Ag⁺, establishing an order Zn > Cu > Ag. This competition principle underlies galvanic cells and metal displacement reactions.
diagram_description: Comparative electron transfer reactions showing why Zn displaces Cu but not vice versa
subject: chemistry

summary: Metals differ in their tendency to release electrons and form ions. Zinc is a stronger reducing agent than copper, which is stronger than silver. This relative strength is revealed by displacement reactions: Zn releases electrons to Cu²⁺ (and Ag⁺); Cu releases to Ag⁺ but not Zn²⁺.
diagram_description: Comparison of displacement reactions showing relative electron-releasing tendency
subject: chemistry

summary: A systematic list of metals and their ions ranked by tendency to release electrons (reducing power). Metals at the top are more reactive and stronger reducing agents. Among halogens: F > Cl > Br > I in oxidising power. Enables prediction of displacement reactions and galvanic cell behaviour.
diagram_description: Activity series table showing metals and halogens ranked by reducing and oxidising power
subject: chemistry

summary: Quantitative analysis using redox reactions with colour-based endpoint detection. Self-indicators (e.g., MnO₄⁻, pink) show the endpoint directly. External indicators (e.g., diphenylamine with Cr₂O₇²⁻) change colour after equivalence. Iodometric titrations use I₂-starch colour.
diagram_description: Redox titration apparatus and colour change indicators for permanganate and dichromate
subject: chemistry

summary: The titrant itself is intensely coloured and serves as the indicator. MnO₄⁻ (purple/pink) is a classic example; the endpoint is reached when the first lasting tinge of pink appears after all reductant is consumed. Ensures minimal colour overshoot beyond equivalence point.
diagram_description: Permanganate titration showing colourless solution turning pink at endpoint
subject: chemistry

summary: A separate indicator substance is oxidised by the titrant after all reductant is consumed, producing a sharp colour change. Dichromate (Cr₂O₇²⁻) with diphenylamine produces intense blue colour at the endpoint. Used when the titrant itself is not coloured.
diagram_description: Dichromate titration with diphenylamine turning blue at the endpoint
subject: chemistry

summary: Oxidising agents oxidise I⁻ to I₂. I₂ forms a blue complex with starch (intense colour). Titration with S₂O₃²⁻ consumes I₂ via: I₂(aq) + 2S₂O₃²⁻(aq) → 2I⁻(aq) + S₄O₆²⁻(aq). Endpoint: blue colour disappears.
diagram_description: Iodometric titration showing I₂-starch blue colour disappearing as thiosulphate is added
subject: chemistry

summary: Redox reactions can occur indirectly in electrochemical cells where electrons transfer through an external circuit instead of directly. Each half-reaction occurs at an electrode; potential difference drives electron flow. Redox couples (Zn²⁺/Zn, Cu²⁺/Cu) define the electrode processes.
diagram_description: Galvanic cell diagram showing anode, cathode, salt bridge, electron and ion flow
subject: chemistry

summary: A redox couple (Oxidised/Reduced, e.g., Zn²⁺/Zn) consists of both forms of a species in an electrode reaction. The electrode potential is the tendency of the couple to undergo reduction. Potential difference between two electrodes drives electron flow in galvanic cells.
diagram_description: Redox couple notation and how potential difference is created between two electrodes
subject: chemistry

summary: Standard electrode potential (E⁰) is the potential at 298 K with all species at unit concentration (gases at 1 atm). By convention, H⁺/H₂ = 0.00 V. Negative E⁰ indicates a stronger reducing agent than H₂; positive E⁰ indicates a weaker reducing agent. Table 7.1 lists standard potentials for many couples.
diagram_description: Standard electrode potential table with increasing oxidising power shown vertically
subject: chemistry

summary: Classic galvanic cell with Zn anode in ZnSO₄ solution and Cu cathode in CuSO₄ solution, connected by a salt bridge. Zn is oxidised (anode): Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; Cu²⁺ is reduced (cathode): Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. Electrons flow externally through a wire; ions flow internally through salt bridge.
diagram_description: Daniell cell diagram showing zinc and copper electrodes, salt bridge, and electron/ion flow
subject: chemistry

summary: Some compounds (C₃O₂, Br₃O₈, Na₂S₄O₆) show fractional average oxidation numbers, which represent atoms in different whole-number oxidation states. C₃O₂ has two C atoms at +2 and one at 0 (average 4/3). Fractional states indicate mixed valence; actual states are revealed by structure, not by fractional number.
diagram_description: Structures of C₃O₂, Br₃O₈, and S₄O₆²⁻ showing different oxidation states of each atom
subject: chemistry

summary: Oxidation number is a bookkeeping tool assuming complete electron transfer; in reality, charge transfer in covalent bonds is partial. Modern redox is redefined as change in electron density around atoms. The oxidation number concept has evolved and continues to evolve with deeper understanding of chemical bonding.
diagram_description: Comparison of oxidation number concept vs. electron density changes in covalent compounds
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Bond Length and Bond Enthalpy Trends
summary: Carbon exhibits tetravalence through hybridisation of its s and p orbitals into sp3, sp2, and sp hybrid states. sp3 hybridisation produces tetrahedral geometry (methane), sp2 produces trigonal planar (ethene), and sp produces linear geometry (ethyne). Hybridisation state directly affects bond length, bond strength, and electronegativity of the carbon atom.
diagram_description: Diagram showing sp3, sp2, and sp orbital hybridisation with bond angles: tetrahedral (109.5°), trigonal planar (120°), and linear (180°)
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: sp3 hybridisation combines one s and three p orbitals to produce four equivalent hybrid orbitals arranged tetrahedrally (109.5° angles). sp2 hybridisation mixes one s and two p orbitals, leaving one unhybridised p orbital perpendicular to the plane, creating trigonal planar geometry (120° angles). sp hybridisation uses one s and one p orbital, producing two linear hybrid orbitals (180° angle) and two unhybridised p orbitals.
diagram_description: Orbital diagrams showing sp3 (4 lobes tetrahedral), sp2 (3 lobes planar with perpendicular p), and sp (2 linear lobes with perpendicular p orbitals)
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

concept_name: Bond Length and Bond Enthalpy Trends
summary: As s character of hybrid orbitals increases (sp > sp2 > sp3), bonds become shorter and stronger because the s orbital is closer to the nucleus. sp hybrid C-H bonds are shortest and strongest, followed by sp2, then sp3. This trend results from the spherical nature of s orbitals bringing electron density closer to the nucleus.
diagram_description: Bar graph showing bond lengths and bond enthalpies for C-H bonds in sp, sp2, and sp3 hybridised carbons
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Greater s character in hybrid orbitals increases electronegativity because s electrons are held closer to the nucleus. sp hybridised carbon (50% s character) is most electronegative, followed by sp2 (33% s character), then sp3 (25% s character). This relative electronegativity affects molecular properties including polarity, reactivity, and chemical behaviour.
diagram_description: Table comparing s character percentage and electronegativity values for sp, sp2, and sp3 hybridised carbons

builds_upon.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: π bonds form through lateral (side-by-side) overlap of unhybridised p orbitals on adjacent atoms and are weaker than σ bonds. Multiple bonds (C=C, C≡C) consist of one σ bond plus one or two π bonds. π bonds restrict rotation about the bond axis and make those bonds reactive sites for nucleophilic and electrophilic attack.
diagram_description: Side view showing p orbital lateral overlap forming π bonds above and below the molecular plane
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: π bonds require parallel orientation of p orbitals on adjacent atoms for proper sideways overlap. In H₂C=CH₂, all atoms lie in one plane with p orbitals mutually parallel and perpendicular to the molecular plane. The electron charge cloud of the π bond extends above and below the bonding atoms.
diagram_description: 3D representation of ethene showing planar geometry with p orbitals perpendicular to the plane and π electron density above and below
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Rotation of one CH₂ fragment relative to another in alkenes interferes with maximum π bond orbital overlap, requiring breaking the π bond. This makes rotation about C=C bonds energetically unfavourable and extremely slow at room temperature, preventing free rotation unlike single bonds.
diagram_description: Animation showing how rotating one CH₂ group in ethene breaks π orbital alignment, increasing energy
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: π electron density lies above and below the molecular plane, making electrons easily accessible to attacking reagents. π bonds provide the most reactive centres in molecules containing multiple bonds and are sites for nucleophilic and electrophilic addition reactions.
diagram_description: Space-filling model showing π electron density cloud above and below C=C or C≡C bonds
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Organic compounds can be represented using various notations: Lewis/dash structures show all atoms and bonds explicitly; condensed formulas omit C-H bonds; bond-line formulas use lines for bonds with implied carbons and hydrogens; and wedge-dash notation indicates 3D geometry on paper. Each format has specific advantages for different applications.
diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison of ethane, ethene, ethyne, and methanol in complete, condensed, and bond-line formats

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Complete structural formulas show all atoms and covalent bonds using Lewis dot notation or dashes. Single bonds are represented by one dash, double bonds by two dashes, and triple bonds by three dashes. Lone pairs on heteroatoms (O, N, S, halogens) may or may not be shown depending on context.
diagram_description: Examples of complete Lewis structures for ethane (C2H6), ethene (C2H4), ethyne (C2H2), and methanol (CH3OH)
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Condensed structural formulas abbreviate structures by omitting some or all dashes for C-H and C-C bonds and using subscripts to indicate repeated groups. Example: CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₃ condenses to CH₃(CH₂)₆CH₃. This notation is used for quick reference and when detailed bonding information is not essential.
diagram_description: Comparison of condensed formulas for various alkanes and functional group compounds

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Bond-line formulas represent structures using only lines for bonds in a zig-zag pattern. Carbon and hydrogen atoms are not explicitly shown; line terminals denote methyl groups (CH₃) and line junctions represent carbons. Only heteroatoms (O, N, Cl, etc.) are explicitly written. This notation is most widely used in organic chemistry.
diagram_description: Bond-line structures of 3-methyloctane, 2-bromobutane, cyclopropane, and chlorocyclohexane in zig-zag notation

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Wedge-and-dash notation represents 3D structures on 2D paper. Solid wedges (broad end toward observer) show bonds projecting toward the viewer, dashed wedges show bonds projecting away, and normal lines show bonds in the molecular plane. This convention allows representation of stereochemistry and spatial relationships.
diagram_description: Wedge-and-dash representation of methane (CH4) showing one bond toward viewer, one away, and two in plane

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Organic compounds are classified as acyclic (open chain) or cyclic (closed ring). Acyclic compounds are aliphatic; cyclic compounds include alicyclic (saturated rings) and aromatic (benzene-like). Within each category, compounds are further grouped by functional groups, which determine their chemical properties and reactivity.
diagram_description: Hierarchical classification flowchart showing acyclic vs cyclic, alicyclic vs aromatic, and benzenoid vs non-benzenoid compounds

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Acyclic (open chain) compounds consist of carbons in straight or branched chains (e.g., ethane, isobutane, acetaldehyde). Cyclic compounds have carbon atoms joined in closed rings. Acyclic compounds are also called aliphatic compounds and exhibit properties similar to alkanes regardless of functional groups.
diagram_description: Examples of acyclic straight-chain compounds (ethane, pentane), branched compounds (isobutane), and cyclic compounds (cyclopropane, cyclohexane)
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Alicyclic compounds are saturated rings with properties similar to aliphatic compounds (e.g., cyclopropane, cyclohexane). Aromatic compounds contain benzene rings or similar π systems with exceptional stability. Benzenoid aromatics include benzene, aniline, and naphthalene. Heteroaromatic compounds contain non-carbon atoms (N, O, S) in aromatic rings.
diagram_description: Structures of cyclopropane and cyclohexane (alicyclic), benzene and aniline (benzenoid aromatic), and furan and thiophene (heteroaromatic)

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: A functional group is an atom or group of atoms bonded in a unique manner that determines characteristic chemical properties. A homologous series is a group of compounds with the same functional group, differing by successive CH₂ units (e.g., alkanes, alcohols, carboxylic acids). Members share similar chemical behaviour but different physical properties.
diagram_description: Table showing first five members of alkane, alcohol, and aldehyde series with structural formulas and boiling points
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subtopic_names: Nomenclature of Compounds with Functional Groups
summary: IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) nomenclature provides systematic rules for naming organic compounds based on structure. A name is derived by identifying the parent hydrocarbon, longest carbon chain containing functional groups, and number of substituents and functional groups. Prefixes and suffixes indicate position and type of functional groups.
diagram_description: Example IUPAC naming showing parent chain, principal functional group, substituents, and position numbers for a complex organic compound

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Straight-chain alkanes are named using prefixes (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-, etc.) with suffix '-ane'. For branched alkanes, identify the longest carbon chain as the parent, number it to give lowest numbers to substituents, and list alkyl branches alphabetically before the parent name. Example: 6-ethyl-2-methylnonane has a 9-carbon parent chain with ethyl at position 6 and methyl at position 2.
diagram_description: Nomenclature examples showing straight-chain alkanes (methane through decane) and branched structures with position numbers and substituent names

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

concept_name: Nomenclature of Compounds with Functional Groups
summary: The principal functional group determines the suffix (-ol for alcohols, -one for ketones, -al for aldehydes, -oic acid for carboxylic acids, etc.). The longest chain containing the principal functional group is numbered to give the functional group the lowest number. Subordinate functional groups are named as prefixes. In polyfunctional compounds, a hierarchy of functional group priorities determines which is principal.
diagram_description: Table of functional groups with IUPAC prefixes and suffixes; examples of mono- and polyfunctional compound nomenclature

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: For monosubstituted benzenes, the substituent is prefixed to 'benzene' (e.g., chlorobenzene). For disubstituted benzenes, positions are numbered 1,2- (ortho), 1,3- (meta), or 1,4- (para); substituents listed alphabetically precede benzene. For polysubstituted benzenes, use IUPAC numbering to give lowest locant numbers. When a functional group takes priority, benzene becomes a substituent named 'phenyl' (C₆H₅-).
diagram_description: Examples of monosubstituted (bromobenzene), disubstituted (ortho-, meta-, para-dibromobenzene), and polysubstituted benzene nomenclature

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Isomerism is the existence of two or more compounds with the same molecular formula but different properties. Structural isomers differ in the arrangement of atoms (chain, position, functional group, or metamerism). Stereoisomers have identical atomic connectivity but differ in spatial orientation (geometrical or optical isomerism).
diagram_description: Hierarchical chart showing structural isomerism (chain, position, functional group, metamerism) and stereoisomerism (geometrical, optical) with examples

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity. Chain isomerism occurs when carbons are arranged in different skeletons (pentane vs isopentane). Position isomerism results from different positions of substituents or functional groups on the same carbon skeleton (propan-1-ol vs propan-2-ol). Functional group isomerism involves different functional groups (propanone vs propanal).
diagram_description: Structures showing chain isomers (pentane/isopentane), position isomers (propan-1-ol/propan-2-ol), and functional group isomers (propanal/propanone)

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Stereoisomers have the same molecular formula and identical connectivity but differ in spatial orientation. Geometrical isomerism occurs around C=C double bonds (cis/trans), prevented by restricted rotation. Optical isomerism arises in molecules with asymmetric carbon centres that exist as non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers). Both types are studied in detail in later classes.
diagram_description: Examples of geometrical isomers (cis- and trans-but-2-ene) showing different spatial arrangements around C=C

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Reaction mechanism describes the step-by-step breaking and forming of bonds during an organic reaction, including electron movements, intermediates, and energetics. A substrate (molecule providing carbon to new bond) reacts with a reagent, proceeding through intermediates to form products. Mechanisms are classified by bond fission type (heterolytic → ionic reactions; homolytic → free radical reactions) and reagent type (nucleophilic or electrophilic).
diagram_description: Reaction coordinate diagram showing energy barriers, intermediates, and transition states for a typical organic reaction

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Heterolytic cleavage breaks a bond with both electrons going to one fragment, producing carbocations (C⁺, electron-deficient, sp² hybridised) or carbanions (C⁻, electron-rich, sp³ hybridised). Homolytic cleavage distributes one electron to each fragment, producing free radicals (unpaired electron, very reactive). Carbocations and carbanions are ionic intermediates; free radicals are neutral. Stability order: tertiary > secondary > primary for both cations and radicals.
diagram_description: Diagrams showing heterolytic cleavage (carbocation/carbanion formation) and homolytic cleavage (free radical formation) with curved arrows and electron distribution
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Nucleophiles (Nu:) are electron-pair donors with lone pairs or π electrons; they attack electron-deficient (electrophilic) centres. Examples: HO⁻, CN⁻, R₃C:⁻, :NH₃, R:OR. Electrophiles (E⁺) are electron-pair acceptors with incomplete octets or partial positive charges; they attack electron-rich (nucleophilic) centres. Examples: C⁺H₃, R₃C⁺, C=O, R-X. In polar reactions, nucleophiles attack electrophilic centres via curly arrow electron transfer.
diagram_description: Examples of nucleophiles (negatively charged ions, neutral molecules with lone pairs) and electrophiles (carbocations, polar C=O, alkyl halides) with arrow showing attack
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Electronic displacement effects explain how electron density is distributed in molecules, affecting reactivity. Inductive effect (permanent) involves polarisation of σ bonds through electron withdrawal/donation by adjacent groups. Resonance effect involves delocalisation through π systems. Electromeric effect (temporary) occurs when π electrons shift on demand of attacking reagents.
diagram_description: Diagrams showing inductive effect (polarisation along chain), resonance in benzene and nitrobenzene, electromeric effect on C=O, and hyperconjugation in carbocations

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Organic reactions are classified into four main types. Substitution reactions replace one group with another. Addition reactions join two reactants by breaking multiple bonds. Elimination reactions remove a group and form a double bond.
diagram_description: Examples of substitution (SN2 on alkyl halide), addition (HBr to alkene), elimination (E2 to form alkene), and rearrangement reactions
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: The inductive effect is permanent polarisation of σ bonds caused by electronegative atoms or groups. Electron-withdrawing groups (-I effect: halogens, NO₂, CN, COOH) pull electron density, creating δ+ on adjacent carbons. Alkyl groups (+I effect) donate electron density. The effect diminishes through three bonds.
diagram_description: Structural diagram of chloroethane showing δ+ and δ- charges along the chain due to inductive effect of Cl; table of electron-withdrawing and donating groups
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Resonance structures are hypothetical Lewis structures representing actual molecule as a hybrid when single structure is inadequate (e.g., benzene). Resonance stabilisation energy is the energy difference between the hybrid and lowest-energy canonical form. Positive resonance effect (+R): electron donation into π system (–OH, –OR, –NH₂, halogens). Negative resonance effect (–R): electron withdrawal from π system (–CHO, –C=O, –CN, –NO₂).
diagram_description: Resonance structures of benzene, nitrobenzene, aniline, and acetate ion showing electron delocalisation with curved arrows
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: The electromeric effect is temporary polarisation of π electrons in multiple bonds on demand of an attacking reagent. Positive E effect (+E): π electrons shift toward the atom being attacked. Negative E effect (–E): π electrons shift away from the atom being attacked. The effect exists only during reaction and vanishes when the reagent is removed.
diagram_description: Diagrams showing +E effect (π electrons toward reagent attack site) and -E effect (π electrons away from attack site) with curved arrows on C=O and C=C

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Hyperconjugation is delocalisation of σ electrons from a C-H bond of an alkyl group into an adjacent π system or empty p orbital. In carbocations, C-H bonds align with the empty p orbital, allowing electron density to partially occupy that orbital and dispersing the positive charge. This stabilises carbocations; stability: tertiary > secondary > primary. Hyperconjugation also occurs in alkenes and explains the stability of the allyl cation.
diagram_description: Orbital diagrams showing C-H bond alignment with empty p orbital in ethyl cation and C-H orbital overlap in propene; stability comparison chart

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Organic compound purification relies on differences in physical properties. Sublimation separates sublimable solids from non-sublimable impurities. Crystallisation exploits differential solubility in solvents at different temperatures. Distillation separates liquids by boiling point differences.
diagram_description: Comparison table showing purification methods, their principles, when to use each, and examples of compounds separated
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Sublimation converts solids directly to vapours without melting; used for compounds like iodine and naphthalene that sublime at accessible temperatures, separating them from non-sublimable impurities. Crystallisation dissolves impure compound in hot solvent where it is highly soluble, then cools to allow pure crystals to form and be filtered. Repeated crystallisation removes impurities of comparable solubility. Activated charcoal removes coloured impurities.
diagram_description: Diagrams of sublimation apparatus and crystallisation flask showing dissolving in hot solvent, cooling, and crystal formation
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Simple distillation separates volatile liquids from non-volatile impurities and liquids with large boiling point differences. Fractional distillation uses a fractionating column for liquids with small boiling point differences, allowing successive condensation and vaporisation. Distillation under reduced pressure lowers boiling points for temperature-sensitive compounds. Steam distillation exploits the principle p = p₁ + p₂ to allow water-immiscible compounds to vaporise at <100°C.
diagram_description: Apparatus diagrams for simple distillation, fractional distillation with fractionating column, vacuum distillation, and steam distillation setups
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Differential extraction separates an organic compound from aqueous solution using an immiscible organic solvent where the compound is more soluble. Two immiscible layers form and are separated using a separatory funnel. The organic solvent is then removed by distillation or evaporation. Continuous extraction is used when the compound has low solubility in the organic solvent, recycling the solvent repeatedly.
diagram_description: Diagram of separatory funnel showing two immiscible layers (aqueous and organic) before and after extraction
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Chromatography separates mixtures based on differential movement through two phases. Stationary phase (solid or liquid) and mobile phase (liquid or gas) interact with mixture components differently. Adsorption chromatography (silica gel, alumina) separates by differential adsorption. Partition chromatography (paper) separates by differential partitioning.
diagram_description: Apparatus diagrams for column chromatography, TLC plate setup showing baseline and solvent front, and paper chromatography with examples of separated dyes
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subtopic_names: Detection of Nitrogen, Sulphur, Halogens, and Phosphorus
summary: Qualitative analysis identifies elements present in organic compounds. Carbon and hydrogen are detected by heating the compound with CuO; CO₂ is detected by lime-water turbidity and H₂O by CuSO₄ colour change. Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus are detected by Lassaigne's test: fusion with sodium metal converts elements to ionic forms (NaCN, Na₂S, NaX, Na₃PO₄) which are detected by characteristic tests.
diagram_description: Flowchart of Lassaigne's test showing sodium fusion, water extraction, and parallel tests for N (Prussian blue), S (black ppt), X (AgX), and P (yellow ppt)

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: The compound is heated with CuO. Carbon oxidises to CO₂ (detected by lime-water developing turbidity/white ppt CaCO₃). Hydrogen oxidises to H₂O (detected by anhydrous CuSO₄ turning blue). Gas mixture is passed through weighed tubes containing calcium chloride (absorbs water) and potassium hydroxide solution (absorbs CO₂). Mass increase determines carbon and hydrogen content.
diagram_description: Apparatus showing combustion tube with CuO, connected to U-tubes for water and CO₂ absorption, with lime-water test
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

concept_name: Detection of Nitrogen, Sulphur, Halogens, and Phosphorus
summary: Compound is fused with sodium metal; elements convert to ionic forms: Na + C + N → NaCN; 2Na + S → Na₂S; Na + X → NaX (X = Cl, Br, I). Extract with water (sodium fusion extract). Nitrogen: boil with FeSO₄, acidify with H₂SO₄, blue Prussian blue ppt Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃. Sulphur: acidify with acetic acid, add Pb(CH₃COO)₂, black PbS ppt.
diagram_description: Flowchart showing sodium fusion extract analysis with four branches for N, S, Cl, and P detection, each showing reagent and characteristic colour/ppt
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Quantitative analysis determines the mass percentage of elements in organic compounds. Results are essential for calculating empirical and molecular formulas. Carbon and hydrogen are estimated by combustion analysis; nitrogen by Dumas or Kjeldahl methods; halogens by Carius method; sulphur and phosphorus by oxidation to sulphates/phosphates and gravimetric determination; oxygen is usually found by difference from 100%.
diagram_description: Table summarising all quantitative methods, equations used, and typical apparatus/reagents for each element estimation

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Known mass of compound is combusted with excess O₂ and CuO. CO₂ and H₂O products are absorbed in weighed U-tubes (CaCl₂ absorbs water, KOH solution absorbs CO₂). Mass increase of each tube gives moles of C and H. Percentage C = (12 × m₂ × 100) / (44 × m); Percentage H = (2 × m₁ × 100) / (18 × m), where m₂ = mass CO₂, m₁ = mass H₂O, m = sample mass.
diagram_description: Complete combustion apparatus showing sample holder, CuO source, condenser, U-tubes for water and CO₂ with mass measurements

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Dumas method: compound heated with CuO in CO₂ atmosphere yields N₂ gas collected over KOH solution; volume measured at STP and converted to mass. % N = (28 × V × 100) / (22400 × m). Kjeldahl method: compound heated with conc. H₂SO₄ converting N to (NH₄)₂SO₄; addition of NaOH liberates NH₃ absorbed in standard H₂SO₄; excess acid titrated with standard NaOH. % N = (1.4 × M × 2(V − V₁/2)) / m.
diagram_description: Apparatus for Dumas method (combustion tube, N₂ collection over KOH) and Kjeldahl method (digestion flask, distillation, NH₃ absorption and titration)

builds_upon.concept_name: Detection of Nitrogen, Sulphur, Halogens, and Phosphorus
builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Compound heated in a Carius tube (hard glass) with fuming HNO₃ and AgNO₃ in a furnace. Halogen oxidises and forms AgX (AgCl, AgBr, or AgI) precipitate. Precipitate is filtered, washed, dried, and weighed. % X = (atomic mass of X × m₁ × 100) / (molecular mass of AgX × m), where m₁ = mass AgX, m = sample mass.
diagram_description: Carius tube apparatus showing sealed capillary tube containing sample, fuming HNO₃, and AgNO₃; diagram of silver halide precipitation
formula: \text{\% X} = \frac{\text{atomic mass of X} \times m_1 \times 100}{\text{molecular mass of AgX} \times m}

builds_upon.concept_name: Detection of Nitrogen, Sulphur, Halogens, and Phosphorus
builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Sulphur: compound heated in Carius tube with Na₂O₂ or fuming HNO₃; S oxidises to H₂SO₄, precipitated as BaSO₄ with BaCl₂; % S = (32 × m₁ × 100) / (233 × m). Phosphorus: compound oxidised to H₃PO₄, either precipitated as (NH₄)₃PO₄·12MoO₃ or as MgNH₄PO₄ (ignited to Mg₂P₂O₇); % P = (31 × m₁ × 100) / (1877 × m) or (62 × m₁ × 100) / (222 × m).
diagram_description: Carius tube setup for sulphur and phosphorus estimations showing oxidation, precipitation, and weighing steps

builds_upon.concept_name: Detection of Nitrogen, Sulphur, Halogens, and Phosphorus
builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Oxygen percentage is usually determined by difference: % O = 100 − (sum of % of all other elements). Alternatively, compound is decomposed in a stream of N₂; O₂ is passed over red-hot coke to form CO; CO is oxidised to CO₂ by warm I₂O₅ (also produces I₂). CO₂ is weighed. % O = (32 × m₁ × 100) / (88 × m), where m₁ = mass CO₂ produced.
diagram_description: Apparatus showing decomposition in N₂ stream, passage over red coke, I₂O₅ oxidation of CO to CO₂, and CO₂ absorption/weighing

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Physical models aid visualisation of 3D molecular geometry. Framework models show only bonds and pattern of connectivity. Ball-and-stick models represent atoms as balls and bonds as sticks; used for C=C with springs. Space-filling models emphasise relative atomic sizes using van der Waals radii and show molecular volume.
diagram_description: Three molecular models of methane or ethane: framework model (bonds only), ball-and-stick model (spheres for atoms), and space-filling model (overlapping spheres)

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

concept_name: Wöhler's Synthesis and the Vital Force Theory
summary: Before 1828, chemists believed a 'vital force' from living organisms was essential for synthesising organic compounds. Friedrich Wöhler disproved this by synthesising urea (an organic compound) from ammonium cyanate (inorganic): NH₄CNO → NH₂CONH₂. This landmark synthesis, along with Kolbe's acetic acid synthesis (1845) and Berthelot's methane synthesis (1856), established that organic compounds could be synthesised in the laboratory.
diagram_description: Historical reaction equation showing Wöhler's synthesis of urea from ammonium cyanate
subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

concept_name: Common and Trivial Names of Organic Compounds
summary: Before IUPAC nomenclature, organic compounds were named by origin or properties. Citric acid derives from citrus fruits; formic acid from Latin 'formica' (ant). Some traditional names persist because systematic names are lengthy (e.g., Buckminsterfullerene for C₆₀ noting its structural similarity to geodesic domes). Common names remain indispensable in practice and literature despite the existence of systematic alternatives.
diagram_description: Table of common/trivial names vs IUPAC systematic names for compounds like acetic acid, acetone, methane, and toluene

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry
chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

summary: Hydrocarbons are classified into three main categories based on the types of carbon-carbon bonds. Saturated hydrocarbons contain only single bonds; unsaturated hydrocarbons contain double or triple bonds; aromatic hydrocarbons possess special cyclic structures with delocalized electrons. This classification determines their chemical reactivity and physical properties.
diagram_description: Structural formulas showing alkane (single bonds), alkene (double bond), alkyne (triple bond), and benzene ring
subject: chemistry

summary: Saturated hydrocarbons contain only carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen single bonds. Their general formula is C_nH_{2n+2} for open-chain alkanes. They are relatively inert toward acids, bases, and oxidizing agents at normal conditions.
diagram_description: Ball-and-stick models of methane, ethane, propane showing tetrahedral geometry and single bonds
subject: chemistry

summary: Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain at least one carbon-carbon double or triple bond. Alkenes have general formula C_nH_{2n} and alkynes have C_nH_{2n-2}. The presence of pi bonds makes them susceptible to electrophilic addition reactions.
diagram_description: Structural formulas showing ethene (double bond) and ethyne (triple bond) with pi bonding illustrated
subject: chemistry

summary: Aromatic hydrocarbons contain benzene rings with cyclic arrangements of pi electrons. Benzene (C_6H_6) is the parent aromatic compound. Despite having unsaturation, they show exceptional stability and prefer electrophilic substitution over addition reactions.
diagram_description: Benzene ring structure with delocalised pi electrons shown as circle; compare to Kekulé structure
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkanes are systematically named using IUPAC rules based on longest carbon chain and substituent position. Carbon atoms are classified as primary (attached to 1 C), secondary (2 C), tertiary (3 C), or quaternary (4 C) based on the number of carbons bonded to them. All carbons in alkanes are sp³ hybridized.
diagram_description: Structural formula of branched alkane with carbon atoms labeled 1°, 2°, 3°, 4° and IUPAC numbering indicated

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: IUPAC nomenclature selects the longest carbon chain as the parent alkane and numbers from the end giving substituents the lowest positions. Substituent names are listed alphabetically (ignoring prefixes like 'di-' and 'sec-') and assigned position numbers. The suffix '-ane' indicates saturated hydrocarbons.
diagram_description: Example of branched alkane structure with numbering from both directions showing why lowest sum is chosen
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkanes with the same molecular formula can have different structural arrangements, called structural isomers. Chain isomers differ in the carbon skeleton arrangement. Branched isomers have lower boiling points than straight-chain isomers due to reduced intermolecular contact area.
diagram_description: Structural formulas of three isomers of pentane showing linear and branched arrangements

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Carbon atoms in alkanes are classified by the number of other carbon atoms bonded to them: primary (1°) attached to 1 C, secondary (2°) to 2 C, tertiary (3°) to 3 C, and quaternary (4°) to 4 C. Terminal carbon atoms are always primary. This classification affects reactivity in substitution and elimination reactions.
diagram_description: Structural formula showing a branched hydrocarbon with each carbon type labeled and circled
subject: chemistry

summary: All carbon atoms in alkanes are sp³ hybridized, resulting in tetrahedral geometry with 109.5° bond angles. C-C and C-H bonds are formed by head-on overlapping of sp³ orbitals, creating strong sigma bonds with lengths of 154 pm and 112 pm respectively.
diagram_description: Orbital diagram showing sp3 hybrid orbitals on carbon with tetrahedral arrangement; space-filling model of methane

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Alkenes and alkynes add dihydrogen gas in the presence of finely divided Pt, Pd, or Ni catalysts to form alkanes. Platinum and palladium catalyse the reaction at room temperature; nickel requires higher temperature and pressure. This process is called hydrogenation.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing ethene + H2 → ethane using Pt/Pd/Ni catalyst
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkyl halides (except fluorides) are reduced to alkanes by treatment with Zn metal and dilute HCl. The halogen is replaced by hydrogen, producing the corresponding alkane and HX. This method is selective for C-X bonds.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing R-X + Zn/HCl → R-H + ZnX2
subject: chemistry

summary: The Wurtz reaction couples two alkyl halide molecules using sodium metal in dry ether to produce higher alkanes with an even number of carbons. Two identical alkyl halides are coupled C-C, with Na providing the reducing and coupling agent.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing 2 R-Br + 2Na in dry ether → R-R + 2NaBr
subject: chemistry

summary: Sodium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime (NaOH + CaO mixture) undergo decarboxylation, eliminating CO₂ to yield alkanes containing one fewer carbon atom. The reaction cleaves the carboxyl group, forming the parent alkane.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing R-COONa + NaOH/CaO heat → R-H + Na2CO3
subject: chemistry

summary: Aqueous solutions of sodium or potassium salts of carboxylic acids undergo electrolysis to produce alkanes with an even number of carbons. At the anode, carboxylate ions are oxidized to free radicals which couple, liberating CO₂. Methane cannot be prepared by this method.
diagram_description: Electrolysis apparatus showing anode and cathode reactions producing alkane at anode
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkanes show limited reactivity due to strong C-C and C-H bonds and non-polar character. Key reactions include free radical substitution with halogens, complete combustion, controlled partial oxidation, and industrial isomerization and aromatization. Physical properties follow trends with molecular size.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing major reaction pathways: halogenation, combustion, oxidation, isomerization
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkanes are non-polar molecules held together by weak van der Waals forces. The first four (C₁-C₄) are gases, C₅-C₁₇ are liquids, and C₁₈+ are solids at 298 K. Boiling points increase regularly with molecular mass due to increased surface area and intermolecular forces. Alkanes are hydrophobic and insoluble in water.
diagram_description: Graph showing boiling point trend vs. number of carbons for alkanes; bar chart of state vs. carbon number
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkane halogenation with F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, or I₂ proceeds via free radical chain mechanism with initiation, propagation, and termination steps. Initiation involves homolytic cleavage of X-X bonds. Reactivity order is F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂; H replacement follows 3° > 2° > 1°. Multiple substitution products form.
diagram_description: Free radical chain mechanism showing initiation step (homolysis), propagation steps, and termination pathways

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Alkanes combust in air or O₂ with heat evolution, producing CO₂ and H₂O. Complete combustion requires sufficient oxygen; incomplete combustion with limited O₂ produces carbon black (soot) used in ink and pigments. The general equation is C_nH_{2n+2} + ((3n+1)/2)O₂ → nCO₂ + (n+1)H₂O.
diagram_description: Balanced combustion equation showing reactants and products; comparison of complete vs. incomplete combustion
subject: chemistry

summary: With regulated O₂ and suitable catalysts at high pressure, alkanes undergo controlled oxidation to alcohols, aldehydes, or carboxylic acids. Tertiary H atoms can be oxidized by KMnO₄. Examples: methane → methanol (Cu catalyst), ethane → ethanoic acid (Mn acetate), 2-methylpropane → 2-methylpropan-2-ol.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing selective oxidation products from different alkane substrates and catalyst systems
subject: chemistry

summary: n-Alkanes on heating with anhydrous AlCl3 and HCl gas isomerize to branched-chain alkanes. Alkanes with 6+ carbons at 773 K with V/Mo/Cr oxides on alumina undergo dehydrogenation and cyclization to benzene and its homologues. This industrial process is called reforming.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing n-hexane isomerizing to 2-methylpentane; n-heptane aromatizing to toluene
subject: chemistry

summary: Higher alkanes at high temperature decompose into smaller alkanes and alkenes via free radical reactions. Dodecane at 973 K over Pt/Pd/Ni yields heptane and pentene. This cracking process is used industrially to produce lighter hydrocarbons and alkenes from heavier fractions.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing decomposition of dodecane into multiple smaller hydrocarbons at high temperature
subject: chemistry

summary: Free rotation about C-C sigma bonds produces infinite conformations. In ethane, the staggered conformation is most stable (minimum torsional strain); the eclipsed is least stable (maximum repulsion). An energy barrier of ~12.5 kJ/mol is easily overcome at room temperature, so conformers rapidly interconvert.
diagram_description: Newman projections and sawhorse formulas showing eclipsed and staggered conformers of ethane side-by-side
subject: chemistry

summary: Ethane exhibits infinite conformations via C-C rotation. Eclipsed conformation has H atoms closest (maximum repulsion); staggered has H atoms farthest apart (minimum repulsion). Intermediate skew conformations exist between these extremes. All bond angles and lengths remain 109.5° and 154 pm.
diagram_description: Three-dimensional ball-and-stick models of ethane showing rotation from eclipsed → staggered → eclipsed
subject: chemistry

summary: Newman projection views the molecule along the C-C bond; front carbon is shown as a point, rear as a circle. Sawhorse projection tilts the C-C bond at an angle. Both methods depict dihedral (torsional) angles and spatial relationships of substituents. Dihedral angle varies from 0° (eclipsed) to 180° (staggered).
diagram_description: Ethane drawn in both Newman and sawhorse formats showing eclipsed and staggered conformations
subject: chemistry

summary: Torsional strain arises from weak repulsive interactions between electron clouds of adjacent bonds. Energy is minimum at dihedral angles of 180° (staggered) and maximum at 0° (eclipsed). The energy barrier for C-C rotation in ethane is ~12.5 kJ/mol, easily overcome at ordinary temperatures.
diagram_description: Energy diagram (y-axis: energy, x-axis: dihedral angle) showing minima at 180° and maxima at 0°, 120°, 240°
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Structural and Geometrical Isomerism in Alkenes
Addition of Halogens and Hydrogen Halides
Addition of Water and Sulphuric Acid
summary: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons with C=C double bonds; general formula C_nH_{2n}. The double bond comprises one sigma (σ) and one pi (π) bond. The weaker π bond makes alkenes susceptible to electrophilic addition. Alkenes are also called olefins.
diagram_description: Ethene structure showing sp² hybridization, sigma and pi bond formation, and orbital overlap

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: The C=C double bond consists of one strong σ bond (397 kJ/mol, head-on sp² overlap) and one weak π bond (284 kJ/mol, lateral 2p overlap). Total bond enthalpy is 681 kJ/mol. Double bond length (134 pm) is shorter than single bond (154 pm). The π bond's weakness makes alkenes reactive toward electrophiles.
diagram_description: Orbital diagram showing sp² orbitals forming sigma bonds and p orbitals forming pi bond in ethene

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Alkene nomenclature uses the suffix '-ene' replacing 'ane'. The longest chain containing the double bond is selected as the parent. Numbering starts from the end nearer the double bond to give it the lowest position number. Position of the double bond is indicated before 'ene' (e.g., but-2-ene).
diagram_description: Examples of alkene structures with IUPAC names showing position numbering and double bond location
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Structural and Geometrical Isomerism in Alkenes
summary: Structural isomerism in alkenes includes chain and position isomers. Geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism arises from restricted rotation about the C=C bond. Cis isomers have similar groups on the same side (more polar); trans has them opposite (less polar). Both must have different groups on each carbon.
diagram_description: Structural isomers of butene (but-1-ene, but-2-ene, 2-methylprop-1-ene); cis/trans isomers of but-2-ene

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Alkenes are prepared by: (1) partial reduction of alkynes with H₂ using Lindlar's catalyst (giving cis alkenes) or Na/NH₃ (trans); (2) dehydrohalogenation of alkyl halides with alcoholic KOH (β-elimination); (3) dehydration of alcohols with conc. H₂SO₄; (4) dehalogenation of vicinal dihalides with Zn.
diagram_description: Four pathways to alkenes with starting materials, reagents, and products shown
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkenes add H₂ in the presence of finely divided Pt, Pd, or Ni catalysts to form alkanes. Reactivity: Pt ≈ Pd (room temp) > Ni (higher T/P). This is a classic addition reaction and the reverse of elimination.
diagram_description: Reaction equation showing propene + H2 → propane with Pt/Pd/Ni catalyst
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Addition of Halogens and Hydrogen Halides
summary: Br₂ and Cl₂ add to alkenes forming vicinal dihalides; I₂ does not under normal conditions. HCl, HBr, and HI add to form alkyl halides via electrophilic mechanism. Bromine solution decolorization is a test for unsaturation. Reactivity order: HI > HBr > HCl.
diagram_description: Reaction schemes showing Br2 addition to ethene and HBr addition to propene
subject: chemistry

summary: Markovnikov rule states that HX adds to unsymmetrical alkenes with H to the carbon bearing more H (giving the more stable carbocation). In presence of peroxides, HBr undergoes anti-Markovnikov addition via free radical mechanism, giving opposite regiochemistry. Peroxide effect occurs only with HBr (not HCl or HI).
diagram_description: Propene with HBr showing two products: Markovnikov (2-bromopropane, major) and anti-Markovnikov (1-bromopropane)

builds_upon.concept_name: Addition of Halogens and Hydrogen Halides

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Addition of Water and Sulphuric Acid
summary: Alkenes add water in the presence of dilute or concentrated H₂SO₄ to form alcohols (hydration) or alkyl hydrogen sulphates, following Markovnikov rule. Concentrated H₂SO₄ adds first to form alkyl hydrogen sulphate, which is then hydrolyzed to alcohol on dilution.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing propene + H2O with dilute H2SO4 catalyst → 2-propanol; cold H2SO4 forming alkyl hydrogen sulphate
subject: chemistry

summary: Cold dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent) oxidizes alkenes to vicinal glycols with MnO₂ formation (test for unsaturation). Hot/acidic KMnO₄ cleaves C=C to carbonyl compounds. Ozonolysis adds O₃ forming ozonides, then Zn-H₂O cleaves to aldehydes/ketones. Ozonolysis determines double bond position.
diagram_description: Reactions showing KMnO4 glycol formation, acidic permanganate cleavage, and ozonolysis with Zn workup
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkenes undergo polymerization at high temperature, high pressure, and with catalysts to form long-chain polymers. Ethene forms polythene (—CH₂-CH₂—)ₙ; propene forms polypropene with methyl branches. Polymers are used for plastic bags, bottles, and industrial products.
diagram_description: Polymerization reaction showing n moles ethene → polythene chain; structural formula of polypropene
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Addition of Halogens and Hydrogen Halides
summary: Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons with C≡C triple bonds; general formula C_nH_{2n-2}. Ethyne (C₂H₂, acetylene) is the first stable member. The triple bond comprises one σ and two π bonds. Alkynes are linear and more unsaturated than alkenes, showing greater reactivity.
diagram_description: Ethyne structure showing sp hybridization, triple bond (σ + 2π), and linear geometry (H-C≡C-H at 180°)

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: The C≡C triple bond consists of one σ bond (head-on sp overlap) and two π bonds (lateral p orbital overlap). Total bond enthalpy is 823 kJ/mol. Triple bond length (120 pm) is shorter than double (133 pm) and single (154 pm). Linear geometry with 180° H-C≡C-H angles.
diagram_description: Orbital diagram showing sp hybridized carbon atoms with sigma bond and two perpendicular pi bonds; linear structure

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Alkynes are named with the suffix '-yne' replacing 'ane'. The longest chain containing the triple bond is selected. Numbering starts from the end giving the triple bond the lowest position. In common nomenclature, alkynes are named as derivatives of acetylene (e.g., methylacetylene for propyne).
diagram_description: Examples of alkyne structures with IUPAC and common names showing position numbering
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkynes show structural isomerism. Position isomers differ in triple bond location (but-1-yne vs. but-2-yne). Chain isomers have different carbon skeletons (hex-1-yne vs. 3-methylpent-1-yne). Unlike alkenes, alkynes cannot show geometrical isomerism.
diagram_description: Structural isomers of hexyne showing position isomers and chain isomers with IUPAC names
subject: chemistry

summary: Terminal alkynes (with H on sp-hybridized carbon) are weakly acidic and react with strong bases (Na, NaH, NaNH₂) to form acetylides/alkynides. The H is acidic due to maximum s-character (50%) in sp orbitals, making C-H electrons more electronegative. Internal alkynes (R-C≡C-R) show no such reaction.
diagram_description: Reaction equations showing ethyne and propyne reacting with Na and NaNH2 to form disodium and monosodium alkynides
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkynes are prepared from vicinal dihalides: (1) treatment with alcoholic KOH causes elimination of HX to form alkenyl halide; (2) treatment of alkenyl halide with sodamide (NaNH₂) causes second elimination to yield alkyne. The two-step process ensures complete dehydrogenation.
diagram_description: Two-step synthesis pathway from vicinal dibromide to alkyne via alkenyl halide intermediate
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkynes add two moles of H₂. Lindlar's catalyst (Pd/C partially deactivated with poisons) gives cis alkenes; Na/liquid NH₃ gives trans alkenes. Complete hydrogenation with Pt/Pd/Ni yields alkanes. These selective reductions allow synthesis of specific alkene stereoisomers.
diagram_description: Alkyne hydrogenation showing different stereoisomeric products with Lindlar vs. Na/NH3 conditions
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Addition of Halogens and Hydrogen Halides
summary: Br₂ adds to alkynes forming first dibromoalkene, then tetrabromoalkane. HCl, HBr, HI add sequentially; second molecule adds to formed alkene (Markovnikov). Two HX molecules yield gem dihalides (both halogens on same carbon). Reactivity order: HI > HBr > HCl.
diagram_description: Alkyne with Br2 addition (1:2 ratio) and successive HBr additions showing gem dibromide formation
subject: chemistry

summary: One mole of water adds to alkynes with Hg²⁺/H⁺ at 333 K to form unstable enols, which tautomerize to carbonyls. Ethyne yields ethanal; propyne yields propanone. This is the major route to carbonyl compounds from alkynes.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing alkyne + H2O forming enol intermediate → tautomerization → ketone/aldehyde
subject: chemistry

summary: Ethyne undergoes linear polymerization under suitable conditions to form polyacetylene (—CH=CH—)ₙ, a conjugated polyene with conductive properties. Thin films can be used as electrodes in batteries. Polyacetylene is lighter and cheaper than metal conductors.
diagram_description: Polymerization reaction showing n ethyne molecules forming polyacetylene with repeating C=C units
subject: chemistry

summary: Three ethyne molecules polymerize via cyclic mechanism when passed through a red-hot iron tube at 873 K to form benzene. This is the best aliphatic-to-aromatic conversion route and provides easy access to aromatic chemistry and benzene derivatives.
diagram_description: Schematic showing three ethyne molecules arranged and condensed into benzene ring structure
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene (C₆H₆) is a planar cyclic compound with six sp² carbons and exceptional stability due to delocalized π electrons. Originally depicted with alternating single-double bonds (Kekulé), it is better represented as a resonance hybrid with a continuous electron cloud. All C-C bonds (139 pm) are equivalent and intermediate between single and double bond lengths.
diagram_description: Kekulé structure (alternating bonds) ↔ Resonance hybrid; circle-in-hexagon representation showing delocalized pi electrons

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Kekulé proposed benzene contains alternating single and double C-C bonds. The concept of oscillating/alternating bonds is explained by resonance theory: benzene is a hybrid of two equivalent resonance structures. The hybrid structure is more stable than either individual structure, explaining benzene's unexpected stability and unusual chemical properties.
diagram_description: Two Kekulé structures (1,3,5-double bonds vs. 2,4,6-double bonds) with double-headed arrow; hybrid structure with delocalized electrons
subject: chemistry

summary: All six carbons in benzene are sp² hybridized. Two sp² orbitals on each carbon form C-C and C-H sigma bonds in the molecular plane; the third sp² orbital is perpendicular to the plane. Six unhybridized p orbitals overlap laterally to form delocalized π electron cloud, creating two doughnuts (π clouds above and below the ring).
diagram_description: Orbital diagram showing sp2 orbitals forming sigma skeleton and p orbitals forming delocalized pi electron clouds
subject: chemistry

summary: A system is aromatic if it satisfies: (1) planarity; (2) complete delocalization of π electrons; (3) presence of (4n+2) π electrons (Hückel rule, n=0,1,2,...). Benzene has 6 electrons (4×1+2), cyclopentadienyl anion has 6, naphthalene has 10. Aromaticity explains exceptional stability and reactivity preference.
diagram_description: Table of aromatic systems showing electron counts and (4n+2) compliance; structures of benzene, cyclopentadienyl anion, naphthalene
subject: chemistry

summary: Aromatic character extends beyond benzene. Cyclopentadienyl anion (5 atoms, 6 π electrons) and tropylium cation (7 atoms, 6 π electrons) are aromatic. Naphthalene (10 π electrons), anthracene, and other fused-ring systems show aromaticity. Non-benzenoid aromatics lack a benzene ring but follow Hückel rule.
diagram_description: Structures of cyclopentadienyl anion, tropylium cation, naphthalene, and anthracene with pi electron counts
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene is prepared industrially by isolation from coal tar. Laboratory preparations include: (1) cyclic polymerization of three ethyne molecules at 873 K over red-hot iron; (2) decarboxylation of benzoic acid sodium salt with soda lime; (3) reduction of phenol vapor over heated zinc dust.
diagram_description: Three synthesis routes to benzene: trimerization of ethyne, decarboxylation, and reduction of phenol
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene undergoes electrophilic substitution via three-step mechanism: (1) generation of electrophile (NO₂⁺, Cl⁺, R⁺, RCO⁺); (2) formation of sigma complex/arenium ion; (3) proton removal to restore aromaticity. Major reactions: nitration (HNO₃/H₂SO₄), halogenation (Cl₂/Br₂/FeCl₃), sulphonation (H₂SO₄), Friedel-Crafts alkylation/acylation (RX or RCOX/AlCl₃).
diagram_description: General mechanism showing electrophile generation, sigma complex formation, and proton removal; examples of each reaction

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene normally resists addition due to aromaticity. Under vigorous conditions: (1) with Ni catalyst, high T/P, it forms cyclohexane; (2) with Cl₂/UV light at 500 K, it forms benzene hexachloride (BHC, gammaxane). These additions require disruption of the stable aromatic pi system.
diagram_description: Addition reactions showing benzene + 3Cl2/UV → benzene hexachloride; benzene + 3H2/Ni → cyclohexane
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene burns in air with a sooty flame (producing carbon black), forming CO₂ and H₂O: C₆H₆ + 15/2 O₂ → 6CO₂ + 3H₂O. The sooty nature reflects incomplete combustion due to aromatic ring instability.
diagram_description: Balanced combustion equation for benzene showing stoichiometry
subject: chemistry

summary: Substituents on benzene direct incoming electrophiles to specific positions. Electron-donating groups (—OH, —NH₂, —CH₃) are ortho/para directors and activate the ring. Electron-withdrawing groups (—NO₂, —CN, —COOH) are meta directors and deactivate the ring. Halogens are unusual: weakly deactivating but ortho/para directing.
diagram_description: Comparison table of activating (ortho/para) and deactivating (meta) substituents with resonance structures showing electron density
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene and polynuclear aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH) with 2+ fused benzene rings are toxic and carcinogenic. Examples: benzanthracene, benzo[a]pyrene, dibenzanthracene. Formed in incomplete combustion of tobacco, coal, petroleum. PAHs enter the body, undergo biochemical reactions, damage DNA, and cause cancer.
diagram_description: Structures of carcinogenic PAH compounds (benzanthracene, benzo[a]pyrene, dibenzanthracene); flow showing incomplete combustion → PAH → DNA damage → cancer
subject: chemistry

summary: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures of two or more components classified by the physical states of solute and solvent. Solutions can be gaseous (gas in gas), liquid (gas/liquid/solid in liquid), or solid (gas/liquid/solid in solid). In binary solutions, the major component is the solvent and minor component(s) are the solute(s). The solvent determines the physical state of the solution.
diagram_description: Table showing three rows (gaseous, liquid, solid solutions) with solute and solvent states and examples
subject: chemistry

summary: Gaseous solutions contain gas as solvent and can dissolve other gases, liquids, or solids. Examples include mixtures of oxygen and nitrogen gases, chloroform mixed with nitrogen gas, and camphor vapour in nitrogen gas.
subject: chemistry

summary: Liquid solutions have a liquid solvent and can contain gaseous, liquid, or solid solutes. Examples include oxygen dissolved in water, ethanol dissolved in water, and glucose dissolved in water. Most biological and industrial processes occur in liquid solutions.
subject: chemistry

summary: Solid solutions have a solid solvent and can contain gaseous, liquid, or solid solutes. Examples include hydrogen dissolved in palladium, mercury amalgam with sodium, and copper dissolved in gold. These form homogeneous crystalline structures.
subject: chemistry

summary: Solution concentration expresses the amount of solute in a given quantity of solvent or solution. Multiple units exist (mass %, volume %, ppm, mole fraction, molarity, molality) each with specific applications. Some are temperature-dependent (molarity) while others are temperature-independent (molality, mole fraction).
diagram_description: Table comparing all concentration units with formulas and example values
subject: chemistry

summary: Mass percentage expresses solute mass as a percentage of total solution mass. Calculated as (mass of component / total solution mass) × 100. Commonly used in industrial applications (e.g., commercial bleaching solution is 3.62% sodium hypochlorite by mass).
formula: ext{Mass \%} = \frac{\text{mass of component}}{\text{total mass of solution}} \times 100
subject: chemistry

summary: Volume percentage expresses solute volume as a percentage of total solution volume. Calculated as (volume of component / total solution volume) × 100. Used for solutions containing liquids (e.g., 35% ethylene glycol antifreeze lowers water freezing point to 255.4 K).
formula: ext{Volume \%} = \frac{\text{volume of component}}{\text{total volume of solution}} \times 100
subject: chemistry

summary: Mass by volume percentage expresses solute mass in grams per 100 mL of solution. Commonly used in medicine and pharmacy for liquid preparations. Useful for prescribing drugs and medical solutions with precise dosing.
subject: chemistry

summary: Parts per million (ppm) expresses solute concentration in trace amounts. Calculated as (number of parts of component / total parts) × 10⁶. Used for pollutants and trace elements (e.g., 1 ppm fluoride prevents tooth decay, 1.5 ppm causes mottling, dissolved oxygen in seawater is ~5.8 ppm).
formula: ext{ppm} = \frac{\text{number of parts of component}}{\text{total parts}} \times 10^6
subject: chemistry

summary: Mole fraction (x) is the ratio of moles of a component to total moles of all components. For a binary mixture: x_A = n_A/(n_A + n_B). Sum of all mole fractions equals 1. Temperature-independent and useful for relating vapour pressure and gas mixture calculations.
subject: chemistry

summary: Molarity (M) is moles of solute per litre of solution. Calculated as M = moles of solute / volume in litres. Temperature-dependent because volume changes with temperature. Example: 0.25 M NaOH contains 0.25 mol NaOH in 1 litre of solution.
subject: chemistry

summary: Molality (m) is moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. Calculated as m = moles of solute / mass of solvent (kg). Temperature-independent, making it ideal for colligative property calculations. Example: 1.00 m KCl contains 1 mol KCl dissolved in 1 kg water.
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Solubility and Effect of Temperature on Solids
Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
summary: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a specified amount of solvent at given temperature and pressure. Dissolution and crystallisation occur simultaneously until dynamic equilibrium is reached. Solutions are classified as saturated (maximum solute) or unsaturated (more solute can dissolve).
diagram_description: Equilibrium diagram showing dissolution and crystallisation arrows reaching saturation point
subject: chemistry

summary: Solubility of solids depends on nature of solute and solvent following the principle 'like dissolves like'. Polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents while non-polar solutes dissolve in non-polar solvents. Temperature increases solubility for endothermic dissolution but decreases it for exothermic dissolution. Pressure has negligible effect on solid solubility.
diagram_description: Graph showing solubility curves for KNO₃ and NaCl increasing with temperature
subject: chemistry

summary: Gas solubility increases with increasing pressure and decreases with increasing temperature. When gas pressure increases, more gas molecules dissolve due to increased collision rate with liquid surface. Higher temperature provides energy for dissolved gas molecules to escape, reducing solubility. This explains why aquatic species prefer cold water.
diagram_description: Diagram showing gas molecule collisions with liquid surface and effect of pressure/temperature
subject: chemistry

summary: Henry's law states that at constant temperature, solubility of a gas in liquid is directly proportional to partial pressure: p = KH·x. The Henry's law constant KH depends on gas nature and temperature. Higher KH means lower solubility at given pressure. Applications include carbonating beverages, scuba diving, and high-altitude effects on blood oxygen.
diagram_description: Graph showing linear relationship between partial pressure and mole fraction of dissolved gas
subject: chemistry

summary: At dynamic equilibrium, dissolution and crystallisation occur at equal rates. Solute + Solvent ⇌ Solution. The concentration remains constant at given temperature and pressure. This equilibrium depends on Le Chatelier's Principle—endothermic dissolution increases with temperature while exothermic dissolution decreases with temperature.
subject: chemistry

summary: Vapour pressure of a solution containing non-volatile solute is lower than pure solvent vapour pressure. Raoult's law relates vapour pressure to mole fraction for volatile components. Solutions obeying Raoult's over entire concentration range are ideal. Deviations lead to positive/negative deviations and azeotrope formation.
diagram_description: Diagram showing vapour molecules escaping from pure solvent vs solution surface
subject: chemistry

summary: Raoult's law states partial vapour pressure of each component equals its pure component vapour pressure times mole fraction: p₁ = x₁p₁⁰. Total pressure is sum of partial pressures (Dalton's law). Applies to ideal solutions and volatile liquids in equilibrium. Relative lowering equals mole fraction of non-volatile solute.
diagram_description: Graph showing linear relationship between partial pressure and mole fraction for ideal solutions
subject: chemistry

summary: Ideal solutions obey Raoult's law over entire concentration range with ΔmixH = 0 and ΔmixV = 0 (no heat or volume change on mixing). Intermolecular forces A-A and B-B are equal to A-B interactions. Examples: n-hexane/n-heptane, benzene/toluene. Perfectly ideal solutions are rare but some are nearly ideal.
diagram_description: Molecular diagram showing equal intermolecular attractive forces between all molecule pairs
subject: chemistry

summary: Non-ideal solutions show vapour pressure higher (positive deviation) or lower (negative deviation) than predicted by Raoult's law. Positive deviation: A-B interactions weaker than A-A and B-B (e.g., ethanol-acetone). Negative deviation: A-B interactions stronger than pure interactions (e.g., chloroform-acetone hydrogen bonding). Large deviations form azeotropes.
diagram_description: Two graphs showing positive and negative deviations from ideal Raoult's law line
subject: chemistry

summary: Azeotropes are binary mixtures where liquid and vapour have same composition, boiling at constant temperature. Minimum boiling azeotropes form from large positive deviations (e.g., ethanol-water ~95% ethanol). Maximum boiling azeotropes form from large negative deviations (e.g., HNO₃-water 68% HNO₃). Cannot be separated by fractional distillation.
diagram_description: Two graphs showing minimum and maximum boiling azeotrope points on vapour pressure curves
subject: chemistry

summary: Colligative properties depend only on number of solute particles, not their identity. Four colligative properties: relative lowering of vapour pressure, elevation of boiling point, depression of freezing point, and osmotic pressure. All relate to reduced effective concentration of solvent due to presence of non-volatile solute particles.
diagram_description: Comparison diagram showing all four colligative properties and their measurement
subject: chemistry
