diagram_description: Flow diagram showing gold extraction via cyanide complex formation and decomposition; nickel purification through Ni(CO)4 formation and thermal decomposition
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Coordination Compounds In Medicine And Chelate Therapy
summary: Chelate therapy uses chelating ligands to remove toxic metal ions: EDTA treats lead poisoning; D-penicillamine removes excess copper/iron. Platinum complexes (cis-platin) are effective anticancer agents that inhibit tumour growth. Cis-platin is selective because cis geometry enables specific DNA interactions. Photography uses [Ag(S2O3)2]³− to fix undeveloped silver bromide.
diagram_description: Structural formula of cis-platin (cis-[Pt(NH3)2Cl2]) and examples showing chelation of Pb2+ by EDTA, Fe3+ by desferrioxime
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Limitations Of Valence Bond And Crystal Field Theories
summary: VBT assumes hybridisation exists (mathematical construct only) and cannot quantify magnetic data or explain colour. CFT assumes ligands are point charges, predicting incorrectly that anionic ligands should dominate the spectrochemical series, and ignores covalent bonding. Neither theory fully explains d⁴−d⁵ ion complications. Ligand Field Theory (LFT) and Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT) address these limitations.
diagram_description: Comparison table showing specific failures of VBT and CFT with examples and limitations

builds_upon.concept_name: Valence Bond Theory Of Coordination Compounds
Crystal Field Theory Of Coordination Compounds

subject: chemistry

summary: Double salts (e.g. KCl·MgCl2·6H2O, FeSO4·(NH4)2SO4·6H2O) completely dissociate into simple ions when dissolved in water, losing salt status and behaving as separate compounds. Coordination complexes (e.g. K4[Fe(CN)6]) do NOT dissociate; complex ion [Fe(CN)6]⁴− remains intact, maintaining colour and properties even in solution.
diagram_description: Comparison table showing dissociation behaviour of carnallite (double salt) vs. K4[Fe(CN)6] (complex) in aqueous solution
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Colour Of Gemstones From Coordination Chemistry
summary: Ruby (Al2O3 with ~1% Cr³⁺) appears red; chromium ions occupy octahedral Al³⁺ sites, and d-d transitions give red colour. Emerald (Be3Al2Si6O18 with Cr³⁺) appears green because absorption bands shift to longer wavelengths. Same d-d transitions in different crystal field environments produce different colours, illustrating how coordination geometry and ligand strength determine gemstone hues.
diagram_description: Photographs of ruby and emerald gemstones with molecular level explanations of Cr3+ coordination and resulting colour absorption patterns

builds_upon.concept_name: Crystal Field Effects On Colour And Magnetism

subject: chemistry

summary: Halogenated organic compounds are classified by the nature of carbon bearing the halogen: haloalkanes (alkyl halides) have halogen on sp³ carbon; haloarenes (aryl halides) have halogen on sp² aromatic carbon. Polyhalogen compounds contain multiple halogen atoms. Classification also considers primary, secondary, and tertiary carbon atoms.
diagram_description: Structural comparison of primary, secondary, tertiary alkyl halides and aryl halides
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloalkanes (alkyl halides) are compounds where halogen is bonded to sp³-hybridised carbon of an alkyl group. They follow the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₁X and are classified as primary (1°), secondary (2°), or tertiary (3°) based on the carbon atom bearing the halogen.
diagram_description: Structures showing primary, secondary, and tertiary alkyl halides
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloarenes (aryl halides) are compounds where halogen is bonded directly to sp²-hybridised carbon of an aromatic ring. They are much less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution than haloalkanes due to resonance stabilisation and aromatic character.
diagram_description: Structures of chlorobenzene and disubstituted haloarenes (ortho, meta, para)
subject: chemistry

summary: Polyhalogen compounds contain multiple halogen atoms on a carbon skeleton. Geminal (gem) dihalides have both halogens on the same carbon; vicinal (vic) dihalides have them on adjacent carbons. Common examples include dichloromethane, chloroform, and carbon tetrachloride.
diagram_description: Structures of geminal and vicinal dihalides
subject: chemistry

summary: IUPAC nomenclature treats halides as halosubstituted hydrocarbons, using 'halo' prefixes (chloro, bromo, iodo, fluoro). Common nomenclature names the alkyl group followed by the halide name. Mono haloarenes use the same IUPAC and common names, while dihaloarenes use ortho (1,2), meta (1,3), para (1,4) positions.
diagram_description: Table comparing IUPAC and common names of haloalkanes

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: IUPAC names haloalkanes as halosubstituted alkanes, using prefixes chloro-, bromo-, iodo-, fluoro- followed by the parent alkane name. The longest carbon chain containing the halogen is selected; the position is numbered to give the halogen the lowest number.
diagram_description: Examples of IUPAC naming for 1-bromopropane, 2-chlorobutane, 1-chloro-2-methylpropane

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Common names are derived by naming the alkyl group followed by the halide name and classification (primary, secondary, tertiary). Examples: n-propyl fluoride, sec-butyl chloride, tert-butyl bromide. Special names include benzyl chloride, vinyl chloride, allyl bromide.
diagram_description: Examples of common names: n-propyl fluoride, sec-butyl chloride, tert-butyl bromide
subject: chemistry

summary: Monohaloarenes use common and IUPAC names interchangeably (e.g. chlorobenzene). Dihaloarenes are named using IUPAC numerals 1,2; 1,3; 1,4 for ortho, meta, para positions respectively, replacing older common prefixes o-, m-, p-.
diagram_description: Structures of mono- and disubstituted haloarenes with ortho, meta, para positions labeled

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: The C—X bond in haloalkanes is polarised due to halogen electronegativity, with carbon bearing δ+ and halogen bearing δ−. Bond length increases from C—F to C—I; bond enthalpy decreases in the same order. This polarity drives nucleophilic substitution reactions.
diagram_description: Table showing C—X bond lengths, bond enthalpies, and dipole moments for all halogens
subject: chemistry

summary: Halogen atoms are more electronegative than carbon, causing the C—X bond to be polarised with carbon bearing a partial positive charge (δ+) and halogen bearing a partial negative charge (δ−). This polarity enables nucleophilic attack and is responsible for chemical reactivity.
diagram_description: Polarised C—X bond with partial charges and dipole arrow shown
subject: chemistry

summary: C—X bond length increases from C—F (139 pm) to C—I (214 pm) as halogen size increases. Bond enthalpy decreases in the same order (C—F: 452 kJ/mol to C—I: 234 kJ/mol). This trend reflects the weaker bonding with larger halogen atoms.
diagram_description: Graph or table showing bond length and bond enthalpy trends across C—F, C—Cl, C—Br, C—I
subject: chemistry

summary: For a given alkyl group, the reactivity of alkyl halides follows: R—I > R—Br > R—Cl >> R—F in both SN1 and SN2 reactions. This order reflects the stability of the halide as a leaving group, with iodide being the best leaving group due to its large size and weak C—I bond.
diagram_description: Reactivity order of alkyl halides with leaving group ability bar chart
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloalkanes are prepared from alcohols using HX, phosphorus halides, or thionyl chloride; from alkanes by free radical halogenation; from alkenes by HX or X₂ addition; and by halogen exchange with metallic fluorides. Each method offers different advantages for specific structural requirements.
diagram_description: Reaction pathways showing multiple routes to haloalkanes from alcohols, alkanes, and alkenes
subject: chemistry

summary: The —OH group of alcohols is replaced by halogen using concentrated halogen acids (with ZnCl₂ catalyst for 1° and 2° alcohols), phosphorus halides (PX₃, PX₅), or thionyl chloride. The latter is preferred as SO₂ and HCl gases escape, yielding pure alkyl halides. Reactivity order: 3° > 2° > 1°.
diagram_description: Reaction schemes showing conversion of alcohols to alkyl halides using different reagents
subject: chemistry

summary: Free radical chlorination or bromination of alkanes in the presence of UV light or heat produces a complex mixture of isomeric mono- and polyhaloalkanes, making isolation of pure compounds difficult. Multiple products form due to random hydrogen abstraction at different positions.
diagram_description: Free radical halogenation mechanism showing initiation, propagation, and termination steps with multiple products
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkenes react with HX (HCl, HBr, HI) to form alkyl halides following Markovnikov's rule. Halogens (Br₂, Cl₂) add to alkenes to form vic-dibromides/dichlorides. Bromine in CCl₄ test detects C=C bonds by decolourization. HX follows Markovnikov; dihalogen gives anti addition.
diagram_description: Reactions showing HX addition (Markovnikov) and X₂ addition to alkenes, with stereochemistry

builds_upon.concept_name: Addition of Halogens and Hydrogen Halides

subject: chemistry

summary: Finkelstein reaction: alkyl chlorides/bromides react with NaI in dry acetone to form alkyl iodides (NaCl/NaBr precipitate drives reaction forward). Swarts reaction: alkyl chlorides/bromides heated with metallic fluorides (AgF, CoF₂) yield alkyl fluorides. Both exploit insolubility or thermodynamic driving forces.
diagram_description: Finkelstein and Swarts reaction mechanisms showing reagent displacement and product formation
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene reacts with Cl₂ or Br₂ in the presence of Fe or FeCl₃ catalyst to form chlorobenzene or bromobenzene. Iodination requires an oxidising agent (HNO₃, HIO₄) to drive the equilibrium forward. Fluorine is too reactive and not used.
diagram_description: Electrophilic substitution of benzene with Cl₂/Fe, Br₂/Fe showing mechanism and products
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary aromatic amines (aniline) are converted to aryl halides via diazonium salt intermediate. Amine is treated with NaNO₂/HX in cold aqueous acid to form ArN₂⁺X⁻, then reacted with Cu(I)X to displace N₂ and form Ar—X. For iodine, KI alone suffices without Cu(I).
diagram_description: Sandmeyer reaction mechanism showing aniline to diazonium salt to aryl halide
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Haloalkanes and haloarenes are colourless when pure but bromides/iodides develop colour on light exposure. Many are volatile with sweet odour. They have higher boiling points than corresponding hydrocarbons due to dipole-dipole and van der Waals forces. They are slightly soluble in water but completely soluble in organic solvents.
diagram_description: Comparison table of boiling points, solubility, and density for various haloalkanes
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Boiling points of haloalkanes are significantly higher than corresponding hydrocarbons due to polar C—X bonds and increased van der Waals forces. For the same alkyl group: R—I > R—Br > R—Cl > R—F. Branching decreases boiling point; para-disubstituted benzenes have higher melting points due to better crystal packing symmetry.
diagram_description: Graph showing boiling point trends across alkyl halides (CH₃X, C₂H₅X, C₃H₇X) vs halogen type
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Haloalkanes are very slightly soluble in water because energy required to break hydrogen bonds in water exceeds energy released forming weak interactions with haloalkanes. They are completely soluble in organic solvents due to similar intermolecular forces (dipole-dipole, van der Waals).
diagram_description: Diagram showing haloalkane molecules interacting weakly with water and strongly with organic solvent molecules
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Density of haloalkanes increases with the number of halogen atoms and atomic mass of halogens. Bromo, iodo, and polychloro derivatives are denser than water. Chloro compounds show lower density, with chloromethane (CH₃Cl) around 0.92 g/mL and higher halogen-containing compounds exceeding 1.3 g/mL.
diagram_description: Table of density values for n-C₃H₇Cl, n-C₃H₇Br, n-C₃H₇I, CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃, CCl₄
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
summary: Pure haloalkanes are colourless liquids or gases, but bromides and iodides develop brown/yellow colour upon exposure to light (decomposition of halide). Many are volatile with characteristically sweet smell. This is relevant to detection and safe handling.
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloalkanes undergo nucleophilic substitution via two competing mechanisms: SN2 (bimolecular, one-step, inversion) and SN1 (unimolecular, two-step, racemisation). Primary halides favour SN2; tertiary favour SN1; secondary undergo both. Nu⁻ attacks δ+ carbon, displacing X⁻ as leaving group.
diagram_description: Comparative energy diagrams for SN1 and SN2 reaction pathways

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: SN2 is a concerted, single-step mechanism where nucleophile simultaneously attacks from the back side of C—X bond while halide departs, resulting in inversion of configuration (Walden inversion). Rate depends on both [RX] and [Nu⁻]; steric bulk dramatically inhibits reaction. Primary halides react fastest; tertiary are unreactive.
diagram_description: SN2 reaction mechanism showing back-side attack, transition state with pentavalent carbon, and inversion

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: SN1 is a two-step mechanism: slow C—X bond cleavage forms a planar sp² carbocation, then fast Nu⁻ attack from either side of plane yields racemic mixture. Rate depends only on [RX]; solvent stabilises carbocation. Tertiary halides react fastest; 3° carbocation most stable. Requires polar protic solvent.
diagram_description: SN1 reaction mechanism showing carbocation intermediate, planar geometry, and racemic products

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Ambident nucleophiles (CN⁻, NO₂⁻) have two nucleophilic sites due to resonance. CN⁻ with ionic KCN attacks predominantly via C (forming R—CN); AgCN (covalent) attacks via N (forming R—NC). NO₂⁻ attacks via O⁻ in aqueous KNO₂ (forming R—O—N=O) or via N⁰ with AgNO₂ (forming R—NO₂).
diagram_description: Resonance structures of cyanide and nitrite ions showing two nucleophilic centres
subject: chemistry

summary: SN2 reactions of optically active haloalkanes give products with inverted configuration. SN1 reactions yield racemic mixtures due to planar carbocation allowing attack from both faces. Chirality (non-superimposable mirror images) causes optical activity; enantiomers differ only in optical rotation direction.
diagram_description: Walden inversion in SN2 and racemisation in SN1 with 3D structures and wedge-dash notation

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Elimination and Metal Reactions of Haloalkanes
summary: β-Elimination occurs when haloalkanes with β-hydrogens are heated with alcoholic KOH. A base abstracts β-hydrogen while halide departs, forming C=C double bond. The major product is the alkene with more alkyl groups on the double bond (Zaitsev rule). Competes with SN1/SN2 based on alkyl group, base strength, and temperature.
diagram_description: β-Elimination reaction mechanism showing hydrogen abstraction from β-carbon and halide departure
subject: chemistry

summary: Base abstracts a proton from β-carbon (adjacent to carbon bearing halogen) while C—X bond breaks simultaneously. For molecules with multiple β-hydrogens, multiple alkene products are possible. The mechanism is concerted (no intermediate); regioselectivity depends on stability of product alkene.
diagram_description: Elimination reaction showing base-carbon-hydrogen interactions and C=C bond formation

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: In elimination reactions, the preferred product is the alkene with the greater number of alkyl groups attached to the doubly bonded carbons (more substituted alkene). For example, 2-bromopentane yields pent-2-ene (major, ~81%) over pent-1-ene (minor, ~19%).
diagram_description: Example showing 2-bromopentane elimination to pent-2-ene (major) and pent-1-ene (minor)
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Elimination and Metal Reactions of Haloalkanes
summary: Grignard reagents (RMgX) form when haloalkanes react with Mg metal in dry ether. The C—Mg bond is polar covalent (C is nucleophilic, Mg—X is ionic). They are highly reactive, reacting with any proton source (water, alcohols, amines) to form hydrocarbons. Sensitive to moisture; must be prepared anhydrously.
diagram_description: Grignard reagent formation and subsequent reactions with water, alcohols, and other electrophiles
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Elimination and Metal Reactions of Haloalkanes
summary: Two moles of alkyl halide react with Na in dry ether to form an alkane with double the number of carbons. The mechanism involves formation of organosodium intermediate which couples to form C—C bond and releases NaX. Useful for preparing larger alkanes from smaller halides.
diagram_description: Wurtz reaction showing coupling of two alkyl halides via sodium to form larger alkane
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloarenes show unique reactivity: extremely unreactive to nucleophilic substitution (C—Ar sp² bond is shorter/stronger; no SN1 due to unstable phenyl cation), but readily undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution. Halogen is weakly deactivating yet ortho/para directing via resonance.
diagram_description: Comparison of reactivity of haloalkanes vs haloarenes in nucleophilic and electrophilic reactions
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloarenes are extremely unreactive towards nucleophilic substitution for four reasons: (1) C—Ar bond has partial double bond character (resonance), (2) sp² carbon is more electronegative and holds C—X electrons tightly, (3) phenyl cation is unstable (no resonance stabilisation), (4) electron-rich aromatic ring repels nucleophiles. Requires extreme conditions or electron-withdrawing groups (NO₂ ortho/para).
diagram_description: Resonance structures of chlorobenzene showing partial double bond character of C—Cl
subject: chemistry

summary: Haloarenes undergo electrophilic substitution (halogenation, nitration, sulphonation, Friedel-Crafts) but more slowly than benzene. Halogen is weakly deactivating (inductive −I effect) yet ortho/para directing (resonance donation of lone pair). Inductive effect controls reactivity; resonance controls orientation.
diagram_description: Resonance structures showing electron density increase at ortho and para positions in chlorobenzene
subject: chemistry

summary: Wurtz-Fittig reaction: mixture of alkyl and aryl halides treated with Na in dry ether yields alkylarenes (C—C coupled product). Fittig reaction: two aryl halides coupled via Na give biphenyls (Ar—Ar). Both involve organosodium intermediates.
diagram_description: Wurtz-Fittig and Fittig reactions showing coupling products
subject: chemistry

summary: Polyhalogenated compounds (CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃, CCl₄, CFCs, DDT) have industrial and agricultural uses but pose environmental hazards. Many persist in ecosystems due to chemical stability; some deplete atmospheric ozone or accumulate in fatty tissues. Health risks include liver damage, nerve damage, and cancer.
diagram_description: Structures of common polyhalogenated compounds with their uses and hazards
subject: chemistry

summary: Dichloromethane (CH₂Cl₂) is widely used as solvent, paint remover, and aerosol propellant. It harms the central nervous system; exposure causes impaired hearing/vision at low levels, dizziness and numbness at higher levels. Direct skin/eye contact causes burning and corneal damage.
subject: chemistry

summary: Chloroform (CHCl₃) is used as solvent and freon precursor; formerly used as anaesthetic. Chronic exposure damages liver/kidneys. It slowly oxidises in air/light to phosgene (COCl₂), an extremely toxic gas. Stored in dark, completely filled bottles to prevent this decomposition.
subject: chemistry

summary: Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄) is produced for refrigerants, propellants, and solvents. Evidence suggests it causes liver cancer; causes dizziness, nausea, nerve damage, and cardiac irregularities. When released to atmosphere, it rises to stratosphere and depletes ozone layer, increasing UV exposure and skin cancer risk.
subject: chemistry

summary: Iodoform (CHI₃) was used as antiseptic; its antiseptic properties stem from liberation of free iodine, not from iodoform itself. Due to objectionable smell, it has been replaced by other iodine formulations. Now mainly of historical interest.
subject: chemistry

summary: Freons (CFCs) are chlorofluorocarbon compounds of methane/ethane (e.g. Freon 12: CCl₂F₂) produced for aerosol propellants, refrigeration, and air conditioning. Extremely stable, non-toxic, non-corrosive. By 1974, 2 billion pounds/year produced.
subject: chemistry

summary: DDT, discovered 1939, was highly effective insecticide against malaria-carrying mosquitoes and typhus-carrying lice. Due to chemical stability and fat solubility, it bioaccumulates in fatty tissues. Many insects developed resistance; toxicity to fish noted. Banned in USA 1973 due to environmental persistence and health concerns, though still used elsewhere.
subject: chemistry

summary: When haloalkane with β-hydrogens reacts with base/nucleophile, substitution and elimination compete. Bulky nucleophiles favour elimination (steric reasons). Primary halides prefer SN2 substitution. Secondary halides can undergo both SN2/E depending on base strength.
diagram_description: Decision tree or table showing factors favouring substitution vs elimination

builds_upon.concept_name: Elimination and Metal Reactions of Haloalkanes

subject: chemistry

summary: Molecules with four different groups on a carbon atom are chiral (asymmetric). Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images showing opposite optical rotation (+ or −). Racemic mixture (dl or ±) contains equal enantiomers with zero net rotation. Optical activity discovered by Pasteur; van't Hoff explained it by tetrahedral carbon (1874).
diagram_description: 3D structures of chiral molecules showing mirror images and wedge-dash representations

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Victor Grignard discovered Grignard reagents (RMgX) in 1900 as his doctoral thesis work. He switched from mathematics to organic chemistry. The C—Mg bond is highly polar, making the carbon strongly nucleophilic. Grignard won the 1912 Nobel Prize in Chemistry (shared with Paul Sabatier) for this discovery.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Alcohols, phenols and ethers are classified by the number and type of hydroxyl groups, and the hybridization state of the carbon atom bearing the functional group. Primary alcohols have –OH attached to sp³ carbon bonded to one alkyl group; secondary alcohols to two alkyl groups; tertiary to three. Allylic alcohols have –OH on a carbon adjacent to C=C. Ethers are classified as simple (symmetrical) when both groups are identical, or unsymmetrical when different.
diagram_description: Structural formulas showing primary, secondary, tertiary, allylic, benzylic and vinylic alcohols side by side with ball-and-stick models
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Alcohols are classified by the number of hydroxyl groups present: monohydric alcohols contain one –OH group (e.g., CH₃OH), dihydric alcohols contain two –OH groups (e.g., ethylene glycol), and trihydric alcohols contain three or more –OH groups (e.g., glycerol). This classification determines reactivity and physical properties.
diagram_description: Table or structures showing one, two, three and multiple hydroxyl groups in different alcohols

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Primary alcohols (1°) have the –OH group attached to an sp³ carbon bonded to one alkyl group (R–CH₂–OH). Secondary alcohols (2°) have –OH on a carbon bonded to two alkyl groups (R₂CH–OH). Tertiary alcohols (3°) have –OH on a carbon bonded to three alkyl groups (R₃C–OH). This classification is crucial for predicting reactivity in oxidation and substitution reactions.
diagram_description: Three side-by-side structural diagrams showing primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols with R groups labeled

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Allylic alcohols have –OH attached to an sp³ carbon adjacent to a C=C double bond. Benzylic alcohols have –OH attached to an sp³ carbon next to an aromatic ring. Both can exist as primary, secondary, or tertiary alcohols and show enhanced reactivity due to conjugation effects with the double bond or ring.
diagram_description: Two structures showing an allylic alcohol next to a C=C bond and a benzylic alcohol next to a benzene ring

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Vinylic alcohols have the –OH group bonded to an sp² hybridized carbon of a C=C double bond or aryl carbon. These compounds are generally unstable and tautomerize to their more stable keto forms through enol-keto tautomerism.
diagram_description: Structures showing vinyl alcohol (enol) and its tautomeric acetaldehyde (keto) form with equilibrium arrow

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Phenols are classified by the number of –OH groups attached directly to an aromatic ring. Monohydric phenols contain one –OH (e.g., phenol, cresols). Dihydric phenols (benzenediols) contain two –OH groups in ortho (catechol), meta (resorcinol), or para (hydroquinone) positions. Trihydric phenols contain three –OH groups.
diagram_description: Benzene ring structures with one, two, and three hydroxyl groups in different positions

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Simple or symmetrical ethers have identical alkyl or aryl groups attached to oxygen (e.g., C₂H₅–O–C₂H₅, diethyl ether). Unsymmetrical or mixed ethers have different groups (e.g., C₂H₅–O–CH₃, ethyl methyl ether; C₂H₅–O–C₆H₅, ethyl phenyl ether).
diagram_description: Side-by-side structures of diethyl ether (simple) and ethyl methyl ether (unsymmetrical)

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: IUPAC nomenclature for alcohols replaces the 'e' of the alkane with 'ol' and numbers from the hydroxyl carbon. Phenols use the parent name phenol with position numbers for substituents. Ethers are named as hydrocarbon derivatives where a hydrogen is replaced by –OR or –OAr group, with the larger alkyl group as the parent.
diagram_description: Three examples each showing common name and IUPAC name for a simple alcohol, phenol, and ether

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Alcohols are named by replacing the 'e' of the corresponding alkane with the suffix 'ol'. The parent chain is numbered starting from the end nearest the hydroxyl group. Polyhydric alcohols retain the 'e' and add multiplicative prefixes (di-, tri-) before 'ol'. Position numbers are indicated using locants (e.g., ethane-1,2-diol for ethylene glycol).
diagram_description: Structural examples with step-by-step IUPAC naming for primary, secondary and polyhydric alcohols

builds_upon.concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: The parent structure is phenol (C₆H₅OH). Substituted phenols are named with position numbers; benzenediols are named as 1,2-benzenediol (catechol), 1,3-benzenediol (resorcinol), or 1,4-benzenediol (hydroquinone). Common names using ortho (1,2-), meta (1,3-), and para (1,4-) prefixes are also widely used.
diagram_description: Benzene ring structures with numbered positions showing IUPAC and common names for cresols and benzenediols

builds_upon.concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Ethers are named as alkoxy or aryloxy derivatives of hydrocarbons. The larger alkyl or aryl group is chosen as the parent, and the smaller group is named as an alkoxy (or aryloxy) substituent with its position. Common names list both groups in alphabetical order followed by 'ether' (e.g., ethyl methyl ether).
diagram_description: Ether structures with both IUPAC and common names labeled; examples of simple and unsymmetrical ethers

builds_upon.concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Common names derive from the alkyl group and the word 'alcohol' (e.g., methyl alcohol for CH₃OH, ethyl alcohol for C₂H₅OH). Phenols often use common names like cresol and o/m/p nomenclature. Ethers are named by listing both groups in alphabetical order with 'ether' (e.g., diethyl ether, ethyl phenyl ether).
diagram_description: Comparison table of common and IUPAC names for representative alcohols, phenols and ethers

builds_upon.concept_name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Reduction of Carboxylic Acids and Esters
summary: Alcohols are prepared by hydration of alkenes, hydroboration-oxidation, reduction of carbonyl compounds using catalysts or NaBH₄/LiAlH₄, reduction of carboxylic acids by LiAlH₄, and reaction of Grignard reagents with aldehydes and ketones. The method chosen depends on functional group present and desired product.
diagram_description: Five reaction schemes showing different methods of alcohol synthesis from various starting materials
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkenes react with water in the presence of acid catalyst (H₂SO₄) to form alcohols via carbocation intermediate. The reaction follows Markovnikov's rule: H adds to the carbon with more hydrogens, OH to the more substituted carbon. Mechanism: protonation → carbocation formation → nucleophilic attack by water → deprotonation.
diagram_description: Three-step reaction mechanism showing alkene protonation, carbocation formation, water attack, and deprotonation
formula: \text{with H and OH added per Markovnikov's rule}
subject: chemistry

summary: Diborane (BH₃)₂ adds to alkenes to form trialkylboranes, which are then oxidized by H₂O₂ in aqueous NaOH to yield alcohols. The reaction follows anti-Markovnikov's rule: OH adds to the less substituted carbon and H to the more substituted carbon, yielding primary alcohols in excellent yield.
diagram_description: Two-step reaction showing borane addition followed by oxidation to give anti-Markovnikov alcohol product
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes and ketones are reduced to alcohols by catalytic hydrogenation (Pt, Pd, Ni), NaBH₄, or LiAlH₄. Aldehydes yield primary alcohols; ketones yield secondary alcohols. NaBH₄ is milder and preferred for simple compounds; LiAlH₄ is stronger but more expensive and requires careful handling.
diagram_description: Two parallel reduction pathways showing aldehyde → primary alcohol and ketone → secondary alcohol
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Reduction of Carboxylic Acids and Esters
summary: Carboxylic acids are reduced to primary alcohols by LiAlH₄ in excellent yield, though the reagent is expensive. Esters are first converted to carboxylic acids via acid hydrolysis, then reduced. Commercially, esters are reduced via catalytic hydrogenation over a catalyst after esterification to avoid LiAlH₄ cost.
diagram_description: Two routes for reducing carboxylic acids: direct LiAlH₄ reduction and esterification followed by catalytic reduction
subject: chemistry

summary: Grignard reagents (RMgX) add to carbonyl groups: methanal yields primary alcohols, other aldehydes yield secondary alcohols, and ketones yield tertiary alcohols. Mechanism involves nucleophilic addition to form a magnesium alkoxide intermediate, followed by aqueous hydrolysis to release the alcohol.
diagram_description: Three parallel Grignard reactions showing methanal, aldehyde, and ketone reacting with RMgX to give different alcohol classes
subject: chemistry

summary: Chlorobenzene is fused with NaOH at 623 K and 320 atm pressure to form sodium phenoxide, which is then acidified to yield phenol. This is a nucleophilic aromatic substitution reaction requiring high temperature and pressure due to the low reactivity of aryl halides.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing chlorobenzene reacting with NaOH under high temperature and pressure to form phenoxide, then HCl to give phenol
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzene is sulphonated with oleum to form benzenesulphonic acid. The sulphonic acid group (–SO₃H) is then replaced by –OH by heating the acid with molten sodium hydroxide to form sodium phenoxide, which is acidified to yield phenol.
diagram_description: Two-step process showing benzene sulphonation followed by nucleophilic displacement of SO3H group by OH
subject: chemistry

summary: Aromatic primary amines are treated with NaNO₂ and HCl at 273–278 K to form diazonium salts. These are hydrolyzed by warming with water or dilute acid, causing loss of N₂ to yield phenols. This is a versatile method useful when the corresponding amine is readily available.
diagram_description: Three-step synthesis showing aniline reacting with nitrous acid to form diazonium salt, then hydrolysis to phenol with N2 evolution
subject: chemistry

summary: Cumene (isopropylbenzene) is oxidized in air to cumene hydroperoxide, which is then cleaved by dilute acid to yield phenol and acetone. This is the dominant industrial method as acetone (a valuable by-product) is co-produced, making the process economically attractive.
diagram_description: Two-step industrial synthesis showing cumene oxidation to hydroperoxide, then acid-catalyzed cleavage yielding phenol and acetone
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
subtopic_names: Boiling Points of Alcohols and Phenols
summary: Alcohols and phenols have high boiling points due to extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding, much higher than comparable hydrocarbons and ethers. They are also highly soluble in water due to hydrogen bonding with water molecules. Boiling points increase with alkyl chain length and decrease with branching.
diagram_description: Graph comparing boiling points of alcohols, ethers, and hydrocarbons of similar molecular mass; structural reasons explained

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Boiling Points of Alcohols and Phenols
parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols and Phenols
summary: Boiling points increase with increasing number of carbon atoms (increased van der Waals forces) and decrease with increasing branching (decreased surface area). The –OH group enables extensive hydrogen bonding between molecules, making alcohols and phenols much higher boiling than alkanes of comparable mass. For example, ethanol (351 K) has a much higher b.p. than propane (231 K) despite similar molar mass.
diagram_description: Boiling point trend chart showing linear alcohols, branched alcohols, ethers, and alkanes; hydrogen bonding diagrams

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols and Phenols
summary: Alcohols and phenols are soluble in water because the –OH group forms hydrogen bonds with water molecules. Solubility decreases with increasing size of the hydrophobic alkyl/aryl group. Lower molecular mass alcohols (methanol, ethanol, propan-1-ol) are miscible with water in all proportions.
diagram_description: Hydrogen bonding between alcohol/phenol molecules and water molecules showing hydrophilic OH and hydrophobic alkyl groups

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols and Phenols
summary: In alcohols, C–O is formed by sp³ orbital overlap; bond angle is slightly less than tetrahedral (109°) due to lone pair repulsion on oxygen. In phenols, C–O connects to sp² aromatic carbon, giving shorter bond (136 pm vs 142 pm in methanol) due to partial double-bond character from conjugation. Ethers have similar C–O length to alcohols but no hydrogen bonding capability.
diagram_description: Structural diagrams of methanol, phenol, and methoxymethane showing bond lengths, bond angles, and hybridization states

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols show reactions involving O–H cleavage (acidity, esterification) and C–O cleavage (substitution, dehydration). They act as Brönsted acids due to the polar O–H bond and are weaker acids than water. Primary alcohols oxidize to aldehydes then carboxylic acids; secondary to ketones; tertiary resist oxidation.
diagram_description: Reaction map showing five major types of alcohol reactions with examples and products

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols are weak Brönsted acids due to the polar O–H bond. They react with active metals (Na, K, Al) to form alkoxides and hydrogen gas. Acidity decreases in the order: primary > secondary > tertiary, because electron-releasing alkyl groups increase electron density on oxygen, decreasing O–H polarity and acid strength.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing alcohol reacting with sodium metal to produce alkoxide and H2 gas; diagram showing electron donation by alkyl groups
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols react with carboxylic acids, acid chlorides, and acid anhydrides to form esters. With carboxylic acids, the reaction occurs in the presence of concentrated H₂SO₄ (reversible); with acid chlorides or anhydrides, a base (pyridine) is used to neutralize HCl formed. Acetylation introduces the acetyl group (CH₃CO–) into alcohols and phenols.
diagram_description: Three esterification pathways: alcohol + acid (H2SO4), alcohol + acid chloride (pyridine), alcohol + acid anhydride (pyridine)
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols react with hydrogen halides to form alkyl halides via nucleophilic substitution. Tertiary alcohols form halides fastest (SN1 via stable carbocation); primary alcohols slower (SN2). The Lucas test exploits these rate differences to distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols by observing turbidity formation with HCl/ZnCl₂.
diagram_description: Three parallel reaction pathways showing primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohol reacting with HX at different rates
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols undergo dehydration in the presence of protic acids (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) or catalysts (ZnCl₂, Al₂O₃) to form alkenes. The reaction follows E1 mechanism via carbocation, with ease increasing: tertiary > secondary > primary. When multiple alkenes possible, the more substituted (Zaitsev) product predominates.
diagram_description: Three-step dehydration mechanism showing protonation, carbocation formation, and proton elimination to form alkene
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary alcohols are oxidized to aldehydes (mild oxidant: CrO₃, PCC) or carboxylic acids (strong oxidant: KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇). Secondary alcohols are oxidized to ketones. Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation but undergo C–C bond cleavage under strong conditions. Oxidation involves loss of H from C and H from O, forming C=O.
diagram_description: Three parallel oxidation pathways showing primary alcohol → aldehyde → acid, secondary alcohol → ketone, and tertiary alcohol resistance
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Reaction of Phenol with Zinc Dust
summary: Phenols undergo characteristic reactions: they are more acidic than alcohols due to resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion; they undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution at ortho and para positions (–OH is activating and directing); they form benzoquinones upon oxidation. Named reactions include Kolbe's (CO₂ insertion), Reimer-Tiemann (aldehyde introduction), and diazo coupling.
diagram_description: Reaction map showing five major phenol reactions: nitration, halogenation, Kolbe, Reimer-Tiemann, and oxidation with products

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

summary: Phenols are much more acidic than alcohols (pKa ~10 vs ~15) because the phenoxide ion is stabilized by resonance delocalization over the aromatic ring. Electron-withdrawing groups (–NO₂) increase acidity (lower pKa); electron-releasing groups (–CH₃) decrease acidity. Nitrophenols are much stronger acids than phenol due to resonance stabilization of the negative charge.
diagram_description: Resonance structures of phenoxide ion showing charge delocalization; pKa table comparing phenol, cresol, nitrophenols, and ethanol
subject: chemistry

summary: The –OH group activates the benzene ring toward electrophilic substitution by donating electron density through resonance, making the ring more electron-rich. It directs incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions where resonance structures place positive charge on oxygen-bearing carbons. This dual activation and directing effect makes phenols much more reactive than benzene.
diagram_description: Resonance structures showing electron donation by OH group and positive charge distribution in ortho, meta, and para carbocations
subject: chemistry

summary: Phenol reacts with dilute HNO₃ at low temperature (298 K) to give a mixture of ortho and para nitrophenols. With concentrated HNO₃, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid) is formed. The isomers can be separated by steam distillation: o-nitrophenol is volatile (intramolecular H-bonding); p-nitrophenol is less volatile (intermolecular H-bonding).
diagram_description: Phenol reacting with dilute HNO3 to form o- and p-nitrophenol; steam distillation separation based on H-bonding
subject: chemistry

summary: In low-polarity solvents (CHCl₃, CS₂) at low temperature, phenol undergoes monobromination at ortho and para positions. In bromine water, 2,4,6-tribromophenol precipitates as a white solid. The –OH group activates the ring so strongly that a Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃) is not required, unlike benzene halogenation.
diagram_description: Two halogenation pathways: low-polarity solvent → monobromophenol; bromine water → tribromophenol precipitate
subject: chemistry

summary: Phenoxide ions (formed by treating phenol with NaOH) are sufficiently nucleophilic to undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution with CO₂, a normally weak electrophile. CO₂ inserts at the ortho position, yielding ortho hydroxybenzoic acid (salicylic acid) as the main product. This reaction only works with phenoxide, not neutral phenol.
diagram_description: Phenol reacting with NaOH to form phenoxide, then CO2 insertion at ortho position to give salicylic acid
subject: chemistry

summary: Phenol reacts with chloroform (CHCl₃) in the presence of aqueous NaOH to introduce a –CHO group at the ortho position, yielding salicylaldehyde. The reaction proceeds via dichlorocarbene intermediate formed from chloroform in basic solution, which inserts into the aromatic ring.
diagram_description: Phenol reacting with chloroform under basic conditions to form intermediate salicylaldehyde
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Reaction of Phenol with Zinc Dust
summary: Phenol is converted to benzene when heated with zinc dust. The zinc acts as a reducing agent, removing the –OH group and replacing it with hydrogen. This reaction demonstrates the difference in stability between the aromatic C–OH and alkane C–H bonds.
diagram_description: Phenol reacting with zinc dust under heat to form benzene and zinc oxide
subject: chemistry

summary: Phenol is oxidized by chromic acid to benzoquinone, a conjugated diketone. In the presence of air, phenols are slowly oxidized to dark-colored mixtures containing quinones. Quinones are important intermediates in biological redox processes.
diagram_description: Phenol oxidation to benzoquinone using chromic acid, showing conjugated diketone structure
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Cleavage of Ethers by Hydrogen Halides
Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution of Aryl Ethers
summary: Ethers are prepared by dehydration of alcohols in the presence of protic acids (intramolecular, forming alkenes, or bimolecular, forming ethers) and by Williamson synthesis (nucleophilic substitution of alkyl halides with alkoxides/aryloxides). Williamson synthesis is preferred for most ethers as it offers better control and product selectivity.
diagram_description: Two complementary synthetic routes to ethers: acid-catalyzed dehydration and Williamson alkoxide/alkyl halide reaction

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols undergo dehydration to form ethers via a bimolecular SN2 mechanism in the presence of protic acids (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) at lower temperatures (~413 K). The method works best with primary alcohols with unhindered alkyl groups. At higher temperatures, elimination dominates, forming alkenes instead of ethers.
diagram_description: Three-step SN2 mechanism for ether formation: protonation of first alcohol, nucleophilic attack by second alcohol, deprotonation
subject: chemistry

summary: In Williamson synthesis, an alkoxide or aryloxide (RO⁻ or ArO⁻) reacts with a primary alkyl halide via SN2 mechanism to yield an ether. Works best with primary alkyl halides; secondary and tertiary halides undergo elimination because alkoxides are strong bases. This is the most general and versatile method for ether synthesis.
diagram_description: SN2 reaction mechanism showing alkoxide nucleophile attacking primary alkyl halide to form ether and displace halide
subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Ether boiling points are determined by van der Waals forces and weak dipole-dipole interactions from the polar C–O bonds. With no intermolecular hydrogen bonding, ether b.p. values lie between those of alkanes and alcohols of comparable molecular mass. For example, ethoxyethane (307.6 K) is much lower than butan-1-ol (390 K) but similar to n-pentane (309.1 K).
diagram_description: Boiling point comparison table: alkane, ether, and alcohol of same molecular mass showing hierarchy

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

parent_concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
summary: Ethers are slightly soluble in water because the oxygen lone pairs can accept hydrogen bonds from water molecules. Solubility of ethers in water resembles that of alcohols of similar molecular mass. For example, ethoxyethane and butan-1-ol have similar water solubility (~7.5–9 g per 100 mL), both much higher than n-pentane.
diagram_description: Ether oxygen hydrogen bonding with water molecules; solubility data comparing ethers, alcohols, and alkanes

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Cleavage of Ethers by Hydrogen Halides
summary: Ethers are cleaved by concentrated HI or HBr at high temperature. The mechanism involves protonation to form an oxonium ion, followed by nucleophilic attack by halide. With primary/secondary alkyl groups, SN2 predominates, forming the alkyl halide from the less substituted carbon. With tertiary alkyl groups, SN1 occurs, forming the tertiary halide.
diagram_description: Three-step mechanism: ether protonation → oxonium ion → halide attack → products; shows SN1 vs SN2 pathways
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution of Aryl Ethers
summary: Aryl ethers (especially anisole, C₆H₅–OCH₃) readily undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution at ortho and para positions because the –OCH₃ group activates the ring by resonance and directs incoming electrophiles ortho/para. Common reactions include halogenation, Friedel-Crafts alkylation/acylation, and nitration.
diagram_description: Anisole reacting with various electrophiles (Br2, alkyl halide, acyl halide, HNO3) to give ortho and para products
subject: chemistry

summary: Methanol (CH₃OH, wood spirit) and ethanol (C₂H₅OH, grain alcohol) are the two most important alcohols. Methanol is synthesized industrially by hydrogenation of CO over ZnO–Cr₂O₃ catalysts and is toxic; ethanol is obtained by fermentation of sugars or hydration of ethene. Both are valuable solvents and chemical feedstocks.
diagram_description: Comparison table of methanol and ethanol properties, production methods, and uses

builds_upon.concept_name: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

summary: Methanol (CH₃OH) is produced industrially by catalytic hydrogenation of CO at 200–300 atm and 573–673 K using ZnO–Cr₂O₃ catalyst. It is a colorless liquid (b.p. 337 K) and highly toxic; even small ingestion causes blindness and death by oxidation to methanoic acid. Primary use is as a solvent and feedstock for formaldehyde production.
diagram_description: Industrial methanol synthesis reactor diagram showing CO + H2 feedstock, catalyst bed, and product recovery
subject: chemistry

summary: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is produced by fermentation of sugars via the enzyme zymase (in yeast) under anaerobic conditions: glucose → ethanol + CO₂. Modern industrial ethanol is also made by hydration of ethene over phosphoric acid catalyst. It is a colorless liquid (b.p. 351 K), solvent, and key chemical feedstock. Commercial alcohol is denatured (made unfit for drinking) by adding copper sulfate and pyridine.
diagram_description: Fermentation pathway and industrial ethene hydration showing reactor conditions and product separation
subject: chemistry

summary: The Lucas test is a rapid chemical test to classify alcohols by reactivity. Alcohols dissolve in Lucas reagent (conc. HCl + ZnCl₂). Tertiary alcohols form a turbid alkyl chloride layer immediately (SN1 via stable 3° carbocation); secondary alcohols within minutes; primary alcohols remain clear at room temperature.
diagram_description: Three test tubes showing Lucas test results for primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols with timing and color changes
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols can be distinguished from ethers and alkanes by comparing boiling points and solubility in water at constant molecular mass. Alcohols show anomalously high boiling points and high water solubility due to hydrogen bonding. Phenols dissolve in aqueous NaOH (forming phenoxide) but alcohols do not, providing a clear distinction.
diagram_description: Property comparison table: boiling point and water solubility of alcohol vs ether vs alkane; NaOH solubility test

builds_upon.concept_name: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols and phenols undergo interconversion with other functional groups: alcohols oxidize to aldehydes/ketones/carboxylic acids or reduce from carbonyls/esters; alcohols substitute to alkyl halides or ethers; alcohols/phenols esterify to protect the –OH group. These transformations form the basis of multi-step organic synthesis.
diagram_description: Functional group map showing alcohol/phenol at center with arrows to aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, esters, ethers, and alkyl halides
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary alcohols are oxidized to aldehydes (mild oxidant) or carboxylic acids (strong oxidant) and reduced from esters/carboxylic acids. Secondary alcohols are oxidized to ketones and reduced from ketones. Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation. This reversible interconversion allows controlled oxidation level adjustment in synthesis.
diagram_description: Oxidation state pyramid showing primary alcohol at center with pathways up to aldehyde and carboxylic acid
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols undergo nucleophilic substitution with HX to yield alkyl halides (SN1 for 3°, SN2 for 1°). Alcohols/alkoxides undergo substitution with alkyl halides to form ethers (Williamson synthesis). These reactions interconvert alcohols to halides and ethers, expanding the synthetic repertoire.
diagram_description: Substitution interconversion showing alcohol → alkyl halide and alkoxide → ether with SN1 and SN2 mechanisms
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols and phenols are converted to esters (acetates, benzoates) to temporarily protect the –OH group during multi-step synthesis, preventing unwanted side reactions. Esters are easily cleaved under mild conditions (base or acid hydrolysis) to regenerate the alcohol/phenol. This protection-deprotection strategy is fundamental to complex organic synthesis.
diagram_description: Protection-deprotection cycle showing alcohol → acetate ester → alcohol with synthesis steps in between
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Physical Properties Of Aldehydes And Ketones
summary: Aldehydes and ketones are carbonyl compounds with C=O double bonds. In aldehydes, the carbonyl is bonded to one carbon and one hydrogen; in ketones, to two carbons. Both undergo nucleophilic addition and are important synthetic intermediates.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques
Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes are named by replacing the -e of alkanes with -al; ketones with -one. The carbonyl carbon in aldehydes is always C1. Greek letters (α, β, γ) denote substituent positions. Common names derive from Latin/Greek sources (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone).
diagram_description: Table showing common and IUPAC names of representative aldehydes and ketones with their structures

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: The carbonyl carbon is sp2-hybridized, forming three σ-bonds with 120° angles. The C=O double bond consists of one σ-bond and one π-bond. Oxygen's higher electronegativity polarizes the bond, making the carbon electrophilic and oxygen nucleophilic. Resonance structures show C⁺=O⁻ character.
diagram_description: Orbital diagram showing sp2 hybridization, sigma and pi bond formation, and planar geometry of carbonyl group with resonance structures

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques
Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes are prepared by: (1) controlled oxidation of primary alcohols using PCC or Jones reagent; (2) Rosenmund reduction of acyl chlorides with Pd-BaSO₄; (3) Stephen reaction—nitriles with SnCl₂/HCl; (4) ozonolysis of terminal alkenes; (5) Etard and Gatterman-Koch reactions for aromatic aldehydes.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing major aldehyde preparation methods with starting materials and conditions
subject: chemistry

summary: Ketones are prepared by: (1) oxidation of secondary alcohols with chromic acid or KMnO₄; (2) hydration of alkynes with H₂SO₄/HgSO₄; (3) treatment of acyl chlorides with dialkylcadmium; (4) nitriles with Grignard reagents; (5) Friedel-Crafts acylation of aromatic hydrocarbons with acid chlorides.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing major ketone preparation methods with conditions and reagents
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Physical Properties Of Aldehydes And Ketones
summary: Methanal is gaseous; ethanal and higher aldehydes/ketones are liquids or solids. Boiling points are higher than comparable hydrocarbons and ethers due to dipole-dipole interactions, but lower than alcohols due to lack of hydrogen bonding. Lower members are water-soluble via H-bonding; higher members are hydrophobic but soluble in organic solvents.
diagram_description: Table comparing boiling points of aldehydes, ketones, ethers, alcohols and hydrocarbons of similar molecular mass
subject: chemistry

summary: Nucleophiles attack the electrophilic carbonyl carbon perpendicular to the sp2-plane, forming a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate that is protonated to yield the product. Key addition reactions: HCN (cyanohydrins), NaHSO₃ (bisulfite addition), alcohols (hemiacetals/acetals), ammonia derivatives (imines, oximes, hydrazones, semicarbazones), and Grignard reagents.
diagram_description: Reaction mechanism showing nucleophilic attack on carbonyl carbon, tetrahedral intermediate formation, and proton transfer

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes reduce to primary alcohols; ketones to secondary alcohols using NaBH₄, LiAlH₄, or catalytic H₂. The carbonyl group can be reduced to CH₂ using Clemmensen reduction (Zn-Hg, conc. HCl) or Wolff-Kishner reduction (NH₂NH₂, KOH/heat), converting aldehydes to CH₃- and ketones to hydrocarbons.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing reduction pathways: aldehydes/ketones to alcohols, and complete reduction to hydrocarbons
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes are easily oxidized to carboxylic acids by KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, HNO₃, Tollens' reagent (Ag⁺), or Fehling's reagent (Cu²⁺). Ketones require vigorous oxidation with C-C bond cleavage. Methyl ketones undergo haloform reaction with NaOX to produce haloforms (CHX₃) and carboxylates. These oxidations distinguish aldehydes from ketones.
diagram_description: Reaction equations showing oxidation of aldehydes to carboxylic acids and methyl ketones to haloforms
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes and ketones with α-hydrogens undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. The α-hydrogen is deprotonated to form an enolate nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl of another molecule, forming a β-hydroxyaldehyde (aldol) or β-hydroxyketone (ketol). Heat drives dehydration to yield α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds. Cross aldol uses two different carbonyl compounds.
diagram_description: Step-by-step mechanism showing enolate formation, nucleophilic attack, aldol intermediate, and dehydration to enone product
subject: chemistry

key_terms: aldehyde to alcohol and carboxylic acid
summary: Aldehydes lacking α-hydrogens (e.g., formaldehyde, benzaldehyde) undergo disproportionation with concentrated alkali. One aldehyde molecule is oxidized to a carboxylic acid salt while another is reduced to a primary alcohol. The reaction requires strong base and produces equal moles of alcohol and carboxylate.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing Cannizzaro disproportionation of formaldehyde to methanol and formate, and benzaldehyde to benzyl alcohol and benzoate
subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids contain a carboxyl group (-COOH) bonded to hydrogen (formic acid) or alkyl/aryl groups. They are weak acids stronger than phenols, forming resonance-stabilized carboxylate anions. They undergo esterification, form anhydrides, and react with metals, bases, and ammonia to form derivatives.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Aliphatic carboxylic acids are named by replacing the -e of alkanes with -oic acid; the carboxyl carbon is always C1. Common names derive from Latin/Greek sources (formic from formica=ant, acetic from acetum=vinegar). Polycarboxylic acids use prefixes like dicarboxylic, with position numbers indicating COOH locations.
diagram_description: Table of common and IUPAC names of representative carboxylic acids with their structures

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: The carboxyl carbon is sp2-hybridized with planar geometry and ~120° bond angles. Resonance structures show equivalent C-O bonds with electron density on both oxygens. The carboxyl carbon is less electrophilic than carbonyl carbon due to resonance donation from the hydroxyl oxygen.
diagram_description: Structural diagram showing planar geometry of carboxyl group with resonance structures indicating equivalent C-O bonds

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids are prepared by: (1) oxidation of primary alcohols or aldehydes with KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, or CrO₃; (2) vigorous oxidation of alkylbenzenes with KMnO₄ (entire side chain → COOH); (3) hydrolysis of nitriles with H⁺/H₂O or OH⁻/H₂O; (4) reaction of Grignard reagents with CO₂ followed by acidification.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing major carboxylic acid preparation methods with starting materials and conditions
subject: chemistry

summary: Aliphatic carboxylic acids (C1–C9) are colorless liquids with unpleasant odors; higher acids are waxy solids. Boiling points are much higher than aldehydes, ketones, and alcohols due to extensive intermolecular H-bonding; even in vapor phase, carboxylic acids exist as dimers. Lower acids (C1–C4) are water-soluble via H-bonding; higher acids are hydrophobic.
diagram_description: Structural diagram showing hydrogen-bonded dimer formation in carboxylic acids, both in condensed and vapor phases
subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids are weaker than mineral acids (pKa ~0–2) but stronger than phenols (pKa ~10) and alcohols (pKa ~16). The conjugate base (carboxylate anion) is stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures with negative charge on the electronegative oxygen atoms. Electron-withdrawing groups (F, Cl, NO₂, CN) increase acidity by stabilizing the anion; electron-donating groups (alkyl, OCH₃) decrease acidity.
diagram_description: Comparison table of pKa values for carboxylic acids with various substituents, showing inductive and resonance effects

builds_upon.concept_name: Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids react with alcohols or phenols in the presence of concentrated H₂SO₄ or HCl gas catalyst to form esters and water. The mechanism involves protonation of the carbonyl oxygen (activating), nucleophilic attack by the alcohol, proton transfer to convert OH to ⁺OH₂ (better leaving group), and H₂O elimination. The reaction is reversible and favored by water removal.
diagram_description: Step-by-step Fischer esterification mechanism showing protonation, nucleophilic addition, proton transfer, and water elimination
subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids form anhydrides by heating with H₂SO₄ or P₂O₅. The O-H group is replaced by Cl using PCl₅, PCl₃, or SOCl₂ to give acid chlorides. Reaction with ammonia yields ammonium salts that dehydrate to amides. LiAlH₄ or diborane reduces the carboxyl group to CH₂OH. Decarboxylation (sodalime heat) converts the sodium salt to RH. α-Halogenation via Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky uses X₂/red P.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing major transformations of the carboxyl group: anhydride, acid chloride, amide, alcohol, and decarboxylation products
subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids with α-hydrogens undergo halogenation at the α-position using Cl₂ or Br₂ with red phosphorus catalyst (Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction), yielding α-halocarboxylic acids. Aromatic carboxylic acids undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution with the carboxyl group acting as a deactivating, meta-directing group. They do not undergo Friedel-Crafts acylation because the carboxyl group complexes with the AlCl₃ catalyst.
diagram_description: Reaction equations showing α-halogenation of aliphatic carboxylic acids and meta-directing aromatic substitution
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Factors Affecting Aldehyde And Ketone Reactivity
summary: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition due to steric factors (two bulky groups on ketones hinder nucleophile approach) and electronic factors (two alkyl groups reduce carbonyl carbon electrophilicity more than one does). Aromatic aldehydes (e.g., benzaldehyde) are less reactive than aliphatic aldehydes due to resonance delocalization of π-electrons into the benzene ring.
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Factors Affecting Aldehyde And Ketone Reactivity
summary: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones because they have only one alkyl substituent (less steric hindrance) and one hydrogen attached to the carbonyl carbon. Ketones with two alkyl groups experience greater steric crowding around the carbonyl, reducing nucleophile accessibility. Electron-donating alkyl groups also decrease the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon more effectively in ketones.
diagram_description: Structural comparison of aldehyde, ketone, and sterically-hindered ketone showing nucleophile approach pathways
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzaldehyde is less reactive than aliphatic aldehydes (e.g., propanal) in nucleophilic addition because the benzene ring's π-electrons delocalize into the carbonyl group through resonance, reducing the polarity of the C=O bond and the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon. The resonance structures C₆H₅-C(=O)⁻ ↔ C₆H₅-C⁺=O diminish the positive character on the carbonyl carbon.
diagram_description: Resonance structures of benzaldehyde showing electron delocalization from benzene ring into carbonyl group

builds_upon.concept_name: Factors Affecting Aldehyde And Ketone Reactivity

subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes and ketones form diverse derivatives through nucleophilic addition. Cyanohydrins form with HCN, hemiacetals and acetals with alcohols, oximes with hydroxylamine, hydrazones with hydrazine, and semicarbazones with semicarbazide. These derivatives are useful for protection of the carbonyl group, characterization, and synthesis.
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes and ketones react with hydrogen cyanide (HCN) in the presence of base to form cyanohydrins (R₂C(OH)CN). Pure HCN reacts slowly, so the reaction is base-catalyzed to generate the stronger nucleophile CN⁻. Cyanohydrins are valuable synthetic intermediates that can be hydrolyzed, reduced, or further transformed.
diagram_description: Reaction mechanism of cyanohydrin formation showing cyanide nucleophilic attack and protonation of the alkoxide intermediate
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes react with one equivalent of monohydric alcohol under dry HCl to form hemiacetals (R-CHO + R'OH → R-CH(OH)-OR'). Further reaction with a second alcohol equivalent yields acetals (gem-diether, R-CH(OR')₂). Ketones form corresponding ketals with ethylene glycol. Acetals/ketals are used as protecting groups for carbonyls and are hydrolyzed back to aldehydes/ketones with aqueous acid.
diagram_description: Step-by-step mechanism of hemiacetal and acetal formation showing nucleophilic attack, proton transfer, and water elimination
subject: chemistry

summary: Ammonia derivatives (H₂N-Z) add to aldehydes/ketones to form imines (C=N-Z). Key examples: hydroxylamine → oximes; hydrazine → hydrazones; phenylhydrazine → phenylhydrazones; semicarbazide → semicarbazones; 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine → 2,4-DNP derivatives (yellow/red solids used for characterization). These reactions are reversible, acid-catalyzed, and favor product formation by rapid dehydration.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing formation of oximes, hydrazones, and semicarbazones from aldehydes/ketones with various amine nucleophiles
subject: chemistry

summary: Grignard reagents (RMgX) are strong nucleophiles that add to aldehydes and ketones. Aldehydes yield primary alcohols after aqueous workup; ketones yield secondary alcohols. Esters, nitriles, and carboxylic acids are also attacked. The reaction proceeds through a magnesium alkoxide intermediate that is protonated by aqueous acid or water to release the alcohol product.
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes react with Grignard reagents (RMgX) to form primary alcohols. The Grignard carbon acts as a strong carbanion nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbonyl carbon. After protonation with aqueous acid, the alkoxide intermediate yields RCH₂OH, enabling conversion of one aldehyde to a primary alcohol with carbon chain extension.
diagram_description: Mechanism of Grignard addition to aldehyde forming alkoxide intermediate and primary alcohol after aqueous workup
subject: chemistry

summary: Ketones react with Grignard reagents to form secondary alcohols. Two alkyl/aryl groups remain on the carbonyl carbon after Grignard addition. The magnesium alkoxide intermediate is protonated with aqueous acid to yield R₂CHOH, enabling conversion of ketones to secondary alcohols with carbon chain extension.
diagram_description: Mechanism of Grignard addition to ketone forming alkoxide intermediate and secondary alcohol after aqueous workup
subject: chemistry

summary: Aldehydes are distinguished from ketones using mild oxidizing agents. Tollens' test (Ag⁺/NH₃) produces a silver mirror (AgO or Ag metal). Fehling's test (Cu²⁺ in alkaline tartrate) produces a reddish-brown precipitate (Cu₂O). Both tests oxidize aldehydes to carboxylate anions.
subject: chemistry

summary: Tollens' test uses freshly prepared ammoniacal silver nitrate solution. Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylate anions while Ag⁺ is reduced to Ag metal or Ag₂O, forming a bright silver mirror in alkaline conditions. Fehling's test mixes equal amounts of Fehling solution A (CuSO₄) and B (NaK tartrate in alkali). Aldehydes oxidize Cu²⁺ to Cu₂O, producing a reddish-brown precipitate.
diagram_description: Reaction equations for Tollens' and Fehling's oxidation of aldehydes with description of visual observations
subject: chemistry

summary: Methyl ketones and secondary alcohols (which oxidize to methyl ketones) react with iodine and base (I₂/NaOH) to undergo the haloform reaction. The methyl group is oxidized and cleaved, producing iodoform (CHI₃), a yellow solid, and the carboxylate salt of the remaining acid. This test specifically identifies methyl ketones (CH₃-CO-R) and CH₃CH(OH)- groups.
diagram_description: Reaction mechanism and equation for iodoform test showing methyl ketone conversion to iodoform and carboxylate
subject: chemistry

summary: Carbonyl compounds are widely used in industry. Formaldehyde is a disinfectant, preservative (formalin), and monomer for bakelite and urea-formaldehyde resins. Acetaldehyde is a precursor for acetic acid and vinyl acetate. Acetone and ethyl methyl ketone are common industrial solvents.
subject: chemistry

summary: Formaldehyde (HCHO) is used as formalin solution (40%) for preserving biological specimens and preparing bakelite and urea-formaldehyde polymers. Acetaldehyde is a starting material for acetic acid, ethyl acetate, and vinyl acetate manufacture. Acetone and ethyl methyl ketone are industrial solvents. Benzaldehyde is used in perfumery and dye industries.
subject: chemistry

summary: Formic acid is used in rubber, textile, dyeing, leather, and electroplating industries. Acetic acid serves as a solvent and vinegar in food. Adipic acid is a monomer for nylon-6,6 synthesis. Benzoic acid esters are used in perfumery; sodium benzoate is a food preservative.
subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids (pKa ~2–5) are significantly stronger acids than phenols (pKa ~10) and alcohols (pKa ~16). The carboxylate anion is stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures with negative charge on electronegative oxygens. Phenoxide ions have non-equivalent resonance structures with negative charge on less electronegative carbon, making phenols weaker acids. Alcohols do not ionize appreciably in water.
subject: chemistry

summary: Alcohols (pKa ~16) are extremely weak acids that do not ionize in water. Phenols (pKa ~10) are weakly acidic because the phenoxide anion is partially stabilized by resonance into the benzene ring, with negative charge distributed over the ring carbons. However, this resonance is less effective than in carboxylate ions because the negative charge is on less electronegative carbon atoms.
diagram_description: Resonance structures of phenoxide ion showing electron delocalization into the benzene ring
subject: chemistry

summary: Carboxylic acids react with weaker bases (CO₃²⁻, HCO₃⁻) that do not affect phenols or alcohols. Phenols react with strong bases (NaOH) but not with weak bases. Alcohols require strong bases and dehydrating conditions for reactions. These reactivity differences reflect acid strength: carboxylic acids are strong enough to protonate carbonate; phenols are strong enough to protonate hydroxide but not carbonate; alcohols do not ionize.
diagram_description: Table comparing reactions of carboxylic acids, phenols, and alcohols with various bases
subject: chemistry

summary: Amines are organic derivatives of ammonia where one or more hydrogen atoms are replaced by alkyl or aryl groups. The nitrogen atom in amines is sp3 hybridized with a pyramidal geometry and contains an unshared electron pair. This structure determines their chemical reactivity and basic properties.
diagram_description: Pyramidal structure of trimethylamine showing nitrogen with three methyl groups and unshared electron pair; comparison with ammonia structure

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Nitrogen in amines is sp3 hybridized with four orbitals: three overlap with carbon or hydrogen atoms, and one contains an unshared electron pair. This arrangement produces a pyramidal geometry with a C-N-E bond angle of 108° (less than 109.5° due to lone pair repulsion).
diagram_description: Three-dimensional pyramidal structure showing sp3 hybrid orbitals, sigma bonds, and unshared electron pair

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

summary: Amines are classified by the number of hydrogen atoms replaced in ammonia: primary (RNH₂), secondary (R₂NH or RNH-R'), and tertiary (R₃N). Simple amines have identical alkyl/aryl groups, while mixed amines have different groups attached to nitrogen.
diagram_description: Structural formulas showing primary, secondary, and tertiary amines with examples
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary aliphatic amines are named as alkanamines (replacing 'e' of alkane with 'amine'). For secondary and tertiary amines, the locant 'N' designates substituents on nitrogen. Arylamines are named by replacing 'e' of arene with 'amine'; aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) is an accepted IUPAC name.
diagram_description: Examples of IUPAC and common nomenclature for various aliphatic and aromatic amines

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Reduction of Nitro Compounds and Nitriles
summary: Amines are prepared by six main methods: reduction of nitro compounds or nitriles, ammonolysis of alkyl halides, reduction of amides, Gabriel phthalimide synthesis, and Hofmann bromamide degradation. Each method has distinct advantages and limitations for producing primary, secondary, or tertiary amines.
diagram_description: Flowchart showing six different synthetic routes to amines with starting materials and reaction conditions
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Reduction of Nitro Compounds and Nitriles
summary: Nitro compounds are reduced to amines using H₂ with Ni/Pd/Pt catalysts or Sn with HCl (preferred due to HCl regeneration from FeCl₂ hydrolysis). Nitriles are reduced by LiAlH₄ or catalytic hydrogenation to give primary amines, allowing preparation of amines with one extra carbon atom.
diagram_description: Reduction reactions showing nitrobenzene → aniline and nitrile → primary amine transformations
subject: chemistry

summary: Alkyl or benzyl halides react with ethanolic ammonia at 373 K in sealed tubes via nucleophilic substitution (SN2). The primary amine product acts as a nucleophile and reacts further to form secondary, tertiary amines, and quaternary ammonium salts. Excess ammonia favors primary amine formation.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing sequential displacement of halide by primary, secondary, and tertiary amines
subject: chemistry

summary: Phthalimide is deprotonated by ethanolic KOH to form an anion that undergoes nucleophilic substitution with alkyl halides. Alkaline hydrolysis of the N-alkylphthalimide product releases the primary amine. Aromatic amines cannot be prepared by this method because aryl halides resist nucleophilic substitution.
diagram_description: Multi-step Gabriel synthesis showing phthalimide anion formation, alkylation, and hydrolysis
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary amides react with Br₂ in aqueous or ethanolic NaOH to form primary amines containing one fewer carbon atom than the starting amide. An alkyl or aryl group migrates from the carbonyl carbon to nitrogen during the degradation, producing CO₂ and the rearranged amine product.
diagram_description: Hofmann degradation mechanism showing rearrangement and loss of CO₂ to form primary amine
subject: chemistry

summary: Lower aliphatic amines are gases with fishy odor; C₃+ are liquids, higher ones are solids. Primary amines form stronger hydrogen bonds than secondary amines (which exceed tertiary), leading to boiling point order: primary > secondary > tertiary. Solubility in water decreases with increasing chain length due to greater hydrophobic bulk.
diagram_description: Table comparing boiling points of isomeric amines (primary, secondary, tertiary) and graph showing solubility vs. molar mass
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary and secondary amines undergo intermolecular hydrogen bonding between N-H of one molecule and lone pair of another, causing elevated boiling points. Primary amines show stronger intermolecular association (two H-bond donors) than secondary (one donor), while tertiary amines show none. Boiling point order: 1° > 2° > 3°.
diagram_description: Diagram showing hydrogen bonding network in primary amines with multiple molecules linked by N-H···N interactions
subject: chemistry

summary: Lower aliphatic amines are water-soluble due to hydrogen bonding with water molecules.
