Solubility decreases with increasing molar mass and alkyl chain length because the hydrophobic portion becomes dominant. Amines are soluble in organic solvents (alcohol, ether, benzene) but higher amines are essentially insoluble in water.
diagram_description: Comparative solubility chart for aliphatic amines in water vs. molar mass
subject: chemistry

summary: Amine reactivity is governed by the unshared electron pair on nitrogen, making them nucleophiles and Lewis bases. Reaction type and product depend on amine classification (primary, secondary, tertiary) and the nature of the electrophile.
diagram_description: Overview diagram showing major reaction classes of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines
subject: chemistry

summary: Amines act as Lewis bases by donating their unshared electron pair to accept protons from acids, forming ammonium salts (R-NH₃⁺X⁻). Basicity is quantified by pKb or Kb; larger Kb and smaller pKb indicate stronger bases. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia (pKb ~4.75) due to +I effect of alkyl groups; aromatic amines are weaker due to electron delocalization into the benzene ring.
diagram_description: pKb values table for various amines showing trend: aliphatic > ammonia > aromatic

builds_upon.concept_name: Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases

subject: chemistry

summary: Primary and secondary amines undergo nucleophilic substitution with alkyl halides to form secondary, tertiary, and quaternary ammonium salts. Acylation with acid chlorides, anhydrides, or esters produces amide products. Benzoylation (reaction with benzoyl chloride C₆H₅COCl) is important for characterization and protection of the amino group.
diagram_description: Reaction sequences showing alkylation to form higher-order amines and acylation to form amides
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary amines (aliphatic and aromatic) heated with CHCl₃ and ethanolic KOH form isocyanides (carbylamines), which are foul-smelling compounds. Secondary and tertiary amines do not give this reaction. This is a characteristic test for primary amines.
diagram_description: Carbylamine reaction showing conversion of primary amine to isocyanide with chloroform and KOH
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary aliphatic amines with HNO₂ form unstable aliphatic diazonium salts that decompose to alcohols and N₂ gas; this is used for amino acid estimation. Primary aromatic amines with HNO₂ at 273–278 K form stable arenediazonium salts, which are important synthetic intermediates. Secondary and tertiary amines react differently with HNO₂.
diagram_description: Comparison of primary aliphatic vs. aromatic amine reactions with HNO₂, showing unstable vs. stable diazonium salt formation
formula: R-NH_2 + HNO_2 \rightarrow [R-N^+\equiv N]Cl^- \rightarrow R-OH + N_2 + HCl
subject: chemistry

summary: Benzenesulphonyl chloride (C₆H₅SO₂Cl, Hinsberg reagent) reacts with primary amines to form N-substituted sulphonamides that are soluble in alkali (acidic N-H). Secondary amine sulphonamides are insoluble in alkali (no acidic H). Tertiary amines do not react. This differential reactivity distinguishes the three amine classes.
diagram_description: Hinsberg test results showing sulphonamide formation and alkali solubility patterns for primary, secondary, and tertiary amines
subject: chemistry

summary: The -NH₂ group in aniline is ortho- and para-directing and strongly activating due to electron donation. Bromination with Br₂/H₂O gives 2,4,6-tribromoaniline. Direct nitration is problematic (tarry products, meta isomers from protonated form). The -NH₂ group is protected by acetylation to acetanilide, allowing controlled monosubstitution followed by deacetylation.
diagram_description: Electrophilic substitution reactions of aniline showing protection/deprotection strategy for regiocontrol
subject: chemistry

subtopic_names: Preparation and Properties of Diazonium Salts
summary: Arenediazonium salts (Ar-N₂⁺X⁻) are generated by treating primary aromatic amines with HNO₂ at 273–278 K. They are short-lived in solution but can undergo two types of reactions: (1) displacement of N₂ by Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻, CN⁻, OH⁻, etc., and (2) coupling with phenols and amines to form azo dyes. These salts enable synthesis of aromatic compounds otherwise inaccessible by direct substitution.
diagram_description: Resonance structures of benzenediazonium ion and general framework for N₂ displacement vs. coupling reactions
subject: chemistry

concept_name: Preparation and Properties of Diazonium Salts
summary: Diazonium salts are prepared by treating primary aromatic amines with NaNO₂ and HCl at 273–278 K; HNO₂ is generated in situ. Benzenediazonium chloride (C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻) is a colorless, water-soluble solid stable in cold but reactive when warmed. Benzenediazonium fluoroborate is water-insoluble and stable at room temperature. The stability arises from resonance delocalization in the diazonium ion.
diagram_description: Resonance structures showing stabilization of benzenediazonium ion through delocalization
subject: chemistry

summary: The diazonium group is a very good leaving group, readily displaced by nucleophiles. Sandmeyer reactions introduce Cl⁻, Br⁻, or CN⁻ in the presence of Cu(I); Gatterman reactions use halogen acids with copper powder. Iodine is introduced via KI without copper. Fluorine requires HBF₄ to form stable fluoroborate, then heating to yield ArF. Hydrogen replacement uses hypophosphorous acid or ethanol as reducing agents.
diagram_description: Sandmeyer and Gatterman reactions showing Cu(I)-catalyzed halide/cyanide introduction
subject: chemistry

summary: Arenediazonium salts couple with phenols and amines at the para position (or ortho if para is blocked) to form azo compounds (Ar-N=N-Ar'), which are often brightly colored. The extended conjugation of the –N=N– linkage between aromatic rings produces colors used industrially in dyes. Coupling with phenol gives p-hydroxyazobenzene (orange); with aniline gives p-aminoazobenzene (yellow).
diagram_description: Coupling reaction mechanism showing diazonium electrophile attacking activated phenol/aniline and product structures
subject: chemistry

summary: Basicity of amines depends on stability of the protonated form and factors including inductive effects, solvation, and resonance. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia in the gas phase due to +I stabilization, but in aqueous solution the order reverses for bulky alkyl groups due to differential solvation. Aromatic amines are weaker bases than ammonia due to resonance delocalization of the lone pair into the benzene ring.
diagram_description: pKb trends table and orbital diagrams showing resonance delocalization in aniline vs. stability of substituted ammonium ions
subject: chemistry

summary: In the gas phase, aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia (order: 3° > 2° > 1° > NH₃) due to +I stabilization of the protonated ammonium cation. In aqueous solution, the order is irregular: 1° > 2° > 3° for methyl derivatives due to competing solvation and steric hinderance. Solvation of the smaller primary ammonium cation outweighs the inductive advantage of tertiary amines.
diagram_description: Comparison of protonated amine stability in gas phase vs. aqueous solution showing solvation shells
subject: chemistry

summary: Aromatic amines are weaker bases than ammonia (pKb of aniline = 9.38 vs. 4.75 for NH₃) because the lone pair on nitrogen is conjugated with and delocalized into the benzene ring. Aniline is a resonance hybrid of five structures; the protonated anilinium ion (kekule structures only) has only two resonating structures and is less stabilized, reducing its basicity.
diagram_description: Resonance structures of aniline (5 forms) vs. anilinium ion (2 forms) showing reduced conjugation in protonated form
subject: chemistry

summary: Electron-releasing groups (–OCH₃, –CH₃) attached to the benzene ring increase basicity of substituted anilines by donating electron density to nitrogen. Electron-withdrawing groups (–NO₂, –SO₃H, –COOH, –X) decrease basicity by withdrawing electron density. These effects modify the ease of lone pair availability for proton acceptance.
diagram_description: Table showing basicity trends in substituted anilines with electron-donating vs. electron-withdrawing groups

builds_upon.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles and Techniques

subject: chemistry

concept_name: Important Industrial Applications and Biological Significance
summary: Amines are intermediates in the synthesis of medicines and fibers. Adrenaline and ephedrine (secondary amines) increase blood pressure. Novocain (synthetic amino compound) is a dental anesthetic. Benadryl (tertiary amine) is an antihistaminic.
diagram_description: Structures of pharmaceutical and industrial amine compounds showing functional groups
subject: chemistry

summary: Carbohydrates are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones with general formula Cx(H2O)y. Classified into monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides based on hydrolysis products. Held together by glycosidic linkages and serve as energy sources and structural materials.
diagram_description: Hierarchical classification of carbohydrates with examples at each level
subject: chemistry

summary: Monosaccharides are carbohydrates that cannot be hydrolysed further, classified by carbon number (triose, tetrose, pentose, hexose, heptose) and functional group (aldose or ketose). About 20 naturally occurring types exist. Examples: glucose, fructose, ribose.
diagram_description: Table showing monosaccharide classification by carbon count and functional group
subject: chemistry

summary: Disaccharides yield two monosaccharide units on hydrolysis. Sucrose (glucose + fructose) is non-reducing; maltose (glucose + glucose) and lactose (galactose + glucose) are reducing sugars. Hydrolysis changes sucrose from dextrorotatory to laevorotatory (invert sugar).
diagram_description: Structures of sucrose, maltose, and lactose showing glycosidic linkages
subject: chemistry

summary: Polysaccharides yield many monosaccharide units on hydrolysis and are non-sweet. Starch (amylose + amylopectin) stores energy in plants; cellulose forms plant cell walls; glycogen serves as animal energy storage. All are polymers of glucose with different linkage patterns.
diagram_description: Comparison of branching patterns in amylose, amylopectin, and cellulose structures
subject: chemistry

summary: Glucose exists as open-chain (aldehyde) and cyclic (hemiacetal) forms. The cyclic form is a six-membered pyranose ring with OH at C-5 involved in cyclisation. Two anomeric forms (α and β) differ in C1 configuration; α-form melts at 419 K, β-form at 423 K.
diagram_description: Fischer, open-chain, and Haworth structures of α- and β-glucose showing hemiacetal formation
subject: chemistry

summary: Glycosidic linkage is an oxide bond between the anomeric carbon of one monosaccharide and a hydroxyl group of another, formed by loss of water. α-linkages (C1-C4) are typical in starch and glycogen; β-linkages (C1-C4) form cellulose. Hydrolysis breaks these bonds.
diagram_description: Mechanism of glycosidic bond formation between two glucose molecules with water elimination
subject: chemistry

summary: Reducing sugars contain free aldehyde or ketone groups and reduce Fehling's solution and Tollens' reagent (turn brick-red precipitate and silver mirror respectively). All monosaccharides are reducing sugars. Non-reducing sugars (e.g., sucrose) have anomeric carbons involved in glycosidic bonds.
diagram_description: Test tube showing positive Fehling and Tollens tests for reducing sugars
subject: chemistry

summary: Proteins are polymers of about 20 different α-amino acids linked by peptide bonds. Ten amino acids are essential and must be obtained from diet. Proteins perform structural and dynamic functions; their activity depends on maintaining specific 3D structure.
diagram_description: Overview of protein structure from amino acid monomers to quaternary assembly
subject: chemistry

summary: α-Amino acids contain –NH2 and –COOH groups on the same carbon (α-carbon). In solution they form zwitter ions with both +NH3 and –COO⁻, showing amphoteric behaviour. Most naturally occurring amino acids have L-configuration. Nine amino acids are essential (cannot be synthesised by body).
diagram_description: Zwitter ionic form of an amino acid showing positive and negative charges
subject: chemistry

summary: Peptide bond is an amide formed between –COOH of one amino acid and –NH2 of another with water loss. Dipeptides contain two amino acids; tripeptides contain three. Polypeptides with >10 amino acids and MW >10,000 u are called proteins. Insulin (51 amino acids) is considered a protein.
diagram_description: Mechanism of peptide bond formation between two amino acids with water elimination
subject: chemistry

summary: Primary structure is the amino acid sequence. Secondary structure arises from hydrogen bonding (α-helix or β-pleated sheet). Tertiary structure is overall 3D folding stabilised by H-bonds, disulphide bonds, and electrostatic forces. Quaternary structure is the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits.
diagram_description: Four-level hierarchical diagram showing primary sequence, α-helix/β-sheet, tertiary folding, and quaternary assembly
subject: chemistry

summary: Fibrous proteins have parallel chains held by hydrogen and disulphide bonds, forming fibre-like structures that are insoluble (keratin, myosin). Globular proteins have chains coiled into spherical shapes, are soluble in water (albumin, insulin), and have specific biological activities.
diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison of extended fibrous and coiled globular protein conformations
subject: chemistry

summary: Denaturation occurs when physical (temperature) or chemical (pH) changes disrupt hydrogen bonds in proteins, causing loss of 3D structure and biological activity. Secondary and tertiary structures are destroyed but primary structure remains intact. Example: coagulation of egg white on boiling, curdling of milk.
diagram_description: Native folded protein transitioning to denatured unfolded state upon heat or pH change
subject: chemistry

summary: Enzymes are globular proteins that catalyse biochemical reactions with high specificity. Almost all enzymes are proteins. They reduce activation energy and speed up reactions under mild physiological conditions. Enzymes are named after the substrate (e.g., maltase) or the reaction type (e.g., oxidoreductase).
diagram_description: Energy profile diagram showing enzyme lowering activation energy for a reaction
subject: chemistry

summary: Enzymes are named by appending '-ase' to substrate or reaction type. Maltase hydrolyses maltose; oxidoreductase enzymes catalyse redox reactions. Each enzyme is highly specific for its substrate and catalyses only one type of reaction. Naming reflects biological function.
diagram_description: Table of enzyme names and their substrates/reactions showing naming convention
subject: chemistry

summary: Enzymes reduce the activation energy required for reaction. Acid hydrolysis of sucrose requires Ea = 6.22 kJ/mol; with sucrase enzyme, Ea = 2.15 kJ/mol. Enzymes work by forming enzyme-substrate complexes that stabilise the transition state, lowering the energy barrier.
diagram_description: Energy reaction coordinate diagram comparing uncatalysed and enzyme-catalysed pathways
subject: chemistry

summary: Vitamins are organic compounds required in small amounts for normal growth and health. Most cannot be synthesised by the body and must be obtained from diet. The term 'vitamin' derives from 'vital amine' but most don't contain amino groups. Deficiency causes specific diseases.
diagram_description: Classification table of vitamins by solubility and their food sources
subject: chemistry

summary: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) dissolve in fat and oils but not water. Stored in liver and adipose (fat) tissues. Vitamin A prevents xerophthalmia and night blindness. Vitamin D prevents rickets and osteomalacia.
diagram_description: Sources and deficiency diseases of vitamins A, D, E, and K
subject: chemistry

summary: Water-soluble vitamins (B-group and C) dissolve in water and are readily excreted in urine. Cannot be stored (except B12) so must be supplied regularly. B-vitamins are cofactors in metabolism; deficiency causes beriberi, cheilosis, convulsions, pernicious anaemia. Vitamin C prevents scurvy.
diagram_description: B-vitamins and vitamin C sources, deficiency diseases, and metabolic roles
subject: chemistry

summary: Vitamin A deficiency causes xerophthalmia and night blindness. B1 deficiency causes beriberi. B2 deficiency causes cheilosis. B6 causes convulsions.
diagram_description: Table mapping each vitamin to its sources and associated deficiency disease symptoms
subject: chemistry

summary: Nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides responsible for heredity and protein synthesis. Two types: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid). DNA contains deoxyribose sugar; RNA contains ribose. Both contain phosphoric acid and nitrogen-containing bases.
diagram_description: Hierarchical structure of nucleic acids from bases to phosphodiester-linked chains
subject: chemistry

summary: Complete hydrolysis of DNA or RNA yields pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic bases. DNA contains β-D-2-deoxyribose (lacks OH at C2); RNA contains β-D-ribose. Both DNAs and RNAs contain adenine (A), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). DNA has thymine (T); RNA has uracil (U).
diagram_description: Structures of β-D-ribose and β-D-2-deoxyribose showing difference at C2 position
subject: chemistry

summary: A nucleoside is a base attached to the 1' carbon of pentose sugar via N-glycosidic bond. A nucleotide is a nucleoside with phosphoric acid attached at the 5' position of sugar. Nucleotides join via phosphodiester linkages between 5' and 3' carbons forming polynucleotide chains.
diagram_description: Structure of nucleoside and nucleotide showing base, sugar, and phosphate positions
subject: chemistry

summary: DNA is a double-stranded helix with two nucleic acid chains wound about each other and held by hydrogen bonds between base pairs. Adenine pairs with thymine (2 H-bonds); guanine pairs with cytosine (3 H-bonds). Strands are complementary. Primary structure is the nucleotide sequence.
diagram_description: Double helix DNA structure showing sugar-phosphate backbone, base pairing, and hydrogen bonds
subject: chemistry

summary: RNA is single-stranded and sometimes folds back on itself. Three types exist: mRNA (messenger RNA) carries protein synthesis instructions; rRNA (ribosomal RNA) is structural; tRNA (transfer RNA) carries amino acids to ribosome. All three types participate in protein synthesis.
diagram_description: Structures and functions of mRNA (linear), tRNA (cloverleaf fold), and rRNA showing secondary structures
subject: chemistry

summary: DNA is the chemical basis of heredity, maintaining species identity over generations. DNA self-duplicates during cell division, transferring identical strands to daughter cells. RNA directs protein synthesis via genetic code. DNA fingerprinting uses unique base sequences for identification in forensics and paternity.
diagram_description: Central dogma showing DNA replication, transcription to mRNA, and translation to proteins
subject: chemistry

summary: Hormones are molecules acting as intercellular messengers produced by endocrine glands and transported via bloodstream to target sites. Some are steroids (estrogens, androgens), others are polypeptides (insulin, endorphins), and some are amino acid derivatives (epinephrine, thyroxine). They regulate biological activities.
diagram_description: Overview of endocrine system showing hormone production, transport, and target cell recognition
subject: chemistry

summary: Steroid hormones are produced by adrenal cortex and gonads. Testosterone (males) develops secondary male characteristics; estradiol (females) develops secondary female characteristics and controls menstrual cycle. Progesterone prepares uterus for fertilised egg implantation. Glucocorticoids control carbohydrate metabolism and inflammation.
diagram_description: Structures and biological roles of major steroid hormones in males and females
subject: chemistry

summary: Peptide hormones are polypeptides. Insulin regulates blood glucose by lowering levels when high; glucagon raises glucose levels. Together they maintain glucose homeostasis. Growth hormones regulate growth and development.
diagram_description: Sequence of insulin showing amino acid composition and structure
subject: chemistry

summary: Hormones maintain biological balance. Thyroxine (from thyroid) is an iodinated tyrosine derivative controlling metabolic rate. Low thyroxine causes hypothyroidism (lethargy, obesity); high causes hyperthyroidism. Low dietary iodine leads to hypothyroidism and thyroid enlargement, prevented by iodised salt.
diagram_description: Feedback loop showing thyroid hormone regulation by TSH and iodine availability
subject: chemistry

concept_name: D and L Amino Acid Configuration
summary: D and L notations indicate relative configuration compared to glyceraldehyde. Most naturally occurring amino acids are L-form. L-amino acids are written with –NH2 on left; D-form has –NH2 on right. Configuration is determined by comparing the lowest asymmetric carbon to glyceraldehyde standard.
diagram_description: Fischer projections of D- and L-glyceraldehyde and L-amino acids showing –OH/–NH2 positions
subject: chemistry

summary: Glucose is prepared industrially by hydrolysis of starch with dilute H2SO4 at 393 K under 2-3 atm pressure. Laboratory preparation: sucrose boiled with dilute HCl or H2SO4 in alcohol gives equimolar glucose and fructose. Glucose occurs naturally in fruits and honey.
diagram_description: Reaction scheme showing starch and sucrose hydrolysis to produce glucose
subject: chemistry

summary: Fructose is a ketohexose with molecular formula C6H12O6, differing from glucose by ketone group at C2 instead of aldehyde. D(–)-fructose is laevorotatory. Forms furanose (five-membered ring) via hemiacetal with C5-OH, not pyranose. Two anomeric forms exist.
diagram_description: Open-chain and furanose forms of α- and β-fructose showing ring closure mechanism
subject: chemistry

summary: DNA fingerprinting uses unique base sequences on DNA to identify individuals. Unlike fingerprints, DNA sequences cannot be altered by surgery and are identical in every cell. Applications: forensic criminal identification, paternity determination, dead body identification in accidents, and evolution studies.
diagram_description: Gel electrophoresis bands showing DNA fingerprint patterns for identification comparison
subject: chemistry

summary: Addison's disease results from adrenal cortex dysfunction, causing deficiency of glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids. Symptoms: hypoglycaemia, weakness, increased stress susceptibility. Fatal unless treated with glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids replacement therapy.
diagram_description: Comparison of normal adrenal function versus Addison's disease showing hormone deficiencies
subject: chemistry
